event_handlers: onFormChildrenChange: Whenever the registration-form updates the children list or their selected sessions, recompute the estimated total using current event and session pricing data.
onEventPricingChange: If the Event or EventSession pricing changes (due to re-fetch), recompute the estimated total.
onRegistrationSuccess: When createEventRegistration succeeds, replace the estimated total with registration.total_amount_due from the API response to ensure the user sees the authoritative amount.
onPaymentMethodChange: Optionally adjust displayed notes or breakdown depending on payment_method (though the calculation remains based on Event and EventSession data).

components.functionality: Displays validation errors, API error messages, and high-level success messages related to loading events and submitting registrations. Consolidates messages from multiple components so the user has a clear overview.
event_handlers: onValidationError: Listen for validation error events from registration-form and show a summary message indicating that some fields need attention.
onApiError: When getEventDetails or createEventRegistration returns an error, show the provided error message in a prominent location.
onApiSuccess: When createEventRegistration succeeds, show a success message (optionally using the message field from the API response) before or along with the confirmation view.

components.functionality: Shown after a successful createEventRegistration call. It displays a confirmation message, guardian information, a summary of registered children and their sessions, the final total_amount_due, and payment method details. It also integrates navigation links back to events.html and index.html.
event_handlers: onShowConfirmation: Triggered when registration-form receives a successful createEventRegistration response; uses the registration and children data to populate the confirmation summary.
onClickBackToEvents: Delegates to event-breadcrumb-nav to navigate to events.html.
onClickReturnHome: Delegates to event-breadcrumb-nav to navigate to index.html.
onUrlViewChange: If the URL view parameter changes to confirmation (e.g., user navigates back/forward), show or hide this panel accordingly, in sync with the registration-form visibility.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear, top-to-bottom structure within a centered, fixed-width container (~960–1040px). Above the main content card, a narrow breadcrumb navigation sits directly under the global header. Below that, a full-width gradient band holds a single dominant white content card. Inside this card, the top portion is reserved for an event summary block in a two-column layout (event image on the left, event title/details text on the right, with a centered caption under the image). Beneath the summary, the card splits into a functional two-column area: the left column stacks session availability and the registration form; the right column stacks the pricing summary and form status/feedback. On successful registration, a confirmation panel appears prominently within the card, visually taking precedence over the form while still respecting the same stacked, linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear, top-to-bottom structure within a centered, fixed-width container (~960–1040px). Above the main content card, a narrow breadcrumb navigation sits directly under the global header. Below that, a full-width gradient band holds a single dominant white content card. Inside this card, the top portion is reserved for an event summary block in a two-column layout (event image on the left, event title/details text on the right, with a centered caption under the image). Beneath the summary, the card splits into a functional two-column area: the left column stacks session availability and the registration form; the right column stacks the pricing summary and form status/feedback. On successful registration, a confirmation panel appears prominently within the card, visually taking precedence over the form while still respecting the same stacked, linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (outside this spec) occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport. Directly beneath, the breadcrumb uses minimal vertical height (~32–40px). The rest of the viewport is dominated by a full-width gradient band with generous vertical padding (~48–64px). Centered within this band, the main white content card spans about 80–85% of the browser width up to a fixed content width of roughly 960–1040px, with 40–60px margin between the card edge and the gradient background on all sides. Inside the card, padding is 24–32px. The event summary block at the top takes roughly the upper third of the card. Its internal columns allocate about 40–45% to the image (left) and 55–60% to the text (right). Below, the functional area uses a similar left/right split: left column ~60% width for session list and form, right column ~40% width for pricing summary and status/confirmation, separated by about 16–24px gutter. Vertical spacing between major sections (summary, sessions, registration, confirmation) is 24–32px, with 32–48px from breadcrumb to card.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page matches the moderately dense, text-centric feel of the mockup. The event summary uses multiple text lines with a relatively small serif body font and tight but readable line lengths. The session list and registration form present several fields and rows in a compact stacking pattern, but ample section headers, 16–24px internal spacing, and clear field groupings keep it from feeling cramped. The pricing summary uses compact line items and totals, while the status and confirmation panels use short blocks of text with modest padding to remain noticeable without overwhelming the page. Overall, the layout feels like an informational brochure: lots of information in a single focal card, but structured clearly and predictably.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains a classic Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left with the global logo (outside this spec) and moves right across the header to the main nav, then drops slightly to scan the breadcrumb from left to right. From there, the eye falls diagonally down to the upper-left of the content card where the event image anchors attention. It then moves horizontally to the right column of the event summary where the title, date, and key information are read. Next, attention flows diagonally down to the left column’s session list and registration form, where most interaction happens, and finally moves across to the right column for the pricing summary and status messages. On successful submission, the confirmation panel appears near the top of the card, intercepting this flow early and acting as the new visual focal point while still leading the eye downward into any remaining details.

layout.overall_description: Within a warm, gradient-tinted background that fills the full browser width, a centered fixed-width content area holds a single prominent white card that carries the entire event detail and registration experience. Under the site’s standard header, a slim breadcrumb line runs horizontally near the top of the page, left-aligned within the same fixed-width container and leaving a band of whitespace before the main card. The card itself sits comfortably in the middle of the screen with generous top and side margins, immediately drawing focus. Its top third showcases the event summary in a two-column composition: a large, rectangular event photograph on the left and a text-heavy block on the right with the event title, date, location, and a few short paragraphs. A centered caption line under the photo gently anchors the left side. Below this, the card’s content splits into two vertical columns. The left, slightly wider column contains first the session availability list, then the registration form, stacked with clear section headers and moderate spacing. The right, narrower column aligns to the top of the functional area and contains a compact pricing summary panel followed by status or feedback messages. When a registration is completed, a confirmation panel appears prominently just below the event summary, often spanning both columns and pushing the lower content down, creating a strong visual cue without breaking the underlying fixed, card-based structure. A semi-transparent loader can temporarily float above this central card when data is loading, dimming the content below while maintaining the sense of a single, cohesive focal panel framed by the gradient background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire gradient band and the central white content card. It is positioned centrally on the page, matching the width of the fixed content area but visually dimming everything beneath, from the breadcrumb down to the bottom of the card. The main loading indicator (spinner or progress message) is centered both vertically and horizontally within the visible card area, roughly at the midpoint of the viewport. The overlay has soft edges (slight darkening or light veil) while preserving the underlying card silhouette, reinforcing the idea that the card remains the primary canvas but is temporarily inactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily appears as a full-surface overlay positioned relative to the main content card and gradient band, sitting above all other components with fixed/absolute positioning and blocking interaction until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-breadcrumb-nav sits directly below the global header, inside the same centered fixed-width container as the main content card. It is a single horizontal row aligned to the left, spanning roughly 70–80% of the container width, with the text starting about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the header/navigation bar. The breadcrumb text is relatively small and unobtrusive, with about 8–12px padding above and below the line. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space below the breadcrumb before the top edge of the main white content card, allowing the breadcrumb to act as a subtle contextual link while clearly separating it from the main event content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page, always positioned immediately above the main content card within the centered fixed-width layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-summary-section occupies the full width of the top portion of the white content card, framed by 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside this section, content is arranged into two columns: a large event photograph on the left occupying about 40–45% of the card’s width, and a right-hand text column occupying about 55–60%. The photograph sits flush with the section’s top padding and is vertically centered relative to the main summary text block. Directly below the photograph, within the same left column width, a single-line caption runs centered under the image with about 8–12px of separation. On the right, the event title appears at the top of the text column, followed by the event date, location, and one or more short descriptive paragraphs, stacked with 8–16px spacing between lines and paragraphs. The baseline of the right-hand text roughly aligns with the bottom of the left image plus caption, giving the section a balanced, rectangular silhouette. A subtle horizontal divider or increased vertical spacing (16–24px) separates this summary section from the functional content below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the main content card, always the first element seen within the card and pushing subsequent components downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session-availability-list begins immediately below the event summary, aligned to the left side of the card. It forms the top portion of the left column in the lower half of the card. This list spans roughly 60% of the card’s internal width, leaving a 16–24px vertical gutter between its right edge and the start of the right column. The section starts with a small heading or label aligned with the left padding of the card, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the summary above. Each session entry in the list is displayed as a horizontally oriented row: session name/details on the left and availability status or a small indicator on the right edge of the same column, with about 8–12px vertical spacing between rows. The entire list fits within a light, subtle boundary (e.g., a faint border or slightly tinted background) to distinguish it from the registration form below while still feeling like part of the same left column. There is 16–24px of vertical margin between the bottom of the session list and the top of the registration form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, expanding vertically as sessions are added and pushing the registration form further down while preserving the left/right column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-form sits directly below the session-availability-list in the left column, aligned to the same left margin and spanning the same ~60% width of the card. There is a clear section heading at the top of the form, with roughly 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the session list. Inside, form fields are arranged in a vertical stack, with each child-registration-row occupying one row, followed by ancillary controls (e.g., add child) and finally the submission controls. The form uses a modest amount of vertical spacing (8–16px) between rows to maintain the moderately dense feel of the mockup. At the bottom of the form, the primary submit button and any secondary action buttons are right-aligned within the left column, with 12–16px padding around them to echo the beveled, skeuomorphic button style. The bottom edge of the form leaves about 24–32px of padding before the bottom edge of the card, so the form feels contained and not pressed against the card’s border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, expanding vertically as additional child-registration-rows are added, and determining the overall height of the card’s lower portion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each child-registration-row is a horizontally oriented strip within the registration form, spanning the full width of the left column (approximately 60% of the card width minus padding). The row height is about 48–56px, providing enough space for labels and inputs without feeling airy. Within the row, fields are laid out side by side: a larger child name input on the left taking roughly 40% of the row width, followed by smaller fields for age and grade (each around 12–15%), and a session or group selector on the right taking the remaining 30–35%. There is 8–12px of horizontal spacing between each field. Inputs align vertically so their baselines and top edges create a clean horizontal line. If an action such as a remove icon is present, it sits at the far right edge, aligned vertically center within the row. Rows are stacked with 8–12px of vertical space between them, creating a compact but legible list of children within the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating unit inside the registration form; rows stack vertically and the form’s height stretches as more rows are added, but the row’s internal layout remains a fixed, horizontally partitioned grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-summary-panel occupies the right column of the main content card’s lower section. It is vertically aligned so that its top edge roughly aligns with the heading of the session-availability-list on the left, creating a clean horizontal line across the card. This panel spans about 40% of the card width (minus the central gutter) and is inset with 24–32px padding on all sides to feel like a self-contained box. The contents are stacked vertically: a small section title at the top, followed by line items (e.g., per-child cost, discounts, fees) listed in a narrow table-like layout, and a bold total at the bottom. Items are arranged to allow quick scanning: labels left-aligned, amounts right-aligned within the panel. Vertical spacing between line items is modest (8–12px), maintaining the moderately dense feel. The panel may use a slightly tinted background or subtle border and possibly a soft gradient to echo the skeuomorphic style and distinguish it from the surrounding card. There is 16–24px of space between the bottom of this panel and any status messages or confirmation elements below it in the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, growing vertically as pricing details are added but always aligned to the top of the functional area opposite the session list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status-messages panel is positioned close to where users focus during interaction, but without disrupting the primary flow of fields. It sits immediately above the registration-form within the left column, spanning the same 60% width. When present, it appears as a horizontally full-width strip or small box with 12–16px internal padding and about 8–12px margin above and below, visually separating it from both the session list above and the form fields below. Error messages might use a soft, warm-tinted background; success or informational messages use a more neutral but still noticeable tone, consistent with the earnest, nonprofit aesthetic. The text within the panel is left-aligned and concise, with line breaks restricted to maintain a compact height. When no messages are present, this area collapses, bringing the form heading closer to the session list and preserving the compact, brochure-like density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered panel within the left column; appears and disappears without shifting the overall column structure, simply expanding or collapsing vertical space between the session list and the registration-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation-panel appears within the same main white card, directly beneath the event-summary-section and above the two-column functional area. When shown, it spans the full interior width of the card, overriding the left/right split for a moment to create a strong, unified message block. It is separated from the summary above by about 16–24px and from the content below by another 24–32px. The panel resembles a highlighted message card: slightly tinted background, soft border, and 16–24px padding. Text is center-aligned or left-aligned depending on tone, with a prominent confirmation heading and a short paragraph explaining next steps. Any important details (e.g., confirmation number, summary of children registered) are grouped within this panel in a compact list. When active, this panel may be accompanied by the de-emphasis or collapse of the registration form below (for example, the form can be minimized, disabled, or replaced with a simple summary), but spatially, the left/right columns remain present under it so the page layout preserves the same Z-pattern and card proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed, inserted between the event-summary-section and the lower two-column area; when visible, it pushes the session-availability-list, pricing-summary-panel, and registration-form downward but does not overlap them, maintaining the overall stacked, static card structure.

name: Donate
title: Donate
description: Donation page to create one-time or monthly gifts to specific ministry funds, including designation, dedication, donor details, payment method selection, and submission via the donation API.

core_features: Initialize the Donate page by calling getDonationPageConfig to load available funds, suggested amounts, processing fee percentage, and allowed payment methods.
Allow donors to switch between one-time and monthly donation types using tabs, with initial selection influenced by the donationType URL parameter.
Offer preset donation amounts based on configuration and/or the selected fund, while also allowing donors to enter a custom amount; initial amount can be pre-filled via the amount URL parameter.
Allow donors to designate their gift to a specific fund from the list returned by getDonationPageConfig, with an optional pre-selected fund based on the fundId URL parameter.
Provide a checkbox option to allow donors to cover processing fees, dynamically estimating and displaying the adjusted total using the processing_fee_percentage from getDonationPageConfig.
Enable optional dedication of gifts (e.g., in honor or memory of someone) by toggling dedication fields for dedication_type, honoree_name, and dedication_message.
Collect required donor information (name, email, mailing address) and validate it before submission.
Display payment method options strictly based on allowed_payment_methods from getDonationPageConfig, including offline options such as bill_me_by_mail and pay_later_instructions.
Submit the donation or monthly pledge by calling createDonation with the selected donation_type, amount, fund_id, dedication details, donor info, and payment_method.
Show a clear confirmation state after a successful createDonation response, including the key details returned in the Donation object and a link back to index.html.
Handle validation and API errors by displaying inline messages and allowing donors to correct and resubmit without losing previously entered information.
user_workflows: One-time donation with fund preselected and preset amount: 1) Donor clicks a Donate call-to-action from index.html or another page that links to donate.html with donationType and/or amount and fundId URL parameters. 2) On page load, the controller component calls getDonationPageConfig and waits for the response. 3) After configuration is loaded, the page selects the donation type tab based on the donationType URL parameter (if valid) or a default type, pre-fills the amount field from the amount URL parameter (if present), and pre-selects the fund that matches fundId (if present and active). 4) The donor reviews or adjusts the amount using the preset buttons or custom field. 5) The donor optionally toggles the processing-fee checkbox; the UI recalculates and displays the estimated total charged based on processing_fee_percentage. 6) The donor fills in donor information and optionally expands the dedication section to enter dedication_type, honoree_name, and dedication_message. 7) The donor selects a payment method from the options defined by allowed_payment_methods. 8) The donor submits the donation form, triggering validation; if valid, createDonation is called with a one_time donation_type and the collected form data. 9) If createDonation returns success, the confirmation component displays the donation details from the Donation object and a link back to index.html; if not, error messages are shown and the donor can retry.

Monthly recurring donation from a sponsorship or fund detail page: 1) Donor originates from sponsorship_list.html or child_profile.html, or a fund spotlight, and clicks Donate; the link opens donate.html with donationType set to 'monthly' and fundId set to the relevant fund identifier, and may include an amount parameter tailored to that campaign. 2) On load, the Donate page controller calls getDonationPageConfig and then applies URL parameters: selects the monthly tab, pre-selects the fund matching fundId, and pre-fills the amount from amount if provided; if no amount parameter exists, suggested recurring amounts from default_recurring_amounts or the fund-specific suggestions are displayed as presets. 3) The donor chooses a preset monthly amount or enters a custom monthly amount. 4) The donor optionally chooses to cover processing fees and sees an updated estimated monthly total based on processing_fee_percentage. 5) The donor completes donor details and optionally enters a dedication. 6) The donor chooses a payment method; if online_card is allowed, they can select it, otherwise they see only offline options such as bill_me_by_mail or pay_later_instructions. 7) On form submission, client-side validation runs; if valid, createDonation is called with donation_type set to 'monthly', the chosen amount, fund_id, cover_processing_fee, dedication payload, donor_info payload, the selected payment_method, and a start_date value (either derived from the current date or another business rule, without exposing date selection in the UI unless required by future enhancements). 8) On success, the page displays a confirmation message with monthly status information coming from the Donation object and a link back to index.html; on error, the donor is shown actionable error messages and can adjust their entries and resubmit.

Donation with offline payment instructions and dedication: 1) Donor navigates to donate.html directly or from about.html (optionally with donationType and fundId URL parameters). 2) After getDonationPageConfig completes, the donor selects a donation type tab and fund (or uses the pre-selected fund from fundId). 3) The donor enters any amount they choose, optionally covers processing fees, and then expands the dedication section to select a dedication_type and enter honoree_name and a personal dedication_message. 4) The donor chooses an offline payment method from the available allowed_payment_methods (bill_me_by_mail or pay_later_instructions if present). 5) The donor fills out contact and mailing information, which is required for offline fulfillment. 6) On submission, validation ensures all required fields for offline processing are completed, and createDonation is called with the offline payment_method and dedication_enabled set based on the UI state. 7) If createDonation succeeds, the confirmation view shows a tailored message (using the returned Donation data, such as payment_method and donation_type) and provides next-step guidance (for example, that they will receive mail or email with instructions) and a link back to index.html; if createDonation fails, errors are displayed with the form still populated for quick correction.
interactions: Donation type tab selection: Clicking a donation type tab (e.g., one_time or monthly) updates the current donation_type in the client state and re-renders the amount presets based on default_recurring_amounts or fund-specific suggestions; this interaction can optionally update the URL (e.g., donationType query parameter) to reflect the current tab without reloading.

Fund selection: Selecting a fund from a dropdown or list binds the selected fund_id in client state, updates available preset amounts using that fund's default_amount_suggestions (if provided by getDonationPageConfig), and may clear or adapt the currently selected amount if it does not match any available preset.

Amount selection and custom entry: Clicking on a preset amount button sets the amount field; typing in the custom amount field clears any active preset selection and updates the amount in client state; validation ensures the entered value is a valid positive number before allowing submission.

Processing fee toggle: Checking or unchecking the 'cover processing fee' option updates cover_processing_fee in client state; when checked, the UI calculates an estimated fee using the current amount and the processing_fee_percentage from getDonationPageConfig and displays an updated total, purely for display (the actual fee is calculated on the server based on cover_processing_fee).

Dedication enable/disable: Interacting with a 'Dedicate this gift' toggle reveals or hides dedication_type, honoree_name, and dedication_message fields; when disabled, dedication_enabled is set to false and the related dedication fields are cleared in client state; when enabled, dedication_enabled is true and the additional fields are required or optional based on validation rules.

Donor information entry: Typing into donor name, email, and address fields updates the donor_info object in client state; blur or submit events trigger validation for required fields and formatting (e.g., email syntax, postal code presence).

Payment method selection: Choosing a payment method option sets payment_method in client state; options displayed are filtered by allowed_payment_methods from getDonationPageConfig; selecting an option may also trigger contextual helper text (for example, explaining offline fulfillment steps) but does not call any external interfaces until final submission.

Form submission: Clicking the primary submit button triggers onSubmit on the main donation form component, which validates all required fields based on the chosen donation_type, fund, amount, dedication, donor_info, and payment_method; if validation passes, the component calls createDonation with the aggregated payload; while awaiting response, the button enters a loading state and double-submission is prevented.

API success handling: When createDonation returns success: true, the confirmation component reads the Donation object from the response, stores it in local page state, and replaces or overlays the form with a confirmation view summarizing the donation_type, amount, fund.name (resolved from the fund_id), payment_method, and status; a 'Back to Home' link navigates to index.html with no additional parameters.

API error handling: If createDonation returns success: false or a network error occurs, the page displays error messages (global and/or field-specific when possible), keeps all form fields populated, and allows the donor to modify entries and resubmit.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the Donate page reads the URL query parameters donationType, amount, and fundId. After getDonationPageConfig resolves, the page controller validates donationType against the allowed donation types (e.g., 'one_time' or 'monthly'); if valid, it sets the current donation_type tab accordingly; if invalid or absent, a default donation_type is applied without relying on localStorage. If the amount parameter is present and is a valid positive number, it is used to initialize the amount field and to select or create an appropriate preset amount state; if absent, the amount field remains empty and the user can select from the presets provided by default_recurring_amounts or fund-specific suggestions. If fundId is present, the controller attempts to match it with one of the DonationFund items in funds from getDonationPageConfig; on a successful match, that fund is pre-selected; if no match is found, the fund selection remains unset or falls back to a default fund selection strategy defined by business rules. The URL parameters only influence the initial state; subsequent user interactions (such as changing tabs, amounts, or funds) override the initial parameter-driven state in memory without writing to localStorage. Optionally, when the user switches donation types or funds, the page may update the URL query string (using history.replaceState or similar) to reflect the current donationType, amount, and fundId for shareability and back-button support, always ensuring that these parameters are used only to hydrate state on load and are never treated as authoritative over explicit user inputs once the page is loaded. Post-submission confirmation state is managed entirely in memory based on the createDonation response, without storing any information in localStorage and without requiring URL changes, except for navigation to index.html when the user clicks the confirmation link.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, calls getDonationPageConfig, parses URL parameters (donationType, amount, fundId), initializes the main donation form state, and routes between the form view and the confirmation view based on createDonation responses.

Presents donation type options as tabs (e.g., one-time and monthly). When a tab is selected, it updates the current donation_type in the shared form state and notifies dependent components (such as amount presets and summary) to refresh. Initial selection is derived from donationType URL parameter after config load.

Displays available donation funds returned by getDonationPageConfig and allows the donor to choose a designation for their gift. Applies the fundId URL parameter when available to pre-select a fund. When a fund is selected, it sets fund_id in the shared donation state and may update available preset amounts based on that fund's default_amount_suggestions.

Manages selection and validation of the donation amount. It renders preset amount options derived from getDonationPageConfig (default_recurring_amounts and/or fund.default_amount_suggestions) and any amount value supplied via the amount URL parameter. Donors can select a preset option or enter a custom amount; the component ensures only one active source (preset or custom) at a time and keeps the donation amount in sync with shared donation state.

Allows donors to choose whether to cover processing fees. Reads processing_fee_percentage from getDonationPageConfig to estimate and display the additional fee and adjusted total when enabled, and sets cover_processing_fee in the donation state accordingly. This information is used for display, while the server calculates actual fee based on the cover_processing_fee flag.

Collects optional dedication details for the donation. Controlled by a primary toggle that sets dedication_enabled; when enabled, the component exposes fields for dedication_type, honoree_name, and dedication_message. It produces a dedication payload object used by the main form to call createDonation.

Captures donor identity and contact information required for processing the donation: donor_first_name, donor_last_name, donor_email, address_line1, address_line2, address_city, address_state, address_postal_code, and optionally address_country. It validates required fields and formats, and prepares a donor_info payload object for the createDonation call.

Displays available payment methods based on allowed_payment_methods from getDonationPageConfig and allows the donor to choose one. It sets payment_method in the shared donation state and can display contextual information depending on the selected method (for example, expectations for bill_me_by_mail versus pay_later_instructions versus online_card).

Provides a real-time summary of the donation based on current state, including donation_type, selected fund name (resolved via fund_id and the funds list), base amount, whether processing fees are being covered, and the estimated total charged or pledged. Updates immediately when any relevant field changes.

Aggregates state from donation-type-tabs, fund-designation-selector, donation-amount-selector, processing-fee-toggle, dedication-form, donor-info-form, and payment-method-selector into a single payload. On submit, performs final validation and calls createDonation with donation_type, amount, fund_id, cover_processing_fee, a nested dedication object, a nested donor_info object, payment_method, and start_date. Manages loading state, prevents duplicate submissions, and delegates response handling to the controller.

Displays after a successful createDonation call. Shows an acknowledgment message using the Donation object returned by the API (including donation_type, amount, fund_id resolved to fund name, payment_method, and status). Provides a 'Back to Home' link that navigates to index.html. It can also display any server-provided message included in the createDonation response.

Handles display of validation and API errors for the donation process. Receives error information from both client-side validation and createDonation responses, maps them to user-readable messages, and highlights fields that require attention while preserving all entered data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header and navigation (global, outside this spec), then a large horizontal gradient band filling the rest of the viewport. Centered within this band sits a single white donation card with rounded/barely-rounded corners and subtle shadow. Inside the card, content is split into two primary columns: the left column is a static ministry-focused panel (photo and short copy managed by the page controller), while the right column stacks all interactive donation components vertically in a clear sequence (type selection → fund → amount → fee toggle → dedication → donor info → payment method → summary → submit). Confirmation and errors appear within the same card, replacing or overlaying sections rather than creating new containers.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header and navigation (global, outside this spec), then a large horizontal gradient band filling the rest of the viewport. Centered within this band sits a single white donation card with rounded/barely-rounded corners and subtle shadow. Inside the card, content is split into two primary columns: the left column is a static ministry-focused panel (photo and short copy managed by the page controller), while the right column stacks all interactive donation components vertically in a clear sequence (type selection → fund → amount → fee toggle → dedication → donor info → payment method → summary → submit). Confirmation and errors appear within the same card, replacing or overlaying sections rather than creating new containers.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The full-width header plus nav occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The gradient donation band fills the remainder, with the central white card taking about 70–75% of the page width (e.g., ~960px within a ~1200px viewport) and centered. Within the card, the left visual column occupies about 40–45% of the card width and the right interactive column about 55–60%. Internal padding for the card is a generous 24–32px on all sides. Vertical spacing between major form sections in the right column is 16–24px, with slightly larger gaps (24–32px) before key breaks like the summary and submit area. The card has 40–60px of space from the gradient band on all sides, making it the clear focal point.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately high: the right column stacks multiple forms and selectors without large decorative gaps, using an 8–16px rhythm between related fields and 16–24px between section groups. Labels and help text are compact, serif or transitional serif body type, with fairly tight line lengths. The left column has a large photo and brief, tightly set text, providing visual breathing room that balances the denser right-hand form content. Overall the page feels like a structured nonprofit brochure form—not airy, but clear and respectful.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a classic Z-pattern: users first see the logo in the global header’s top-left, then scan to the centered site title and nav. From there, attention drops diagonally down to the large image and ministry message in the left column of the donation card. The eye then moves rightward into the donation interaction column: first the donation type tabs and fund selector, then proceeds down through the form sequence. The lower-right of the card is anchored by the donation summary and the primary "Donate" button, creating a strong final focal point that encourages completion. Error messages appear near the top of the right column, and the confirmation view replaces the right column’s form content while keeping the same overall card framing, maintaining flow and orientation.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width header and navigation, the Donate page opens into a soft, vertical gradient band that fills most of the viewport, centered on a single prominent white donation card. This card sits on the gradient with generous margins, its subtle bevel and shadow giving it a slightly skeuomorphic, early-2010s nonprofit feel. Inside, the card divides into two columns: on the left, a large, warm-toned photograph of children in ministry fills most of the column, with a short, centered scripture or mission statement beneath it. On the right, aligned with the top of the photo, runs a vertically stacked sequence of donation controls: first the donation type tabs, fund designation, and amount; then the processing-fee toggle, dedication, donor information, and payment method; and finally a bordered summary box and a large beveled "Donate" button anchored to the bottom-right. Error notices appear as a pale, full-width bar at the top of the right column when needed, while successful donations transform the right column into a centered confirmation message within the same card, leaving the left ministry image intact. The fixed-width, centered layout, tight typographic rhythm, and clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow make the form feel organized and trustworthy while preserving the earnest, traditional visual language of a church-based nonprofit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation page controller manifests as the main white content card centered within the full-width gradient band beneath the site header. It defines a fixed content width of roughly 960px and applies consistent 24–32px padding on all sides inside the card. Within this card, it divides space into two side-by-side columns: the left column occupies about 40–45% of the card width and runs from near the top padding to just above the bottom padding, housing a large ministry photograph and a short block of text directly below it. The right column occupies the remaining 55–60% of the card width and aligns vertically with the top of the left image, stacking all interactive donation components from top to bottom. The controller also manages state transitions: when a donation is confirmed, the right column’s form stack is replaced by the confirmation view while the surrounding card, left image panel, and header remain in place, preserving layout continuity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered container that sets the fixed-width, two-column grid and controls swapping between form and confirmation views within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-type tabs sit near the very top of the right column, directly under any right-column heading or introduction that the page controller may provide, and are aligned flush with the right column’s left and right edges. They form a single horizontal row of 2–3 beveled tab buttons (e.g., "One-Time Gift", "Monthly Gift"), each with about 8–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, filling approximately 80–90% of the right column’s width and centered or slightly left-weighted. There is about 8–12px of whitespace above and 12–16px below the tabs, clearly separating them from other form sections while keeping them visually tied to the top of the form sequence as the primary choice for how the donation behaves over time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the right column’s form stack; does not scroll independently or stick, but remains within the main card’s natural document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fund designation selector appears immediately below the donation-type tabs in the right column, separated by roughly 12–16px of vertical spacing. It spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 90–100% of the column, minus internal padding) and is left-aligned. A bold label sits above a beveled dropdown or set of radio buttons, with 4–8px of space between label and control. The selector occupies about 1/6–1/5 of the visible height of the right column’s initial viewport segment, making it clearly visible without feeling oversized. Its position directly under the tabs reinforces the flow: users choose donation frequency first, then where the gift is designated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level component in the right column, expanding vertically as needed if multiple fund options or descriptions are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation amount selector sits directly beneath the fund designation selector, with about 16–20px of vertical gap to emphasize its importance. It spans the full right column width and occupies a taller vertical block—roughly 1/4 of the right column’s height in the initial viewport. At the top of this block, a label and any brief helper text are left-aligned; below them, a grid of preset amount buttons (e.g., $25, $50, $100) is arranged in one or two horizontal rows. Each amount button uses the same beveled style as the navigation and tabs, with approximately 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding and 8px gaps between buttons. Beneath the presets, aligned left, a "Custom amount" input field stretches across about 60–70% of the column width. The entire block is visually grouped, possibly with a faint border or background tint, so that the user perceives it as a single step in the donation flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding vertically as needed according to the number of preset buttons and presence of helper text, but always anchored within the right column’s natural top-to-bottom sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The processing fee toggle is a compact horizontal row placed just beneath the donation amount selector, separated by about 8–12px of space to keep it visually associated with the amount. It sits in the right column, occupying roughly the full width but with the main descriptive text left-aligned and the checkbox/toggle control typically aligned to the right end of that same row. The row height is modest—about the height of a single line of text plus some 4–8px vertical padding—so it reads as a light, optional add-on rather than a major step. Its positioning directly under the amount keeps the relationship between the gift size and fee coverage intuitively clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline row element; remains in place and does not stick or float, but its narrow height allows it to move naturally with the content when the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dedication form appears below the processing fee toggle, separated by approximately 16–20px to mark a transition into more detailed, optional information. It spans the full width of the right column and is presented as a bordered or subtly shaded sub-card within the main right column, with 16–24px internal padding. At the top, a slightly bolder section heading (e.g., "Dedicate this gift") is left-aligned with 4–8px of spacing before any descriptive line. Below, the form fields (such as honoree name, occasion, and optional message) are arranged in one or two compact rows: on larger desktop widths, shorter fields may be displayed in two columns (each about 48–50% of the right column width with an 8–12px gap between) to keep the block from becoming unnecessarily tall, aligning with the moderately dense, brochure-like feel. The section overall occupies about 1/5–1/4 of the right column height when expanded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that can expand vertically as more fields are shown. If the dedication feature is optional, the section may initially appear collapsed to a single-row toggle or link, expanding inline within the same right column space when activated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donor info form sits directly beneath the dedication form (or beneath the fee toggle if dedication is disabled), with around 24px of vertical spacing to clearly mark it as the next major step. It spans the full right column width and forms one of the taller blocks in the stack, often occupying 1/3–1/2 of the right column’s visible height depending on field count. Inside, fields are arranged in a compact, two-column layout where appropriate: for example, first and last name share a row (each about 48% width with a small gap), as do city/state or phone/email, while longer fields like street address and email can take the full width of the right column. Labels sit directly above each input with 4–6px spacing, and vertical gaps between rows of fields are tight (8–12px), in line with the information-dense style. The overall block maintains 16–24px of internal padding around the fields, visually grouping donor information as a single, important step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding form section within the right column, scrolling naturally with the page; no sticky behavior, but due to its height it may often form the central content of the viewport when filling in details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector is positioned just below the donor info form, with roughly 24px of vertical spacing to signal a shift from personal information to payment details. It spans the full width of the right column and is organized as a two-part block. At the top, a row of horizontally arranged radio buttons or tab-style selectors (e.g., "Credit/Debit Card", "Bank Transfer") occupies about 70–80% of the right column’s width, left-aligned, using small bevelled pill-style options. Below this row, the active method’s specific fields (card number, expiry, etc.) are shown within a lightly bordered inner panel that fills the right column’s width. Field layout inside the panel follows the same compact pattern as donor info: some fields share a row in two columns, others span full width. The entire selector block is moderately tall—about 1/4–1/3 of the right column height—and visually anchored as a crucial step immediately above the summary and submit area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section that swaps method-specific fields within its own vertical space when users change the selected payment method, without affecting the structure of the rest of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation summary panel appears near the bottom of the right column, directly beneath the payment method selector with a gap of about 24–32px to give it breathing room and emphasis. It is presented as a concise, bordered or lightly shaded box stretching across the full width of the right column, with 16–24px internal padding. Inside, summary lines are arranged in a compact vertical list: fund name, donation type, base amount, processing fee (if selected), and total. Labels are left-aligned, while numeric amounts are right-aligned on the same lines, creating a balanced receipt-like look within about 60–70% of the panel’s height. The final "Total" row uses slightly bolder typography and perhaps a slightly darker tint. The panel’s height is modest (roughly 1/6 of the right column’s visible height), ensuring it feels like a quick confirmation before the submit button rather than another large form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static summary block that updates content dynamically but remains in the same position near the bottom of the right column. It does not stick on scroll; instead, users naturally encounter it just before the submit action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation submit form occupies the lowest part of the right column, immediately under the summary panel, with about 16–20px of vertical separation. It is a relatively shallow horizontal strip spanning the full width of the right column. On the left side of this strip, any required checkboxes (e.g., agreeing to terms or email receipt preferences) and short helper text are stacked or arranged in one or two compact lines. On the right side, aligned with the right column’s edge and slightly weighted towards the bottom of the card, sits the large primary "Donate" button. This button uses the skeuomorphic beveled style with a gradient fill, about 12–16px vertical and 28–36px horizontal padding, making it the most visually prominent control on the page. The button typically extends to roughly 30–40% of the right column’s width. There is an extra 24–32px of padding between the submit strip and the bottom edge of the main card, giving the CTA a clear platform and preventing it from feeling cramped against the card’s border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored at the bottom of the right column’s content. On shorter viewports, this section scrolls into view rather than being sticky; the user completes the form then naturally scrolls down to reach the summary and submit button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful donation, the confirmation view replaces the entire right column’s form stack inside the main card while the left column’s photo and ministry message remain unchanged, preserving the overall two-column balance. The confirmation content is vertically centered within the right column’s available height as much as possible, with generous 32–40px padding above and below. At the top of the confirmation block, a large, centered heading (e.g., "Thank you for your gift") spans about 70–80% of the right column width. Beneath it, a short paragraph of confirmation text and reference details is set in compact serif type, left- or center-aligned, occupying roughly the upper half of the right column. Below this, a smaller summary of the gift (mirroring key items from the summary panel) is presented in a simple, borderless list. Optionally, secondary buttons like "Make another gift" or "Print receipt" appear in a small horizontal row at the lower-right of the right column. The overall effect is a calm, centered thank-you message framed by the unchanging left image and the same white card boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown conditionally in place of the form content within the right column when the donation is successful. Static within the card, with no overlay; the surrounding gradient band and left column remain fixed, helping users recognize they are still on the Donate page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Donation error messages appear as a prominent, but not overwhelming, horizontal alert bar near the top of the right column, just under the donation-type tabs and above the fund designation selector. The bar spans the full width of the right column with 12–16px internal padding and uses a pale red or warm-tinted background with a subtle beveled or bordered edge to fit the skeuomorphic theme. An error icon, if present, sits on the left, followed by the main error message text, constrained to one or two lines before wrapping. If there are multiple errors, a concise summary is shown here, while details may be indicated field-by-field lower down. The bar’s height is modest—roughly equivalent to two lines of body text—and it pushes down the form content when visible, ensuring it doesn’t cover or overlap any fields. Vertical spacing of 8–12px separates this bar from both the tabs above and the fund selector below, keeping it visually distinct but integrated into the form flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block-level component that appears in the right column’s flow when errors are present and collapses entirely (removing its vertical space) when there are no errors; it does not float or overlay the form.

name: Crafts & Activities
title: Crafts & Activities
description: Browse, filter, sort, and save crafts and activities for children’s ministry and families. Users can adjust filters (age range, seasonal topic, duration, difficulty), sort results, and save activities into their personal Saved Resources for the current session.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for crafts and activities using getCraftActivityFilterOptions, then initialize filter controls and URL state.
Display a paginated list of CraftActivity cards (grid or list) using searchCraftActivities with filters and sort_by derived from URL parameters.
Allow filtering by age range, seasonal topic, maximum duration, and difficulty level, updating URL parameters instead of local storage.
Allow sorting results by a user-selected sort option provided by getCraftActivityFilterOptions and reflected in the URL parameter 'sort'.
Provide a Save action on each craft/activity card that calls saveResource with resource_type='craft_activity' and the activity id, updating the in-page saved state.
Allow navigation to individual craft/activity detail pages (e.g., via card click) using URL parameters for the selected activity id.
Provide navigation to the My Saved Resources page (saved_resources.html) after saving items or via a persistent header link.
user_workflows: Pre-filtered entry from homepage: user clicks a link such as 'View crafts for a specific age/topic' on index.html; the link opens crafts_activities.html with URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration, sort); on page load, getCraftActivityFilterOptions is called to populate filter options; URL parameters are mapped into the current filter state; searchCraftActivities is called with filters derived from those parameters; the user sees a pre-filtered list without extra input.

Adjust filters while browsing: user modifies age range, seasonal topic, maximum duration, or difficulty in the filter controls; the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration) and removes parameters for filters that are cleared; after updating the URL, the page calls searchCraftActivities with the new filters and current sort_by; the results list updates to reflect the refined criteria.

Change sort order: user selects a different sort option from the sort control; the page updates the 'sort' URL parameter with the selected sort option value from getCraftActivityFilterOptions.sort_options; searchCraftActivities is called with the same filters but new sort_by; the results list re-renders in the new order.

Paginate through results: user clicks a pagination control to move to another page of results; the page updates a 'page' URL parameter to the selected page index; searchCraftActivities is called with the same filters and sort_by but updated page; the list shows the corresponding slice of activities.

Save an activity to My Saved Resources: user clicks the Save button/icon on a craft/activity card; the page calls saveResource with resource_type='craft_activity' and the selected activity id; if the response indicates success, the page updates an in-memory saved-state map so the UI can reflect that this activity is saved (for the current view); the page optionally shows a non-marketing confirmation message or exposes a 'View Saved' call-to-action that links to saved_resources.html.

Open craft/activity details: user clicks the title or image of a craft/activity card; the page navigates to a dedicated craft detail page (for example, craft_activity.html with an id URL parameter corresponding to the selected CraftActivity.id); this navigation uses URL parameters only and does not require additional interfaces from this page.

Quick-view details (optional flow): user clicks a quick-view action on a card; the page opens a lightweight modal or panel showing more details of the selected CraftActivity using already-fetched data from searchCraftActivities; the user can close the quick view, save the activity (triggering saveResource), or proceed to full detail via a navigation link.
interactions: Initial load: on page initialization, the page reads URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration, sort, page, and any additional supported filter parameters); it calls getCraftActivityFilterOptions with no parameters; once filter options are loaded, it maps the URL parameter values into the appropriate filter control states; then it builds a filters object and calls searchCraftActivities with {filters, sort_by, page, page_size} to render the initial results.

Filter control interaction: when a user changes a filter (for example, selecting a new age range or seasonal topic), the corresponding filter component updates the in-memory filter state; the page serializes the full filter state into URL parameters (adding or replacing minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration, and any difficulty parameter; removing ones that are cleared); the page then calls searchCraftActivities with a filters object derived from this state and resets pagination to the first page; results update accordingly.

Clear filters: when a user clicks a 'Clear filters' control, all filter states are reset; the page removes related URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration, and any difficulty parameter) from the query string while leaving unrelated parameters (such as sort) unchanged; searchCraftActivities is called with either an empty filters object or only the remaining active criteria; results show the unfiltered or less-filtered list.

Sort control interaction: when a user selects a sort option, the control updates the in-memory sort state; the page writes the selected sort option value into the 'sort' URL parameter; searchCraftActivities is called again with the unchanged filters and updated sort_by; the page may also reset the 'page' parameter to the first page when sort changes.

Pagination interaction: when a user selects a page number or next/previous page, the pagination component updates the 'page' URL parameter; searchCraftActivities is called with the same filters and sort_by but the new page; the page scroll position may be adjusted (implementation-specific) to keep list browsing comfortable.

Save button click on card: when a user clicks the Save control on an activity card, an event handler calls saveResource with resource_type='craft_activity' and resource_id equal to the CraftActivity.id from that card; if the response.success is true, the handler updates an in-memory structure storing ids of saved activities for the current page session so that the card can visually reflect its saved status; any optional confirmation text is derived from the response.message, without hardcoded messaging; a separate navigation element can link the user to saved_resources.html.

Navigation to My Saved Resources: when the user clicks the 'My Saved Resources' link in the header or a contextual 'View Saved' call-to-action, the page performs a standard navigation to saved_resources.html without modifying API state on this page.

Card navigation: when a user clicks the main clickable area of a craft/activity card (such as the title or thumbnail), the page navigates to a craft detail page URL that includes the selected CraftActivity.id as a URL parameter; this navigation does not require API calls from this listing page.

Quick-view open/close: when a user clicks a quick-view control, an event handler locates the corresponding CraftActivity object already present in the activities array returned by searchCraftActivities; it sets an in-memory 'selectedActivity' state and opens a modal component bound to this object; when the user closes the modal, the 'selectedActivity' state is cleared and the modal is hidden.
page_functionality.state_logic: The canonical state for this page is maintained via URL query parameters and in-memory page state, without using localStorage. On every page load or URL change, the page parses known parameters: minAge and maxAge (numeric) map to age range filters; seasonalTopic (string) maps to the seasonal topic filter; maxDuration (numeric) maps to a maximum duration filter; sort (string) maps to the sort control and is passed directly as sort_by to searchCraftActivities (it should match one of the values returned by getCraftActivityFilterOptions.sort_options); page (numeric) maps to the current pagination index; any additional filter parameters (such as a difficulty parameter) are read and mapped consistently to filter state and to the filters object passed into searchCraftActivities. After mapping URL parameters into filter and sort state, the page constructs a filters object that aligns with CraftActivity fields (for example, setting minimum and maximum age, seasonal topic, and maximum duration fields when present). It then calls searchCraftActivities with this filters object, the sort_by value from URL or from a default derived from getCraftActivityFilterOptions, the page value from URL (or a default representing the first page if missing), and a page_size derived from application-level configuration or an optional URL parameter (not hardcoded here). When the user interacts with filters, sorting, or pagination, the page updates the URL query string to reflect the full current state, replacing or removing parameters as needed, and then re-runs searchCraftActivities. Saved state for individual activities is tracked in-memory based on responses from saveResource and is not encoded into URL parameters; persistence beyond the current session is handled by the backend via the SavedResource model. The page does not rely on static defaults for filter options or sort choices; instead, it always uses values returned from getCraftActivityFilterOptions and the current URL.

components.functionality: On page load, retrieves filter and sort metadata via getCraftActivityFilterOptions, merges this metadata with any existing URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, seasonalTopic, maxDuration, sort, page, and other supported filters), initializes the in-memory filter and sort state, and triggers the initial searchCraftActivities call.

Provides interactive controls for filtering the crafts and activities list, including age range, seasonal topic, maximum duration, and difficulty level. Uses options from getCraftActivityFilterOptions and current values from URL parameters to display the active filter state. When filters change, it updates the URL parameters and notifies the results component to re-run searchCraftActivities.

Displays a sort dropdown or selector populated from getCraftActivityFilterOptions.sort_options. Reflects the current sort option based on the 'sort' URL parameter. When the user selects a different sort option, it updates the 'sort' parameter in the URL, optionally resets pagination to the first page, and triggers a new searchCraftActivities call.

Executes searchCraftActivities using the current filters, sort_by, page, and page_size, then renders the returned activities as a grid or list of CraftActivity cards. Reacts to state changes triggered by filter-toolbar, sort-control, and pagination-controls, and refreshes when those components update the URL parameters. Handles empty result sets by showing a non-hardcoded, generic message derived from application configuration rather than static copy.

Renders pagination based on total_count, page, and page_size from searchCraftActivities. When a user selects a new page, updates the 'page' URL parameter and triggers activity-results-list to re-call searchCraftActivities with the same filters and sort_by but the updated page index.

Represents an individual CraftActivity in the list, showing key functional data such as title, age range, seasonal topic, estimated duration, and difficulty level based on the CraftActivity model. Provides clickable regions to open the full detail page and optional quick-view. Includes a Save button that delegates to the save-resource-handler component.

Handles saving of a craft/activity when triggered by an activity-card. Calls saveResource with resource_type set to 'craft_activity' and the selected resource_id. Processes the response to update an in-memory map of saved activity ids and exposes a flag that activity-card can use to reflect saved state. May surface a confirmation status based on response.message but does not hardcode any specific message text.

Provides an optional quick-view for a selected CraftActivity using the data already retrieved via searchCraftActivities. When opened, it reads the currently selected activity from in-memory state (set by activity-card), displays additional summary or metadata fields from CraftActivity, and allows the user to trigger saveResource via save-resource-handler or navigate to the full detail page. When closed, it clears the selected activity state.

Provides navigation to the My Saved Resources page. Typically displayed in the header and optionally near a save confirmation. Does not call any interfaces; it simply navigates to saved_resources.html, relying on that page to load and display the user’s SavedResource items.

Centralizes reading from and writing to URL query parameters for filter, sort, and pagination state. On initialization and on browser navigation events, it parses URL parameters into a normalized internal state object used by filter-toolbar, sort-control, and pagination-controls. When those components change state, this manager updates the URL query string accordingly (adding, updating, or removing parameters) and notifies activity-results-list to re-run searchCraftActivities.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: global header and primary navigation sit above, then the Crafts & Activities page content begins with a wide, soft gradient band spanning the full width of the viewport. Centered within this band is a single, large white content card using a fixed-width (about 960–1100px) layout. Inside this card, the page is divided into a two-column structure: a narrower left column for filters and controls (filter-toolbar) and a wider right column for the activity results list, sort controls, and pagination. Supporting behaviors (data initializer, save handler, URL sync) are invisible but associated conceptually with this main card.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: global header and primary navigation sit above, then the Crafts & Activities page content begins with a wide, soft gradient band spanning the full width of the viewport. Centered within this band is a single, large white content card using a fixed-width (about 960–1100px) layout. Inside this card, the page is divided into a two-column structure: a narrower left column for filters and controls (filter-toolbar) and a wider right column for the activity results list, sort controls, and pagination. Supporting behaviors (data initializer, save handler, URL sync) are invisible but associated conceptually with this main card.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and navigation bar occupy the top 15–20% of the viewport. Below, the main gradient band with the Crafts & Activities card fills the remaining area. The centered white content card spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (capped at around 1024px), with generous gradient margins of about 40–60px on all sides. Inside the card, the left column (filters) occupies about 35–40% of the internal width, while the right column (sort control, results list, pagination) uses about 60–65%. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale: 24–32px padding inside the card edges, 16–24px between stacked elements, and 32–40px between the top row of controls and the bottom pagination row.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense, similar to a brochure-style nonprofit site. Filters are grouped into compact sections with small but readable type and limited whitespace. The results grid shows multiple activity cards at once (typically two columns of cards per row on desktop), making efficient use of space without feeling cramped due to consistent padding and clear card boundaries. Typography leans slightly smaller with tighter line length, especially in descriptions and filter labels, in line with the earnest, text-forward style of the original mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern: the eye starts at the global logo and navigation at the top, then moves down into the gradient band and first lands on the top-left of the white content card where the page title (if present) and filters begin. From there, the eye crosses to the top-right where the saved resources navigation and sort control sit, then moves diagonally down into the right column’s first row of activity cards. Scanning continues horizontally across the results grid, and finally down to the pagination at the bottom-right of the card. The gradient background subtly frames the card, and the left-to-right emphasis of filters-to-results reinforces this classic reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header and main navigation row, the Crafts & Activities page opens into a wide, warm gradient band that stretches edge to edge across the screen. Centered within this band is a large, slightly elevated white content card with soft shadows and a hint of beveled edging, echoing a traditional nonprofit brochure. This card has generous padding inside and is divided into two clear columns: on the left, a vertically stacked filter panel runs from near the top of the card downward, grouping age range, seasonal topics, duration, and difficulty into tidy, labeled sections; on the right, aligned with the top of the filters, a narrow strip holds a saved-resources button and sort control above a two-column grid of activity cards. Each activity card is a small white sub-card, echoing the main card’s skeuomorphic style, with its own image and text and a save control. At the very bottom of the right column, a row of beveled pagination buttons sits aligned to the right, giving a clear end to the content card. Invisible technical components handle data initialization, saving, and URL state behind the scenes, but visually the page reads as a single, centered, text-rich panel within a calm, gradient background that guides the eye from filters on the left to activities and navigation on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters-initializer has no direct visual presence. Conceptually, it sits behind the main white content card inside the gradient band, preparing filter data before the filter-toolbar appears. From a layout perspective, the filter-toolbar area within the left column of the card should not shift noticeably as initialization completes; any loading indication (if used) would occupy the same left-column region where the filters will appear, approximately 35–40% of the card’s width starting at the top-left inner corner of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, runs before and during initial render to ensure the filter-toolbar can occupy a stable region in the left column without layout jumps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-toolbar is anchored at the top-left inside the main white content card, forming the full height of the left column. It spans about 35–40% of the card’s internal width and runs vertically from just below the card’s top padding downwards. The top of the filter-toolbar is aligned horizontally with the top of the sort-control in the right column. Inside this panel, filters are arranged in stacked sections: each section uses a slightly shaded or lightly bordered box with a subtle beveled header bar and about 16–24px of internal padding. Section headings (e.g., Age Range, Seasonal Topic, Duration, Difficulty) are left-aligned and separated from their controls by 8px. Individual filter inputs are arranged in compact vertical lists, with 8–12px vertical spacing between options. The left edge of the filter-toolbar maintains a 24–32px inset from the card edge, and there is about 16–24px of space between the right edge of this panel and the start of the results column. The toolbar scrolls with the page; it is not sticky, preserving the traditional, static-column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a fixed-height column within the main content card; scrolls with the page rather than remaining sticky, maintaining its left-aligned position relative to the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control is positioned at the top of the right column, aligned horizontally with the top of the filter-toolbar. It sits just inside the top-right corner of the main white card, respecting the 24–32px internal padding. The control itself appears as a labeled dropdown or segmented selector with a slightly beveled border, roughly 220–280px wide. The label (e.g., Sort by) is left-aligned and sits directly to the left of the dropdown, or runs above it with only 4–8px separation, in keeping with the dense, text-forward style. To the right of or slightly above the sort-control, a saved-resources-navigation button can sit in the same horizontal band, aligned to the far right of the card, with about 8–16px horizontal gap between them. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the bottom of the sort-control row from the first row of activity cards below, clearly demarcating controls from results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, forming the top control row above the results grid; moves up and down with page scroll together with the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-results-list occupies the majority of the right column beneath the sort-control row. It begins approximately 16–24px below the sort-control and extends vertically toward the bottom of the main white card, with its right edge inset about 24–32px from the card border and its left edge about 16–24px from the filter-toolbar’s right boundary. The list is arranged as a two-column grid of activity-card components: each card takes roughly 48% of the right column width, with a 16–24px horizontal gap between the two columns. Vertically, cards are separated by 16–24px. This creates around 2–3 rows of cards visible in a typical viewport. The grid feels structured and moderately dense, echoing the brochure style: cards line up cleanly, with consistent baselines and no masonry staggering. The bottom of the results-list leaves enough space (about 24–32px) above the pagination-controls row, keeping the pagination visually separate and clearly at the end of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard grid within the right column, expanding vertically and pushing the card’s bottom edge down as more activities are shown; scrolls as part of the page rather than in its own scrolling container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the main white content card, directly below the activity-results-list in the right column. They sit on a single horizontal line aligned to the right edge of the card’s content area, approximately 24–32px above the card’s lower edge to match the internal padding. The controls appear as a compact row of beveled buttons (Previous, individual page numbers, Next), each with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, echoing the style of the primary navigation buttons in the header but slightly smaller. There is about 8px of horizontal spacing between buttons and 16–24px of vertical space separating the pagination row from the last row of activity cards above. The left side of this controls row may be visually anchored by a small line of text like “Page X of Y,” keeping the main focus on the right-aligned buttons while still maintaining balance.
