component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row anchored to the bottom of the right column’s content area; moves with the page and always appears immediately after the results list without floating or sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each activity-card is a small, self-contained white sub-card, styled to echo the main content card with a subtle shadow, soft corner radius, and a faint beveled border. Within the activity-results-list grid, each card occupies roughly half of the right column’s width (about 48%), with consistent vertical height determined by its content. Inside the card, the layout loosely mirrors the original mockup: a thumbnail image or graphic sits on the left side of the card (around 35–40% of the card’s width), and the activity’s title and brief description occupy the right side (about 60–65%). The card has 16–24px padding on all sides. The title is top-aligned, with a short description beneath it and small metadata (age range, duration, difficulty) stacked compactly below. A Save button or icon is positioned in the top-right corner of the card, aligned with the title baseline, using a small beveled button style. A Quick View link or button may sit along the bottom-right edge of the card, 8–12px above the lower padding, giving a clear, consistent tap/click area. Visually, cards are moderately dense but readable, with tight but not cramped line spacing, keeping the grid feeling information-rich yet organized.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating unit within the results grid; each card maintains its internal left-image/right-text layout regardless of its position in the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-resource-handler is not visible itself but is tied to Save controls on each activity-card and the saved-resources-navigation control. Visually, its effects are localized to those components: when an activity is saved, the Save button within that activity-card may change state (color, label, or a small checkmark icon), and the saved-resources-navigation indicator may update a count. No separate space is reserved for this handler; it functions entirely within the existing footprint of the cards and navigation link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates behind the scenes without affecting the overall layout or causing reflow beyond state changes within existing components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-view-modal appears as a centered overlay on top of the entire page when triggered from an activity-card. The background page (including the gradient band and main content card) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark veil, but remains partially visible at the edges. The modal itself is a prominent white card, approximately 60–70% of the viewport width and 60–70% of the viewport height, with a slightly stronger shadow and beveled edge to distinguish it from the base card. It sits horizontally and vertically centered within the viewport with at least 48–64px margin from the window edges on smaller screens. Inside, the layout mirrors the original mockup closely: a larger version of the activity image occupies the left 40–45% of the modal width, while detailed text content (full description, instructions, materials) fills the right 55–60%. A close button is placed in the modal’s top-right corner, aligned with the internal padding (around 24–32px), and primary actions (Save, Print, or Start) can be centered or right-aligned along the bottom edge of the modal content area. Vertical scrolling within the modal is allowed if content exceeds available height, but the modal itself remains fixed in the center of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a dimmed full-screen backdrop; remains in place while the page beneath does not scroll (or appears locked) until the modal is closed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-resources-navigation appears as a compact but visually distinct button or pill-shaped link within the top control row of the main content card. It is aligned to the far right edge of the card’s inner content area, sharing the same horizontal band as the sort-control but taking precedence in positioning. The button uses a beveled look consistent with the site’s primary navigation: slightly rounded corners, subtle gradient (lighter at the top, darker at the bottom), and 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. If a saved count is shown, it appears as a small badge or text label within the button (e.g., “Saved Resources (3)”), maintaining a single-line label. There is about 8–16px of horizontal space between this button and the nearest element (usually the sort-control) to its left. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the top edge of the main card, visually anchoring the top-right corner of the content area and acting as a secondary navigation element focused on the current session.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control within the top row of the right column; does not stick or float, but remains at the top of the card content and scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer is invisible and occupies no independent space in the visual layout. It conceptually ties to the main white content card, ensuring that the state of filters, sort-control, pagination, and possibly which activity is open in the quick-view-modal are reflected in the browser’s URL. It operates entirely behind the scenes, so the layout for visible components remains consistent and stable during state updates; filters stay in the left column, results and pagination remain in the right column, and the main card’s position within the gradient band does not shift because of URL changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; hooks into stateful components without altering their spatial behavior or causing visible reflow, maintaining the fixed, centered layout established by the mockup.

name: Saved Resources & Collections
title: Saved Resources & Collections
description: Workspace where users view all their saved crafts and lesson plans, organize them into collections, and open specific collections for planning and reuse.

core_features: Load and display all saved resources (crafts, activities, lesson plans) for the current user using getSavedResourcesOverview.
Load and display all existing resource collections (folders) for the current user using getSavedResourcesOverview.
Optionally auto-select and open a collection detail view when a collectionId URL parameter is present, using getCollectionDetail.
Allow users to create new collections with a custom name and optional description using createResourceCollection.
Allow users to select multiple saved resources via checkboxes and maintain a list of selected saved_resource_ids in page state.
Provide an 'Add to Collection' control that lets users choose a collection and bulk-add all currently selected saved resources using addSavedResourcesToCollection.
Show a focused view of a selected collection’s contents (resources grouped together) using getCollectionDetail.
Allow users to rename an existing collection using renameResourceCollection.
Allow users to delete an existing collection (without deleting the underlying saved resources) using deleteResourceCollection.
Provide navigation links from the saved resources and collections views to browse additional crafts/activities and lesson plans on their respective pages.
user_workflows: View all saved resources: 1) User navigates to saved_resources.html without specifying collectionId. 2) Page calls getSavedResourcesOverview and stores the returned saved_resources and collections in page state. 3) Saved resources are listed with thumbnails, titles, type labels, and age range labels. 4) Collections are listed in a sidebar. 5) User can click into individual saved resources’ originating pages via existing resource details (handled on other pages).

Create a new collection: 1) User opens saved_resources.html. 2) getSavedResourcesOverview loads existing collections. 3) User opens the 'New collection' form in the sidebar. 4) User enters a collection name (and optionally description) and submits. 5) Page calls createResourceCollection with the entered name and description. 6) On success, the new ResourceCollection object from the response is appended to the collections list in page state. 7) The sidebar re-renders to include the new collection, which can immediately be selected as a target in the 'Add to Collection' control.

Select resources and add them to a collection: 1) After getSavedResourcesOverview has loaded, user checks one or more checkboxes next to saved resources in the main list. 2) Page updates a selectedSavedResourceIds array in state to reflect the checked items. 3) User opens the 'Add to Collection' dropdown and selects a target collection from the collections loaded in page state. 4) User clicks the 'Add' action. 5) Page calls addSavedResourcesToCollection with the chosen collectionId and the selectedSavedResourceIds array. 6) On success, the page may optionally detect if the currently open collection (via URL collectionId) matches the target; if so, it can refresh that collection’s details by calling getCollectionDetail again. 7) The selection is cleared (selectedSavedResourceIds becomes empty) to avoid accidental re-use.

Open and view a specific collection from the sidebar: 1) User views saved_resources.html with collections listing visible. 2) User clicks the name of a collection. 3) The click updates the browser location to saved_resources.html?collectionId={id} (either via full page load or client-side routing). 4) On load or URL change, the page reads the collectionId parameter and calls getCollectionDetail with that collectionId. 5) The collection detail view shows the collection’s name and the list of resources it contains (items array with title, resource_type, age_range_label, thumbnail_image_url). 6) The clicked collection is visually indicated as active in the sidebar using the collectionId from the URL.

Open and view a specific collection via direct link: 1) User navigates to a saved link such as saved_resources.html?collectionId={id}, potentially from another page. 2) On initial page load, the page calls getSavedResourcesOverview to get all saved resources and collections. 3) In parallel or after that call resolves, the page reads collectionId from the URL and calls getCollectionDetail with that value. 4) The collection’s items are displayed in the collection detail view, while the sidebar collection list highlights the matching collection. 5) If the provided collectionId does not match any collection in the collections list, the page handles this gracefully (e.g., hide the detail view and show a message or fallback to showing all saved resources).

Rename an existing collection: 1) User has a collection detail view open (via collectionId) or sees a collection listed in the sidebar. 2) User activates a 'Rename' control associated with that collection. 3) A rename input field is shown pre-filled with the current collection name. 4) User edits the name and submits. 5) Page calls renameResourceCollection with collectionId and new_name. 6) On success, the updated ResourceCollection object in the response replaces the corresponding collection entry in the collections list in state. 7) If the renamed collection is currently selected (collectionId matches URL), the collection detail header is also updated.

Delete a collection: 1) User has a collection visible in the sidebar or open in the detail view. 2) User activates a 'Delete collection' control. 3) Page shows a confirmation interaction (e.g., confirm dialog) referencing the collection name from state. 4) On confirmation, page calls deleteResourceCollection with that collectionId. 5) On success, the collection is removed from the collections list in state. 6) If the deleted collectionId matches the collectionId URL parameter, the page updates the URL to saved_resources.html (without collectionId) and hides the collection detail view, falling back to showing the general saved resources list. 7) Underlying SavedResource records remain untouched; they remain visible in the main saved resources list if still saved.

Browse more crafts and activities from the Saved Resources page: 1) User is on saved_resources.html viewing their saved items. 2) User clicks a 'Find more crafts & activities' navigation control located near the saved crafts section. 3) Browser navigates to crafts_activities.html (no parameters). 4) User can save additional resources there, then later revisit saved_resources.html to see them in this workspace.

Browse more lesson plans from the Saved Resources page: 1) User is on saved_resources.html viewing saved lesson resources. 2) User clicks a 'Browse lesson plans' navigation control. 3) Browser navigates to lesson_plans.html (no parameters). 4) User can save additional lesson plans there, then return to saved_resources.html to organize them into collections.
interactions: Page load without collectionId: on initial load of saved_resources.html, the page reads the URL; if no collectionId is present, it calls getSavedResourcesOverview and shows the saved resources list and collections sidebar, while keeping the collection detail view hidden or in an unselected state.

Page load with collectionId: on initial load of saved_resources.html with a collectionId parameter, the page first calls getSavedResourcesOverview to populate sidebar collections and saved resources; then it calls getCollectionDetail with that collectionId to populate the collection detail section and indicates the matching collection in the sidebar as active.

Resource selection via checkbox: each saved resource in the general list includes a checkbox; when the user toggles a checkbox, the page adds or removes that saved resource’s id from the selectedSavedResourceIds array in state; a 'select all' checkbox may toggle all listed resources, updating selectedSavedResourceIds accordingly.

Add to Collection dropdown and submit: the 'Add to Collection' control references the collections array from getSavedResourcesOverview to populate a dropdown; when the user picks a collection and submits, the page calls addSavedResourcesToCollection with the chosen collectionId and the selectedSavedResourceIds; on success, the page clears selections and optionally refreshes the currently viewed collection if relevant.

Create collection form submission: when the 'New collection' form is submitted, the page validates that a name is present and calls createResourceCollection; on success, it appends the returned collection to the collections list in state and clears the form fields.

Collection selection in sidebar: when a user clicks a collection name in the sidebar, the page updates the browser location to saved_resources.html?collectionId={collection.id} and triggers a load of the collection detail via getCollectionDetail; the clicked collection is marked as active based on the current collectionId parameter.

Collection rename interaction: when a user clicks a 'Rename' control for a collection, the component toggles into edit mode, showing an input with the existing name; after editing and submitting, the page calls renameResourceCollection; on success, the collections list and any open collection detail header are updated with the new name and the edit mode is closed.

Collection delete interaction: when a user clicks a 'Delete' control for a collection, a confirmation step is shown; upon confirmation, the page calls deleteResourceCollection; on success, the collection is removed from the collections list; if the deleted collection is currently selected via URL, the URL is updated to remove collectionId and the detail section is cleared.

Collection detail rendering: when getCollectionDetail returns, the collection detail component binds to the returned collection and items arrays, displaying each item’s title, resource_type label, age_range_label, and thumbnail_image_url (if present); this list is independent of the main saved resources list but refers to the same underlying SavedResource records via collection_item.saved_resource_id.

Navigation to crafts_activities.html: a button within the saved resources context triggers standard browser navigation to crafts_activities.html when clicked, without calling any additional interfaces from this page.
Navigation to lesson_plans.html: a link near saved lesson resources triggers navigation to lesson_plans.html when clicked, without calling any additional interfaces from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the collectionId URL parameter. On every load or URL change, the page performs these steps: (1) Parse the collectionId parameter from the current URL (if present). (2) Call getSavedResourcesOverview with no parameters to retrieve the user's saved_resources array and collections array; the response is stored in page-level state, which is then used by the saved resources list and collections sidebar. (3) If a collectionId parameter exists and appears to be a valid identifier, call getCollectionDetail with that collectionId to load the collection detail view; the returned collection and items are stored in state dedicated to the active collection view. (4) The active collection in the sidebar is determined solely by comparing each collection.id to the collectionId parameter; no active state is stored outside of what can be derived from the URL. (5) When a user clicks on a collection name in the sidebar, the page updates the URL to saved_resources.html?collectionId={id}, causing the collectionId-based logic above to run (in a full reload or within a client-side router). (6) When a user deletes a collection that matches the URL collectionId, the page updates the URL to saved_resources.html (no collectionId) so that the collection detail view is cleared and only the general saved resources overview is shown. (7) When a user wants to clear the collection selection without deleting the collection, the page can navigate to saved_resources.html without any collectionId parameter, which results in getSavedResourcesOverview being called and the collection detail not being loaded. (8) Transient UI selections, such as the list of selectedSavedResourceIds for bulk actions, are maintained in in-memory page state only and are not written into the URL or any external storage; they are cleared after successful addSavedResourcesToCollection calls or when the page is reloaded. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used; the only persistent state outside the backend is the URL query parameter collectionId.

components.functionality: On page load or when the user navigates back to the root saved_resources.html view, this component calls getSavedResourcesOverview to fetch all saved resources and collections, and stores them in a shared page state used by the list and sidebar components.
Displays the list of existing collections, highlights the currently active collection based on the collectionId URL parameter, and provides entry points to create, rename, and delete collections.
Provides inputs for collection name and optional description, and on submit calls createResourceCollection; on success, it updates the collections list in state and clears the form.

Allows renaming an existing collection by pre-filling the current name, accepting a new name, and calling renameResourceCollection; updates the corresponding ResourceCollection in the collections list and, if applicable, the active collection detail header.

Triggers deletion of a collection after user confirmation by calling deleteResourceCollection; removes the collection from the collections list and, if it is currently active (matches collectionId in URL), updates the URL to remove collectionId and clears any loaded collection detail state.

Shows all saved resources (regardless of collection) using data from getSavedResourcesOverview; each item includes basic metadata and a checkbox for multi-selection used in bulk add-to-collection actions.

Uses the current selection of saved resources from page state and the collections list from getSavedResourcesOverview to let the user choose a collection and bulk-add selected saved resources by calling addSavedResourcesToCollection.

Whenever a collectionId exists in the URL, this component calls getCollectionDetail with that collectionId and stores the returned collection and items in state for rendering in the collection detail view.
Displays the contents of the currently selected collection using the collection and items data from getCollectionDetail, including resource titles, types, age ranges, and thumbnails.
Provides navigation controls from the Saved Resources page to crafts_activities.html and lesson_plans.html so users can find additional resources to save.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width site header (defined elsewhere), followed immediately by a centered row of large navigation links specific to Saved Resources. Below this, a wide horizontal gradient band fills most of the remaining viewport height and holds a single prominent white content card in the center. Inside this card, content is arranged as a two-column layout: a left column for collections navigation and management, and a right column for viewing and managing individual resources and collections. Forms and controls are embedded within these columns rather than floating as modern minimal overlays, echoing a traditional application-style workspace.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width site header (defined elsewhere), followed immediately by a centered row of large navigation links specific to Saved Resources. Below this, a wide horizontal gradient band fills most of the remaining viewport height and holds a single prominent white content card in the center. Inside this card, content is arranged as a two-column layout: a left column for collections navigation and management, and a right column for viewing and managing individual resources and collections. Forms and controls are embedded within these columns rather than floating as modern minimal overlays, echoing a traditional application-style workspace.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top header area consumes roughly 15–20% of viewport height, with the Saved Resources navigation links directly underneath as a distinct horizontal row. The gradient band then occupies the remaining 80–85% of the screen height. The main white content card sits centered in this band, spanning about 80–85% of the fixed-width content area (roughly 960–1100px) with generous outer margins of about 40–60px top and bottom and 32–48px left and right within the gradient. Inside the card, the left column (collections sidebar and collection creation control) uses about 40–45% of the card width, and the right column (resource lists, bulk actions, rename/delete controls) uses about 55–60%, separated by about 16–24px of gutter. Vertical spacing between functional bands inside the card (titles, actions, lists) follows the 16–32px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The card is moderately dense, mirroring the nonprofit brochure feel: lists show multiple rows at once, labels and helper text are present, and paragraphs or multi-line labels are allowed. Internal padding of 24–32px around the edge of the card keeps breathing room. The sidebar stacks a heading, list of collections, and a compact create form with 8–16px spacing between elements. The right column stacks a title row with controls, a bulk action strip, then the main list, keeping everything visible without feeling airy. Forms are compact, with labels close to their fields and buttons slightly bevelled, continuing the early-2010s skeuomorphic tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow closely follows a Z-pattern. The eye starts in the global header at the top-left logo, moves horizontally to the page title and Saved Resources navigation links centered below. From there, the eye drops diagonally into the gradient band and lands first on the left side of the white card (collections sidebar), which acts like the dominant image block from the mockup. Then attention moves horizontally across to the right column, where the collection title, rename/delete controls, and bulk action strip lead into the main list of resources. A subtle left-to-right emphasis is maintained with slightly lighter gradient tones behind the left edge of the card and marginally darker tones towards the right, reinforcing progression from navigation (collections) to details (resources).

layout.overall_description: The Saved Resources & Collections page appears as a traditional, centered application workspace framed by a warm gradient background. At the top of the screen, the existing site header and main navigation run full-width, followed closely by a single, centered row of large beveled navigation links for the Saved Resources area. Below this, a soft gradient band fills the bulk of the viewport, within which a single white, slightly raised card is centered, echoing a printed page laid on colored paper. This card has generous padding and is split into two columns. On the left, a vertical collections sidebar occupies a bit less than half the card width, listing existing collections with a small embedded form for creating new ones at the top or bottom. On the right, a broader content area shows the currently selected collection or the general saved resources list, with the collection name and management controls at the top, a slightly shaded bulk action bar beneath, and then a dense list of resource rows. Loading states softly overlay either the entire card or just the right panel when needed, dimming content while a spinner or progress indicator appears at center. The whole composition feels stable, text-forward, and conservative—more like a structured desktop application trapped in a fixed-width web layout than a modern, fluid app—while still guiding the user smoothly from top navigation to left collections to right details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview loader is positioned as an overlay exactly matching the inner bounds of the main white content card within the gradient band. It respects the card’s centered fixed width and rounded corners, sitting 24–32px inside the card’s edges on all sides. When active (such as on initial page load), it lightly dims all internal content in both the left collections sidebar and the right resources area with a semi-transparent veil, and places a medium-sized spinner or progress indicator in the visual center of the card. This center point aligns horizontally with the midline between the left and right columns and vertically about halfway between the card’s top header area and the bottom of the lists. When inactive, it collapses and does not consume visible space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-card overlay positioned relative to the main content card, using absolute positioning within the card container. It does not affect layout flow and scrolls together with the card’s content if the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the collections sidebar is anchored to the left side, spanning from near the top padding down towards the bottom, with about 24–32px of card padding separating it from the card’s top and left edges. It occupies approximately 40–45% of the card’s width. The sidebar is structured vertically: at the top, a heading such as “Your Collections” sits left-aligned, followed by a vertical list of collection items. This list fills most of the sidebar’s height, scrolling internally if necessary while maintaining the card’s outer dimensions. Each collection entry appears as a horizontally full-width row with a slightly shaded or bordered background on hover or when selected, preserving the skeuomorphic feel. At the bottom area of the sidebar (or just beneath the list), there is reserved space for the collection-create-form, with about 16–24px of vertical separation. A 16–24px gutter separates the sidebar’s right edge from the start of the right-hand content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card, occupying a fixed column on the left. If the sidebar content overflows vertically, it scrolls within its own column while the card remains fixed-height relative to viewport, reinforcing a panel-like feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection create form is embedded within the lower portion of the collections-sidebar column. It is left-aligned, spanning about 90–100% of the sidebar’s inner width, with 16–24px of vertical spacing above it separating it from the last collection item. The form is compact, consisting of a single-line text field for the new collection name and a beveled “Create” button positioned immediately to the right of the field or directly beneath it if horizontal space is tight. Internal padding within the form block is minimal (8–16px) to match the moderately dense feel, and the whole form may be outlined by a faint border or subtle background tint to distinguish it from the list above while still feeling part of the same column. There is about 16–24px of bottom margin before the sidebar’s bottom padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored within the left column. It does not float or overlay; it scrolls with the sidebar content if the collections list is long.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection rename form appears in the upper portion of the right column, closely associated with the currently selected collection’s title. It occupies a horizontal strip about 60–70% of the right column’s width, centered vertically within the title area. By default, the right column shows the collection name as a text heading near the top left of the column. When renaming, that heading transforms into or is replaced by the rename form: a single-line text input spanning roughly 70–80% of the width of this title area, aligned to the left, with Save and Cancel bevelled buttons immediately to its right (or stacked beneath on narrow widths within the fixed content area). This form is positioned about 24–32px below the card’s top padding and 16–24px to the right of the inner card edge. It does not extend below the bulk action panel which sits underneath; instead, the panel moves slightly downward to maintain 12–16px spacing beneath the rename form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the collection title row in the right column. It remains static within the layout and pushes content beneath it downward while active, without floating over other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection delete control is a small but visually distinct beveled button (often with a warm accent like muted red) positioned in the top right area of the right column’s header row. It aligns horizontally with the collection title or rename form, but flush to the right edge of the right column’s inner padding (about 24–32px in from the card’s right edge). Vertically, it sits on the same baseline as the title text or input field, with 8–12px of horizontal gap between it and any adjacent rename or options controls. The button’s size is compact—around the same height as the title’s text line plus padding—ensuring it is visible but not overpowering. There is 12–16px of space below this header row before the add-to-collection-control begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top-right corner of the right column’s header area. It scrolls with the rest of the card content and does not float or stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved resources list occupies the main body portion of the right column when the user is viewing their general saved resources rather than a specific collection. It begins immediately below the add-to-collection-control, with about 16–24px of vertical separation, and extends down towards the bottom padding of the card. The list spans the full inner width of the right column (minus 24–32px of side padding), displaying each resource as a horizontal row. Each row may include a title, brief description, tags, and a checkbox for selection, arranged in a slightly dense, text-forward style. Rows are separated by 8–12px vertical spacing or faint dividing lines. If content overflows, the entire card scrolls within the page, but the right column’s list is visually continuous and not boxed separately. When a specific collection is selected to view, this component either hides or is replaced by the collection-resources-view in the same vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, filling available vertical space under the bulk action panel. It scrolls with the page and is swapped out contextually with collection-resources-view depending on state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-collection control appears as a horizontal bulk-action strip at the top of the right column, directly beneath the collection title/rename area. It spans nearly the full width of the right column, with 16–24px of vertical spacing above (separating it from the title row) and below (before the main resource list begins). This strip can use a subtle shaded or gradient background to distinguish it as a control bar, echoing the skeuomorphic style. On the left side of the bar, there is explanatory text such as “Add selected items to a collection,” aligned left with a modest 8–16px padding. On the right side, there is a dropdown or select input for choosing a collection plus a beveled confirmation button (e.g., “Add”), aligned horizontally. The overall height of this band is around 48–64px, making it tall enough to feel like a dedicated toolbar without becoming dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static toolbar-like strip within the right column. It stays attached to the top of the resource list and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down the page; it is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection detail loader overlays only the right column’s content area, not the entire card. It is constrained within the right column boundaries starting just below the title/rename/delete header and extending down to the bottom padding of the card. When active—such as when switching between collections—it softly dims the resource list (saved-resources-list or collection-resources-view) and the bulk action strip, while keeping the left collections sidebar fully visible for continued navigation. A spinner or loading indicator is centered within the right column’s visible area, roughly midway between the top of the bulk action strip and the bottom of the list content. The overlay follows the internal padding of the right column, maintaining about 16–24px inset from the card’s inner border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay positioned absolutely relative to the right column container. It does not impact layout flow and scrolls with the card; the left column remains interactive or visually undimmed to indicate that navigation remains available during loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection resources view occupies the same spatial region as the saved-resources-list but is shown when a specific collection is selected. It begins directly beneath the add-to-collection-control bar, with 16–24px of vertical spacing, and expands downward to the card’s bottom padding. It spans the full width of the right column’s content area and is structured as a vertical list of resource rows belonging to the selected collection, each row similar in density and styling to the saved-resources-list entries: titles, brief details, and selection controls. An optional small summary line (e.g., “12 items in this collection”) can appear at the top of this view, left-aligned above the list with 8–12px spacing. If the list is long, the entire page scrolls. When the user switches back to an “All Saved Resources” context, this view is hidden and the saved-resources-list takes over the same space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, alternately rendered in place of saved-resources-list. It scrolls in the normal page flow and does not overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved resources navigation links form a distinct horizontal row positioned directly beneath the site’s main header and top navigation bar, before the gradient band and main content card. Centered within the fixed-width layout, this row spans approximately 70–80% of the content width and is horizontally aligned with the card below. The links are styled as large, beveled buttons with 8–12px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding each, arranged side-by-side and centered as a single line or wrapping to two lines if there are many options. There is around 16–24px of vertical spacing above this row (separating it from the global header) and 24–32px below it before the gradient band begins. The background behind this row is a solid or slightly textured bar that visually connects to the header above, emphasizing it as part of the primary navigation path into the Saved Resources area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal navigation bar within the main page flow. It does not stick on scroll; as the user scrolls down, it moves off-screen along with the header.

name: Lesson Plans & Series Creation
title: Lesson Plans & Series Planner
description: Browse and filter lesson plans, view Sundays for a selected month, create lesson series for specific age ranges and dates, add lesson plans into a series, and save individual lesson plans for later use.

core_features: Initialize and display filter controls for lesson plans using getLessonPlanFilterOptions and any matching URL parameters (age range, topic category, topic subcategory), then load results via searchLessonPlans.
Provide a lesson plan listing with pagination, showing key details such as title, age range, scripture focus, duration, and usage counts, sourced from searchLessonPlans.
Offer additional filters for scripture reference, complexity, and duration fields and re-query searchLessonPlans when they change.
Render a month/year planner panel that calls getLessonPlannerCalendar to list all Sundays for the selected month and year, and keep month/year in sync with URL parameters.
Provide a series creation form that uses createLessonSeries with the current age range and selected month/year, then exposes navigation to series_detail.html for the newly created series.
Enable an 'Add to Series' action from each lesson plan card that opens a dialog, lets the user select an existing or newly created series and a week number, and calls addLessonToSeries.
Track lessons added to series during the current session via SeriesLessonAssignment responses and show per-lesson indicators when a lesson is part of the currently focused series.
Allow users to save any lesson plan as a personal saved resource by calling saveResource with resource_type set to the lesson type, and provide a link to navigate to saved_resources.html.
Support navigation out to series_overview.html and series_detail.html via explicit links and buttons, passing required URL parameters such as the lesson series id.
Maintain all page state that needs to survive refresh or sharing (filters, sort, pagination, month/year, focused series) through URL parameters instead of local storage.
user_workflows: Workflow: Browse and filter lesson plans. Steps: (1) User opens lesson_plans.html (via navigation or a deep link including optional URL parameters such as minAge, maxAge, topicCategory, topicSubcategory, month, year, seriesId). (2) On page initialization, the filter form component calls getLessonPlanFilterOptions, merges those options with URL parameters to set initial filter values, and then calls searchLessonPlans with filters and initial sort/page information. (3) The lesson plan list displays results and total count. (4) User adjusts filters (age, topic, scripture text input, complexity input, duration range, sort option); each change updates the URL query parameters and triggers searchLessonPlans with updated filters, resetting the page number when appropriate. (5) User uses pagination controls; selecting a page updates the page parameter in the URL and calls searchLessonPlans with the new page.

Workflow: Plan a month and create a lesson series. Steps: (1) If the URL includes month and year, the series planner panel calls getLessonPlannerCalendar with those values; otherwise, it waits for the user to choose a month and year in its selector and then calls getLessonPlannerCalendar. (2) The panel displays returned Sundays for the selected month as planning context. (3) The series creation form pre-fills target_min_age and target_max_age from current filter state or minAge/maxAge URL parameters if present, and pre-fills month and year from the planner panel/URL. (4) User enters a series name (and optional description) and submits the form. (5) The form calls createLessonSeries with name, description, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, and a number_of_weeks value that can be derived from the current planner Sundays list or entered by the user. (6) On success, the form stores the returned LessonSeries in page state, updates the URL to include seriesId for that series, and displays a confirmation with an "Open in Planner" button that navigates to series_detail.html?id={lessonSeries.id}.

Workflow: Add a lesson plan to a series using the planner. Steps: (1) User identifies a lesson in the lesson plan list and clicks its "Add to Series" action. (2) The add-to-series dialog opens with the current lessonPlanId and pre-selects a series if there is a seriesId URL parameter or a series recently created on this page; otherwise it leaves the series selector empty. (3) The series selector lists known series options, including: the focused series from URL (if present, labeled using available identifiers) and any LessonSeries created on this page via createLessonSeries. (4) The dialog also shows a week selector linked to the Sundays currently loaded by getLessonPlannerCalendar; the user selects a week number (the implementation may show dates and underlying week indices without hardcoding week labels). (5) On submit, the dialog calls addLessonToSeries with lessonSeriesId (selected series), lessonPlanId (from the card), and initial_week_number (selected week). (6) On success, the dialog stores the returned SeriesLessonAssignment in a mapping in page state, shows a success message, and closes or allows additional assignment actions. (7) The lesson card now displays an indicator that this lesson is part of the currently focused series for the selected week number.

Workflow: Save a lesson plan for later and view saved resources. Steps: (1) User clicks the "Save" or favorite toggle on a lesson plan card. (2) The card calls saveResource with resource_type set to represent a lesson plan and resource_id set to the LessonPlan.id. (3) On a successful response, the card updates its local saved state (for example, marking the control as active) without hardcoding any descriptive labels beyond generic status. (4) If the user clicks the "View My Saved Resources" link on the page, the browser navigates to saved_resources.html, optionally preserving some context via URL parameters if the surrounding application provides them.
interactions: On page load, the filter form component triggers getLessonPlanFilterOptions (onInit). Once options are loaded, it reads current URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, topicCategory, topicSubcategory, month, year, seriesId, and any additional filter parameters such as scripture, complexity, duration, sortBy, page, pageSize) and initializes its internal state accordingly before calling searchLessonPlans.

When the user selects or changes an age range in the filter form (onChange), the component updates its internal min_age/max_age, writes the new minAge and maxAge values into the URL query string, resets the page parameter to represent the first page, and calls searchLessonPlans with the updated filters.

When the user selects a topic category or subcategory (onChange), the filter form updates its topic_category/topic_subcategory filter values, syncs topicCategory/topicSubcategory query parameters in the URL, resets pagination as needed, and calls searchLessonPlans with the new filters.

When the user types in a scripture reference or keyword (onChange or onSubmit), the scripture filter field updates the filter state (for example, filters.scripture_reference) and, on explicit submission or debounce, calls searchLessonPlans, also updating an appropriate scripture-related URL parameter so that the search can be restored on refresh.

When the user supplies a complexity filter value (for example, entering a level or range) or adjusts duration-related inputs, the filter form updates corresponding fields within the filters object, writes these values to related URL parameters, and re-runs searchLessonPlans.
When the user changes the sort option, the filter form updates a sortBy value in state and URL, and calls searchLessonPlans with this new sort_by value and current filters.

Pagination controls respond to user clicks on next/previous or a specific page (onClick). The pagination component updates the page parameter (and optionally page_size if the user can change it) in the URL and invokes searchLessonPlans with existing filters and the new page/page_size values.

The series planner panel reads month and year from the URL on initialization (onInit). If both are present, it calls getLessonPlannerCalendar and stores the resulting Sundays for display and for mapping week numbers. If either is missing, it waits for the user to select month and year before calling getLessonPlannerCalendar.

When the user changes the month or year in the planner panel (onChange), the component updates its internal month/year state, updates the month and year query parameters in the URL, and re-calls getLessonPlannerCalendar with the new values.

The series creation form listens to age and month/year state (onInit and onStateChange). When the user submits the form (onSubmit), it calls createLessonSeries with name, description, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, and number_of_weeks derived from current context or user input. On success, it adds the returned LessonSeries to an in-memory list of known series, sets the URL seriesId parameter to the new series id, and exposes navigation to series_detail.html with the id in the query string.

Clicking the "Open in Planner" action for a created series (onClick) changes the location to series_detail.html, appending id={lessonSeries.id} as a URL parameter so the Series Planner page can load the relevant series.
The "View all series" or "Series Planner" link (onClick) navigates to series_overview.html without additional parameters, unless the surrounding application adds more.

Clicking the "Save" action on a lesson card (onClick) triggers saveResource with resource_type corresponding to a lesson plan and resource_id equal to the card's LessonPlan.id. On a successful response, the component updates a SavedResource-related state to reflect that the current user has saved this lesson, changing the control's appearance or status without hardcoding resource text.

Clicking the "Add to Series" action (onClick) on a lesson card opens the add-to-series dialog with the selected lessonPlanId and any available context, such as the current seriesId and loaded Sundays. The dialog can pre-populate series and week fields using this context.

Within the add-to-series dialog, when the user selects a series (onChange), the dialog updates its selectedSeriesId; selecting a week index or Sunday (onChange) updates the initial_week_number it will submit. On form submission (onSubmit), the dialog calls addLessonToSeries with the chosen lessonSeriesId, lessonPlanId, and initial_week_number. If successful, it stores the resulting SeriesLessonAssignment in a data structure (for example, keyed by lesson_plan_id and lesson_series_id) and signals the associated lesson card to render a membership indicator.

Each lesson card subscribes to the membership data structure. When that data indicates the card's LessonPlan.id is associated with the currently focused series (for example, seriesId from URL or selectedSeriesId in local state), the card displays an indicator showing it is part of that series, possibly including the recorded week_number from the assignment.
The "View My Saved Resources" link (onClick) navigates the browser to saved_resources.html, leaving lesson_plans.html.

All components rely on URL parameters instead of local storage for any state that must persist across reloads or be shareable as a link. Inter-component communication for transient UI state (such as which dialog is open) remains in memory and does not touch the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: Page-level state is anchored in URL query parameters and is read on initialization, then kept in sync as the user interacts. On initial load, the page reads minAge, maxAge, topicCategory, topicSubcategory, month, year, seriesId, and any additional known parameters such as page, pageSize, sortBy, scripture, complexity, and duration-related parameters. The filter form combines this with data from getLessonPlanFilterOptions to set initial dropdown and input values and then calls searchLessonPlans with filters derived from those values. Age-related URL parameters (minAge, maxAge) map to the filters fields representing min_age and max_age, and they may also be used as defaults for series creation (target_min_age and target_max_age). Topic-related URL parameters (topicCategory, topicSubcategory) map directly to filters.topic_category and filters.topic_subcategory; changing these via UI updates both the internal filter state and the URL to reflect the new values. Pagination state is tracked using URL parameters such as page and pageSize; whenever the user changes page, those parameters are updated and searchLessonPlans is invoked with the new values. Sorting state is tracked similarly with a sortBy parameter that maps to the sort_by argument of searchLessonPlans. The planning context uses month and year URL parameters as the source of truth: when both are present, the series planner panel calls getLessonPlannerCalendar; when the user changes them, the panel writes new month and year values back into the URL and re-fetches Sundays. The seriesId URL parameter represents the currently focused series for this page. If present, the add-to-series dialog and membership indicator treat that series as the default series selection and use its id when deciding how to label cards as being part of the 'current series'. When a new series is created via createLessonSeries, the page updates the URL's seriesId to the new lesson_series.id so that subsequent interactions remain focused on that series, and the in-page series list is updated to include this new entry for use in dropdowns. The mapping of lessons to series created during the current session is held in in-memory state keyed by lesson_plan_id and lesson_series_id and is not persisted via URL; instead, the URL's seriesId is used purely to decide which parts of this map to expose in the UI. Scripture, complexity, and duration filter values are stored in the filters object passed to searchLessonPlans and mirrored to custom URL parameters (for example, scripture, complexity, durationMin, durationMax) so that deep-linking to a specific filtered view is possible. No localStorage or similar client-side persistence is used; refreshing the page reconstructs state exclusively from URL parameters combined with fresh calls to getLessonPlanFilterOptions, searchLessonPlans, getLessonPlannerCalendar, and any other necessary interfaces defined for this page.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages all lesson plan search and filter inputs, including age range, topic category, topic subcategory, scripture reference, complexity, duration constraints, and sorting. On initialization, it calls getLessonPlanFilterOptions to obtain age ranges, topic hierarchies, and sort options, then merges them with any matching URL parameters. When the user changes filter values or submits the form, it constructs the filters object, determines the sort_by, page, and page_size values, updates corresponding URL parameters, and calls searchLessonPlans. It provides the resolved filters and sort settings to downstream components such as the lesson-plan-list.

Displays the list of lesson plans returned from searchLessonPlans, including fields such as title, summary, age range, scripture_reference, estimated_duration_minutes, and download_count. It receives the lesson_plans array, total_count, page, and page_size from the latest searchLessonPlans response and renders one lesson-plan-card component per result. It also passes any membership and saved-resource state down to cards so they can render indicators and controls appropriately.

Represents a single LessonPlan item in the list. It shows key attributes from the LessonPlan model and exposes actions: a save toggle using saveResource and an 'Add to Series' trigger opening the add-to-series-dialog. It also subscribes to series membership state keyed by LessonPlan.id and the currently focused seriesId to show a status indicator (for example, that the lesson belongs to the current series and its recorded week_number) whenever a relevant SeriesLessonAssignment is present in page state.

Renders pagination controls based on total_count, page, and page_size returned by searchLessonPlans. When the user selects a different page or changes page size, it updates the page and pageSize URL parameters and triggers a new searchLessonPlans call with unchanged filters and sort_by but updated paging values. It ensures that the visible page of lesson plans is always consistent with the URL.

Manages the month/year planning context and the list of Sundays for that period. On initialization, it reads month and year from the URL and, when both are valid, calls getLessonPlannerCalendar to retrieve the Sundays array. It displays a control for selecting or changing the month and year; each change updates the URL month/year parameters and recalls getLessonPlannerCalendar. It provides the Sundays list and derived week indices to other components such as add-to-series-dialog and series-creation-form.

Allows the user to create a new LessonSeries associated with a particular age range and month. It pre-populates age range fields from current filter or URL values (minAge, maxAge) and uses the planner panel's month/year (or URL equivalents). The user supplies a series name and optional description and may confirm or adjust the number_of_weeks based on the current Sundays list. On submission, it calls createLessonSeries with name, description, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, and number_of_weeks. On success, it adds the resulting LessonSeries to an in-page list of known series, updates the URL's seriesId parameter to the new id, exposes an 'Open in Planner' button linking to series_detail.html?id={lessonSeries.id}, and informs the add-to-series-dialog of the new series option.

Handles assignment of a selected LessonPlan to a LessonSeries. It opens when a lesson-plan-card triggers the 'Add to Series' action, receiving the lessonPlanId. On open, it reads the current seriesId from the URL and any LessonSeries objects stored in page state (for example, those just created via series-creation-form) to populate a dropdown of selectable series. It also reads the Sundays list and week indices from the series-planner-panel so the user can pick a week number or corresponding date. On submission, it calls addLessonToSeries with lessonSeriesId (from the dropdown), lessonPlanId (from the initiating card), and initial_week_number (from the week selector). When the call succeeds, it records the returned SeriesLessonAssignment in shared state, shows a confirmation, and updates the associated lesson-plan-card membership indicator.

Displays whether a given LessonPlan is already part of the currently focused LessonSeries. It receives the current seriesId (from URL or selected series state) and the in-memory collection of SeriesLessonAssignment records. For the specific LessonPlan.id, it checks whether any assignment exists for the current series; if so, it renders a status label showing that the lesson has been added, optionally using the week_number from the SeriesLessonAssignment to give planning context. It updates automatically whenever new assignments are added.

Provides a control on each lesson-plan-card to save or unsave a lesson plan as a SavedResource. When activated for saving, it calls saveResource with resource_type set to indicate a lesson plan and resource_id equal to the LessonPlan.id. On a successful response, it updates the associated SavedResource state so subsequent renders indicate that the lesson is saved. If unsaving is supported by the broader application, this component can similarly trigger the appropriate operation, but on this page it only uses the saveResource interface.

Provides simple navigation actions from this page to other relevant pages. A link labeled for series planning navigates to series_overview.html when clicked. Another link, available in the context of saved resources, navigates to saved_resources.html. After a successful createLessonSeries action, an 'Open in Planner' navigation as part of the series-creation-form leads to series_detail.html?id={lessonSeries.id}. These links do not call APIs; they rely on standard URL navigation and the existing query parameters that have been set by the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header area with logo and centered site title, followed immediately by a horizontal row of navigation links. Beneath this, a full-width gradient band contains one dominant, centered white content card. Inside that card, content is split into two main columns: the left column is the more visual, planner-focused side (series planner panel over the series creation form), while the right column is the text-heavy browsing side (filter form at the top, lesson plan list in the middle, pagination at the bottom). Each lesson plan card includes a membership indicator and saved-resource toggle within it. The add-to-series dialog appears as a modal overlay centered above the rest of the content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header area with logo and centered site title, followed immediately by a horizontal row of navigation links. Beneath this, a full-width gradient band contains one dominant, centered white content card. Inside that card, content is split into two main columns: the left column is the more visual, planner-focused side (series planner panel over the series creation form), while the right column is the text-heavy browsing side (filter form at the top, lesson plan list in the middle, pagination at the bottom). Each lesson plan card includes a membership indicator and saved-resource toggle within it. The add-to-series dialog appears as a modal overlay centered above the rest of the content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and navigation occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below, the gradient band fills the remaining space with generous top and bottom padding. The central white content card spans about 80–85% of the fixed-width content area (roughly 960–1100px), leaving comfortably thick gradient margins on all sides. Inside the card, the left planner column takes about 40–45% of the card width and the right browsing column about 55–60%, echoing the mockup’s asymmetric two-column structure. Vertical spacing between major sections inside the card follows the 24–32px rhythm, with 16–24px between related elements such as filters and lists.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately dense, brochure-like feel. Text in the lesson plan list and series forms is relatively compact, with multiple lines of information per card, but generous padding within the content card and clear separation between columns prevent a cramped feeling. The planner panel uses larger visual elements (e.g., month selector, weekly slots) to balance the more text-heavy right column, mirroring the mockup’s balance between image and text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a classic Z-pattern. The eye starts at the logo in the upper left, moves horizontally through the centered title and navigation links, then diagonally down into the main content card, landing first on the left-side series planner panel as the primary focal element. From there, attention moves across to the right column for filters and the lesson plan list. Within the right column, the eye moves from the filter form at the top, down through the stacked lesson plan cards, and finally to the pagination controls. Modal interactions (add-to-series dialog) momentarily interrupt this flow by centering the user’s focus.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page presents as a traditional, centered nonprofit website: a full-width, softly colored header with the ministry logo aligned left and the site title and page name centered, sitting above a row of beveled navigation links stretching horizontally across the content width. Below, a warm gradient background band fills the rest of the viewport, and in its center sits a large white content card with rounded edges and subtle shadow, clearly framed by generous gradient margins. Inside this card, the content is divided into two columns. On the left, a visually prominent series planner shows the selected month’s Sundays and a structured area for building a lesson series, with the series creation form directly beneath it. On the right, aligned to the same top baseline, a compact filter bar allows users to choose age ranges, topics, and dates, followed by a vertical stack of lesson plan cards, each showing key details along with status indicators and save toggles, ending with a centered row of pagination controls at the bottom. The entire composition feels text-forward but orderly, with skeuomorphic touches in buttons and panels, and the user’s gaze naturally flows from header to planner to the list of lessons, then down through the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the lesson plan filter form is positioned at the top of the right column, aligned to the top edge of the card and horizontally aligned with the top of the series planner panel in the left column. It spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 90–95% of that column’s width), leaving small 16–24px inner margins on the left and right. The form fields (keyword, age range, topic, date/month selector) are arranged in one or two compact rows, using horizontally aligned fields with modest 8–16px gaps, echoing the mockup’s preference for dense but tidy forms. There is about 16–24px of vertical padding above and below the form, clearly separating it from the lesson plan list beneath. Visually, it reads as a simple, lightly outlined strip or slightly beveled box anchored at the top right of the main content card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter form scrolls with the rest of the page; it is not sticky. It stays fixed at the top of the right column inside the main card and defines the starting point of the text-heavy browsing area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the filter form, the lesson plan list occupies the remainder of the right column inside the main white card, extending from just under the filter form down toward the bottom of the card. It uses the full usable width of the right column, with consistent inner side margins of about 16–24px. Lesson plan cards are stacked vertically with 16px vertical gaps, producing a column of 4–8 visible cards depending on viewport height before scrolling. The list aligns its left edge cleanly with the left edge of the filter form and its right edge with the right column boundary, creating a tidy rectangular block of content. The bottom of the list stops about 24px above the pagination controls, giving them breathing room while still feeling visually connected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list scrolls within the overall page, extending beyond the viewport if there are many lesson plans. It remains anchored within the main content card’s right column and does not have its own internal scroll region; the page scrolls as a whole.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each lesson plan card is a compact, white or slightly off-white panel within the lesson plan list, echoing the main card’s style but at a smaller scale. It spans nearly the full width of the list area, with 16–24px padding on all sides inside the card. The top portion contains the lesson title aligned to the left, with the saved-resource toggle aligned to the top right, creating a header bar feel. Just beneath the title, on the left, subtle metadata lines (age range, scripture passage, duration, and tags) are arranged in one or two lines with a serif or clean sans-serif font, using tight but readable spacing. The series-membership-indicator sits near the top of the card, just below or to the right of the title, aligned to the left, using a small label or badge style that doesn’t overpower the title. Additional descriptive text or a short summary appears beneath, filling the card’s central area. The bottom of the card is unadorned or contains a small action row for buttons like “View details” and “Add to series,” aligned left or right, with 8–16px spacing from the lower edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lesson plan cards are static list items within the lesson plan list. They do not float or overlap and maintain a consistent height based on their content, creating a rhythmically repeating vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the right column inside the main content card, directly below the last lesson plan card. There is a 24px gap between the bottom of the list and the pagination row. The controls are centered horizontally within the right column, forming a short, wide strip with numbered page links and previous/next buttons styled as small beveled buttons consistent with the header navigation style. The pagination row spans about 60–70% of the right column width, leaving equal whitespace on the left and right, and sits about 24–32px above the bottom edge of the main content card, giving it a comfortable margin that visually closes the right column’s content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The pagination is fixed at the bottom of the content in the right column; as the user scrolls down past the list, it comes into view and then scrolls off the screen like any other element. It does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series planner panel sits in the left column of the main content card and is aligned flush with the top edge of the card, mirroring the vertical starting position of the filter form in the right column. It occupies roughly the top two-thirds of the left column, spanning almost the full width of that column with 16–24px inner padding all around. At the top of the panel, a title such as “Series Planner” is centered or left-aligned, followed by a month selector and navigational controls, presented in a slightly larger, more visual style (e.g., a faux-calendar or weekly grid) that recalls the image-dominant left side of the original mockup. Below this, the Sundays in the selected month are displayed as rows or a calendar grid, each row representing a Sunday slot where selected lesson plans will appear. The panel’s design uses soft borders, subtle gradients, or light drop shadows to make it feel like a distinct module within the card, with a clear outline against the white background. The bottom of the planner panel leaves a 24px gap before the series creation form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The series planner panel remains fixed in place within the left column and scrolls with the page. It does not float or stick, reinforcing the stable, brochure-like feel of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the series planner panel in the left column, the series creation form fills the remaining vertical space of that column within the main card. It is separated from the panel above by a 24px gap, and from the bottom of the card by about 24–32px, maintaining the card’s internal padding rhythm. The form spans almost the full width of the left column with 16–24px internal padding, and includes inputs for series title, age range, start and end dates, and perhaps a short description. Fields are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column, with labels above each field and 8–16px spacing between fields, reflecting the moderately dense, text-oriented style. A primary action button (e.g., “Save Series”) is placed at the bottom right of the form, with a secondary button (e.g., “Cancel” or “Clear”) to its left or beneath it, styled as beveled buttons consistent with the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The series creation form scrolls with the page as part of the left column. It does not expand into a modal; instead, it remains an always-visible section beneath the planner within the main card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-series dialog appears as a modal overlay that sits on top of the entire page, centered within the viewport both horizontally and vertically. The background behind it (including the header, gradient band, and main content card) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark overlay. The dialog itself is a medium-sized white panel with subtle shadow and beveled edges, about 40–50% of the page width and up to 40% of the viewport height, following the skeuomorphic, card-like style. Inside, there is a clear title at the top, aligned left, with a close icon in the top right. Below, the content area presents a dropdown or list of existing series, a date or Sunday selector tied to the planner, and action buttons aligned to the bottom right (e.g., “Add to Series” and “Cancel”). Padding inside the dialog follows the 24–32px spacing, ensuring the content feels spacious despite the dense overall aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This dialog is fixed relative to the viewport, remaining centered even if the underlying page is scrollable. The dimming overlay stretches full-screen and blocks interaction with all other components until the dialog is closed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series membership indicator appears within each lesson plan card, near the top where key information is clustered. It is a small, pill-shaped label or badge aligned just below the lesson title on the left side, or inline to the right of the title if space permits. The label uses a slightly tinted background color with rounded corners and a subtle inner shadow, matching the skeuomorphic aesthetic, and text such as “In Current Series,” “Scheduled,” or “Not in Series.” Its size is compact, occupying only a small horizontal strip (roughly 20–30% of the card’s width), leaving ample room for the title and metadata. When a lesson is not part of a series, this area may be empty or display a subdued “Available” label.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The indicator is a static inline element within each card; it does not float or overlay other content. Its presence slightly adjusts the vertical spacing inside the card but does not change the card’s overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved resource toggle is positioned in the top-right corner of each lesson plan card, opposite the lesson title. It is visually compact but noticeable, styled as a small icon-based control (such as a bookmark or star) with a subtle beveled button background when active. The toggle sits within the card’s padding area, about 8–12px from the top edge and 8–12px from the right edge, creating a tight but consistent margin. In its off state, it appears as a simple outlined icon; in its on state, it gains a filled color and slight inner glow or gradient to fit the skeuomorphic style. This placement creates a clear, quick interaction point without interrupting the card’s main text flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The toggle is a fixed-position element within the top-right of each lesson plan card and scrolls along with the list. It does not remain visible independently of its parent card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links form a horizontal group just below the main site header, spanning across the full content width of the page while remaining centered within the browser window. They appear as a row of large, beveled buttons with warm color fills and soft gradients, each representing a top-level section (e.g., Home, Lesson Plans, Series, Resources). The buttons are arranged side by side, each with 8–12px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding, and 16px gaps between them. The row is centered so that the middle button lines up approximately with the center of the page, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on centered navigation. There is about 16–24px of vertical spacing above the row (from the header title) and 24–32px below (down to the start of the gradient band and the main content card), reinforcing clear separation between navigation and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The navigation links are part of the normal document flow under the header. They do not stick to the top of the viewport on scroll, in keeping with the early-2010s, non-sticky navigation pattern suggested by the mockup.

name: Series Overview
title: Lesson Series Overview
description: Page for children’s ministry leaders to view, filter, and manage lesson series by month and age group, including creating new series and navigating to detailed lesson planning.

core_features: Load and display a list of lesson series using getLessonSeriesList, optionally filtered by month and year from the URL.
Allow users to change the focused month and year, updating the URL and reloading series via getLessonSeriesList.
Load calendar context (all Sundays for the selected month and year) using getLessonPlannerCalendar to support planning and suggested number_of_weeks.
Provide a form to create a new lesson series using createLessonSeries, prefilled with the currently selected month and year and optionally suggested number_of_weeks based on the calendar.
Enable duplication of an existing series’ metadata (name, ages, month, year, number_of_weeks) into a new series via createLessonSeries.
Provide a direct navigation action to open series_detail.html for any series to manage its lessons.
Provide a direct navigation action to open lesson_plans.html for any series with prefilled query parameters (age range, month, year, seriesId) for adding more lessons.
user_workflows:
View existing lesson series for a particular month and year:
1) User navigates to series_overview.html, optionally with month and/or year in the URL query string.
2) The page reads URL parameters month and year.
3) The planning context is established: getLessonPlannerCalendar is called with the effective month and year.
4) The series list is loaded: getLessonSeriesList is called with filters including the same month and year (if present).
5) The lesson-series-list component displays the retrieved series, each showing key fields such as name, age range, month, year, and number_of_weeks.
6) If the user changes the month or year via the filter controls, the URL is updated, getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList are called again with the new values, and the list refreshes.

Create a new lesson series for the selected month:
1) User selects the desired month and year using the month-year filter controls (or relies on the initial defaults).
2) User opens the series-create-form (e.g., via a “New Series” button).
3) The form initializes its month and year fields from the currently selected context and may suggest number_of_weeks based on the count of Sundays returned by getLessonPlannerCalendar.
4) The user fills in required fields (name, target_min_age, target_max_age) and any optional fields.
5) On form submission, the component calls createLessonSeries with the entered values.
6) On success, the component either:
   - Inserts the returned lesson_series object into the existing series list in memory, or
   - Triggers a refresh of the list via getLessonSeriesList with the current filters.
7) Optionally, the user can click through to manage the new series in series_detail.html.

Duplicate an existing series for a different age group or date range:
1) User locates a series in the lesson-series-list component.
2) User clicks the “Duplicate” action for that series.
3) The series-duplicate-dialog opens, prefilled with that series’s metadata (name, description if available, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, number_of_weeks).
4) The user adjusts fields such as month, year, age range, and name as needed.
5) On submission, series-duplicate-dialog calls createLessonSeries with the updated values.
6) On success, the new lesson_series is added to the list (or the list is refreshed via getLessonSeriesList) while the original remains unchanged.
7) The user can immediately click “Manage” on the new series to open series_detail.html and assign or adjust lessons there.

Add more lessons to a series from the lesson plan library:
1) User identifies a series in the lesson-series-list that needs additional lessons.
2) User clicks the “Add more lessons” action for that series.
3) The page constructs a URL to lesson_plans.html with query parameters: minAge = lessonSeries.target_min_age, maxAge = lessonSeries.target_max_age, month = lessonSeries.month, year = lessonSeries.year, seriesId = lessonSeries.id.
4) Browser navigates to the constructed URL, where the lesson library page can use the parameters to prefilter lesson plans and link any selections back to the originating series.

Open and manage a specific series in detail:
1) User clicks a series row or its “Manage” button in the lesson-series-list component.
2) The page navigates to series_detail.html with query parameter id = lessonSeries.id.
3) The user manages week-by-week lesson assignments on the destination page (outside the scope of this page’s interfaces).
interactions: On page load, read month and year from the URL query string; use them to call getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList.
When the user changes the month or year in the month-year filter controls, update the URL parameters accordingly, then re-call getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList with the new values.
