When the user opens the series-create-form, initialize its fields from the current filter context and calendar data (for example, using the number of Sundays as a suggested number_of_weeks).

On series-create-form submission, validate the required fields and call createLessonSeries; handle success by updating the series list and optionally closing the form or navigating to the new series detail page.
When the user clicks a series row or a dedicated “Manage” control, trigger navigation to series_detail.html?id={lessonSeries.id}.
When the user clicks the “Add more lessons” control for a series, trigger navigation to lesson_plans.html with minAge, maxAge, month, year, and seriesId derived from that series.

When the user clicks the “Duplicate” control for a series, open the series-duplicate-dialog prefilled with that series’s values so the user can adjust and submit; on submit, call createLessonSeries and then refresh or extend the series list.

If any API call (getLessonSeriesList, createLessonSeries, getLessonPlannerCalendar) fails, show a functional error message in the relevant component and provide a retry action without reloading the entire page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The main page state is controlled through URL query parameters and in-memory component state.

URL parameters:
- month: Optional numeric value representing the calendar month to focus on. When present, it determines which month’s series and calendar context are loaded.
- year: Optional numeric value representing the calendar year to focus on. When present, it is used together with month.

Initialization:
1) On initial load, the page parses the URL query string for month and year.
2) If both are present and valid, these values define the active planning context.
3) If either is missing or invalid, the page derives a default month and year from the client’s current date and updates the URL to include them. This ensures the planning context is always explicit in the URL.
4) With the effective month and year, the page calls:
   - getLessonPlannerCalendar({ month, year }) to retrieve the list of Sundays for that month.
   - getLessonSeriesList({ filters: { month, year } }) to retrieve the relevant LessonSeries records.

Ongoing state updates:
- When the user changes the month or year via the filter controls, the new values are immediately written to the URL query string (replacing any previous values). After the URL is updated, the page re-calls getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList using the updated month and year.
- The list of LessonSeries shown on the page is always consistent with the current URL month and year. If month/year are changed, the list is refreshed from the API, not cached from a previous context.

Creation and duplication:
- When a new series is created via createLessonSeries, the month and year values sent in that request default to the current planning context unless the user explicitly changes them in the form.
- After createLessonSeries returns successfully, the response’s lesson_series object is relevant only if its month and year match the current URL context. If they match, it can be merged into the existing series list; otherwise, the series list is refreshed by calling getLessonSeriesList again for the current context. This keeps the displayed list consistent with URL filters.

Navigation to other pages:
- For series detail navigation, the only query parameter passed is id (from LessonSeries.id). This does not alter the current page state but uses the current series selection.
- For lesson_plans navigation, the current series metadata determines the outgoing URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, month, year, seriesId). These parameters are not stored back to this page’s URL state but are constructed at the time of navigation based on the selected LessonSeries object.

components.functionality: Controls initial data loading and refreshes based on the month and year in the URL. On initialization, it parses the URL for month and year, derives defaults if needed, updates the URL, and then calls getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList with the effective month and year. It exposes the resulting Sundays and LessonSeries collection to other components and listens for month/year changes from the filter controls to re-run both interfaces.

Provides interactive controls to select the planning month and year. On mount, it reads the current month and year values (from the planning-context-loader or URL) and displays them. When the user selects a different month or year, it updates the URL query string and notifies the planning-context-loader of the change so that getLessonPlannerCalendar and getLessonSeriesList are re-executed with the new values.

Displays the planning calendar context for the selected month and year using the Sundays returned by getLessonPlannerCalendar. It can show the count of Sundays and a simple list or summary of the Sunday dates. This information is used to guide the user when setting or reviewing the number_of_weeks field for a lesson series, and is also read by the series-create-form to suggest a default number_of_weeks.

Renders the collection of LessonSeries returned by getLessonSeriesList for the current month and year. For each series, it displays key fields (such as name, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, and number_of_weeks) and exposes actions for managing the series, adding lessons, and duplicating the series. It also handles empty states when no series are returned for the selected filters.

Allows users to create a new lesson series. It presents input fields for name, description, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, and number_of_weeks. On open, it initializes the month and year fields from the current planning context and may suggest a number_of_weeks based on the length of getLessonPlannerCalendar.sundays. On submission, it calls createLessonSeries with the entered values. On success, it either appends the returned LessonSeries to the current list (if its month/year match the current filters) or triggers a full list refresh via getLessonSeriesList. It can optionally redirect users directly to series_detail.html for the new series.

Provides a focused flow to duplicate an existing lesson series. When opened from a particular series, it pre-populates fields (name, description, target_min_age, target_max_age, month, year, number_of_weeks) with that series’s values. The user can adjust these before submitting. On submission, the dialog calls createLessonSeries with the edited values. On success, it either inserts the new LessonSeries into the current list if appropriate for the active month/year, or triggers a full refresh through getLessonSeriesList, then closes the dialog.

Centralizes navigation logic for actions originating from the lesson-series-list. When a user chooses to manage a series, it constructs and triggers navigation to series_detail.html?id={LessonSeries.id}. When a user selects “Add more lessons” for a series, it constructs navigation to lesson_plans.html with query parameters minAge={LessonSeries.target_min_age}, maxAge={LessonSeries.target_max_age}, month={LessonSeries.month}, year={LessonSeries.year}, and seriesId={LessonSeries.id}. It ensures parameter values are taken directly from the selected LessonSeries object so that navigation is always consistent with current data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a classic, centered, fixed-width layout. A full-width header with logo and site title sits at the very top, followed by the global navigation row of beveled buttons. Beneath this, a full-width vertical band with a subtle warm gradient holds a single, prominent white content card centered on the page. Inside this card, content is arranged top-to-bottom: (1) a page title and slim planning context/status bar; (2) a horizontal control band with month/year filters on the left/center and series navigation actions on the right; (3) a two-column main area with a calendar-style summary panel on the left and the lesson series list on the right; (4) a full-width, centered series-create form at the bottom acting as the main call-to-action. The series-duplicate dialog appears as a modal overlay centered on top of everything when invoked.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a classic, centered, fixed-width layout. A full-width header with logo and site title sits at the very top, followed by the global navigation row of beveled buttons. Beneath this, a full-width vertical band with a subtle warm gradient holds a single, prominent white content card centered on the page. Inside this card, content is arranged top-to-bottom: (1) a page title and slim planning context/status bar; (2) a horizontal control band with month/year filters on the left/center and series navigation actions on the right; (3) a two-column main area with a calendar-style summary panel on the left and the lesson series list on the right; (4) a full-width, centered series-create form at the bottom acting as the main call-to-action. The series-duplicate dialog appears as a modal overlay centered on top of everything when invoked.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and global navigation together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The remaining vertical space is dominated by the gradient background band and the central white content card. That card is fixed-width (around 960–1040px), centered horizontally, and framed by generous margins of approximately 40–60px on all sides within the gradient band. Inside the card, internal padding is approximately 24–32px on all sides. The main two-column region allocates about 40–45% of the card width to the left column (calendar summary) and 55–60% to the right column (lesson series list). Vertical gaps between stacked sections inside the card follow an 8px base scale: small separations of 8–16px between closely related elements (status bar and filters) and larger separations of 24–32px between major sections (filters band, two-column area, and bottom create form). The series-duplicate dialog occupies roughly 50–60% of the card width, centered in the viewport with a dimmed scrim covering the rest of the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense, reflecting an early-2010s nonprofit brochure style. Text uses fairly small serif body type with relatively tight line length and several paragraphs per block. Controls are grouped closely enough to feel efficient and information-rich, but each group has clear padding and separation to avoid clutter: the status bar is slim but readable, filters and navigation actions are compact but well spaced, and the series list can show several items in one view without feeling cramped. The calendar summary is visually substantial, balancing the text-heavy right column. The create form at the bottom is more open and centered to signal a primary action while still maintaining the overall earnest, text-forward feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern. The eye starts at the logo on the top-left of the header, crosses to the centered site title and global navigation, then moves diagonally down to the left side of the main content card where the calendar summary panel anchors attention. From there, the gaze moves horizontally to the right into the lesson series list, where most interaction happens. The horizontal control band of filters and action buttons just above the columns acts as a wayfinding bridge between header and body content. Finally, the eye is gently drawn down to the centered create-series form at the bottom of the card, which acts as the concluding focal call-to-action. When the series-duplicate dialog appears, it interrupts this flow with a high-contrast centered modal, capturing the user's focus before they return to the main Z-pattern layout.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a traditional, centered ministry website with a warm, slightly skeuomorphic personality. A full-width header bar at the very top contains a church-friendly logo on the far left and a centered site title and tagline, set against a soft, subtle gradient. Directly beneath, a horizontal strip of large, beveled navigation buttons stretches across the center, each with ample padding and a gentle glow or bevel to match an early-2010s nonprofit aesthetic. Below this header zone, the background transitions into a broader gradient band that fills the rest of the viewport, within which a single white content card sits perfectly centered. The card has soft, rounded corners, a faint drop shadow, and generous breathing room around it, making it the clear focal container. Inside the card, a modest page heading and a slim contextual status bar sit at the very top, followed by a full-width control band where month/year filters and age-group controls are balanced with right-aligned navigation actions. Beneath this band, the card splits into two columns: on the left, a substantial calendar-style summary feels like the visual anchor; on the right, a taller, text-dense list of lesson series provides the main working area. At the very bottom of the card, spanning its full width, a centered create-series form with beveled submit buttons serves as the main call-to-action, mirroring the centered caption treatment from the reference mockup. When the user chooses to duplicate a series, the entire screen gently dims and a medium-sized, beveled dialog appears centered over the card, reinforcing the classic, earnest tone of the design while keeping interactions clear and focused.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the planning-context-loader appears near the top as a slim, full-width status strip immediately below the page title (which is part of the card’s static content, not this component). It spans nearly the entire card width (about 90–95% of the 960–1040px card, aligned with the card’s internal padding), with a height of roughly 32–40px. The strip uses a very light tinted background (for example, pale gold or cream) with a subtle inset bevel to match the skeuomorphic style, and it includes a small spinner or status icon on the left and contextual text (e.g., current month, year, and selected age group) aligned left or center. There is a modest 8–12px margin above it (from the page title) and a slightly larger 16px margin below it before the filter controls begin, making it feel clearly related but subordinate to the rest of the controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The planning-context-loader is statically positioned within the vertical flow of the content card. It does not stick or float during scrolling; as the user scrolls within the page, it moves naturally with the card’s content, appearing just above the filter and navigation control band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month-year-filter-controls sit directly beneath the planning-context-loader, forming the left and central portion of a full-width control band near the top of the content card. This band spans nearly the full inner width of the card (about 90–95% of the card width) and is visually treated as a shallow toolbar: a gently beveled, slightly darker strip than the card background, echoing the horizontal navigation row in the header but on a smaller scale. Within this band, the month and year dropdowns, plus age-group filters, are arranged from left to center in a horizontal row, with each control given generous padding (8–12px vertical, 16–20px horizontal) and 8–16px gaps between them. Collectively, the filter controls occupy roughly the left two-thirds of the band’s width, stopping short of the right edge to leave space for the series-navigation-actions. There is a 16px vertical gap above this band (following the loader) and around 24px of vertical space below it before the main two-column layout begins, giving the filters a clearly defined, but not overwhelming, presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter controls are fixed within the internal flow of the card and scroll with the rest of the content. They form part of a shared horizontal band with the series-navigation-actions but remain left/center anchored; they do not behave as a sticky toolbar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-summary-panel anchors the left column of the main content area inside the white card. It begins directly below the control band (filters and navigation actions), aligned with the card’s left inner padding. This left column occupies approximately 40–45% of the card’s inner width and runs vertically from just below the control band toward the lower half of the card, with its height flexible based on content but visually balanced against the right column. A vertical 24px gutter separates it from the right column. Inside the panel, there is a structured, skeuomorphic card treatment: a lightly framed box with a subtle gradient header displaying the selected month and year, and a calendar grid or summary list beneath. The panel includes generous internal padding (24–32px) so the calendar cells and legend do not feel cramped. At the bottom of this panel, there might be a brief textual summary or legend, reinforcing the text-centric nature of the design. Overall, it creates a large, visually solid block on the left that substitutes for the dominant photograph in the mockup, drawing the eye downward from the header into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The calendar-summary-panel is a static element within the left column of the content card. It does not scroll independently; instead, it moves in tandem with the rest of the card content as the user scrolls the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lesson-series-list occupies the full height of the right column of the main content area and serves as the primary working surface. Starting from the same top baseline as the calendar-summary-panel, it is aligned with the card’s right inner padding, with a 24px horizontal gap separating it from the left column. This right column takes up around 55–60% of the card’s inner width, making it visibly wider and more text-dense than the left. Within this space, the series list is presented as vertically stacked rows or mini-cards, each with a slightly beveled header bar (showing series name and key metadata) and a lightly shaded content area for schedule, age range, and quick actions. Row padding is moderate (12–16px), allowing several series to be visible at once without feeling cramped. There is a consistent 8–12px vertical gap between each row. The list extends downward, potentially beyond the fold; as the page scrolls, the entire card, including this list, moves. A 24–32px margin at the bottom of the list separates it from the series-create-form below, maintaining a clear structural hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The lesson-series-list scrolls as part of the overall page content; it does not have its own internal scroll bar. It remains within the right column of the card and expands vertically as needed, with the page itself handling overflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-create-form is placed at the bottom of the main content card as a full-width section that sits beneath both the left and right columns, mirroring the centered caption line beneath the image in the original mockup. It begins after a clear vertical separation: roughly 32–40px of space below the lowest point of both the calendar-summary-panel and the lesson-series-list, so it feels like a concluding, page-level call-to-action. The form spans nearly the entire card width but its inner form fields are constrained to a centered column occupying about 55–65% of the card’s width, giving it a more focused and approachable appearance. The outer area of this section has a subtle top border or change in background tone (a very soft tint) and a gentle inset bevel to set it apart from the content above. Within the centered form area, labels and fields are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing, and the primary submit button is centered horizontally, styled like the navigation buttons (beveled, gradient, warm-toned). The overall height of this section is moderate, but it feels more open and inviting than the dense series list above, drawing the eye as the final step of the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The series-create-form is statically positioned at the bottom of the content card. As the page is scrolled, it enters and leaves the viewport with the rest of the card content; it does not float or stick. When validation errors or expanded advanced options appear, the section simply grows downward, pushing the page content below (if any).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-duplicate-dialog appears as a classic modal overlay centered within the browser viewport, above the gradient background and white content card. When triggered, the rest of the page—including the header, navigation, and main card—dims with a semi-transparent dark overlay. The dialog itself is a medium-width white panel, approximately 480–560px wide (roughly 50–60% of the content card width) and proportionally tall enough to hold a small form (about 35–50% of the viewport height). It is horizontally and vertically centered, with a soft drop shadow and slight rounded corners, and it uses a beveled title bar in a warm accent color at the top to convey focus. Inside, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing and comfortable padding on all sides (24–32px). The primary and secondary buttons are right-aligned in a footer bar, styled similarly to other buttons on the site. This dialog cleanly interrupts the underlying layout, drawing full attention to the duplication task while preserving the conservative, skeuomorphic visual language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The series-duplicate-dialog is fixed-position relative to the viewport, remaining centered even if the user attempts to scroll the background. The background content becomes non-interactive while the dialog is open. Closing the dialog restores the view, returning the user to the same scroll position in the underlying content card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-navigation-actions share the same horizontal band as the month-year-filter-controls near the top of the content card, occupying the right side of this control stripe. The band itself spans nearly the full width of the card’s interior, just below the planning-context-loader. Within this band, the navigation actions—buttons such as "Back to Dashboard," "View Upcoming Lessons," or "Print Plan"—are grouped as a right-aligned cluster. This cluster typically occupies about the rightmost 25–35% of the band’s width, leaving the left and center portions to the filter controls. Each navigation button uses the design’s characteristic beveled, gradient style with 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding and 8–12px gaps between buttons. The entire band has a height of about 56–72px, enough to comfortably hold both filters and actions without feeling cramped. Visually, this arrangement mirrors the header’s horizontal navigation, reinforcing the early-2010s nonprofit aesthetic and providing a clear bridge between filtering and navigating among series-related views.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The series-navigation-actions are statically positioned in the control band and scroll together with the rest of the card content. They do not remain fixed at the top of the viewport; instead, they are visible only while the top region of the card is in view, encouraging users to set their context before scrolling down into details.

name: Series Detail
title: Lesson Series Detail
description: Planner page for arranging lessons within a specific lesson series week by week, viewing the month’s Sundays, and saving the order back to the series.

core_features: Load a specific lesson series based on the URL parameter and display its summary, including month, year, and age range.
Retrieve and display all lesson assignments for the series, grouped by their week_number, with associated lesson plan details.
Provide a drag-and-drop interface to reorder lessons within a week and move lessons between weeks.
Allow the user to adjust the relative order (display_order) of lessons inside each week after drag-and-drop.
Expose a primary "Save Series Order" action that calls updateSeriesLessonOrder to persist the current week_number and display_order for every assignment.
Retrieve and show calendar context for the series month (all Sundays) using getLessonPlannerCalendar, visually aligning Sundays to week numbers.
Optionally display per-week notes fields for the planner that are editable in the current session (ephemeral, not persisted via API).
Provide navigation back to the overall series list and a deep link to the lesson plans page to add additional lessons to this series with pre-filled filters.
user_workflows:
Load a series and view its plan:
1. User navigates to series_detail.html with a URL parameter id representing the lesson series ID.
2. The page reads the id parameter and triggers getLessonSeriesDetail(lessonSeriesId = id).
3. Once lesson_series and assignments are loaded, the page reads lesson_series.month and lesson_series.year.
4. The page calls getLessonPlannerCalendar(month, year) to obtain the Sundays for that month.
5. The planner groups assignments by assignment.week_number and displays them in week columns, with lesson titles and key metadata visible.
6. The calendar panel shows the Sundays for the month and visually associates them with week numbers (for example, the first Sunday aligns with Week 1).

Reorder lessons across weeks and save:
1. Within the planner grid, the user clicks and drags a lesson card from its current week column.
2. The user drops the lesson card into a new position, either in the same week column (changing display_order) or in a different week column (changing week_number and display_order).
3. The planner updates its in-memory assignments state to reflect the new week_number and recalculated display_order for all affected lessons.
4. A "Save Series Order" button becomes enabled or indicates that there are unsaved changes.
5. The user clicks "Save Series Order".
6. The page constructs an assignments_order payload derived from the current assignments state (referencing each SeriesLessonAssignment.id and its new week_number and display_order) and calls updateSeriesLessonOrder(lessonSeriesId, assignments_order).
7. On success, the page updates its assignments from updated_assignments returned by the API, clears the dirty/unsaved state, and displays a success message.
8. If the API returns an error, the page shows an error message and leaves the current in-memory order so the user can retry or adjust.

Use calendar context to review scheduling:
1. After data load, the calendar panel shows the Sundays array from getLessonPlannerCalendar.
2. The page maps each week_number to a Sunday from the sundays array (for example, the first Sunday corresponds to Week 1, the second Sunday to Week 2, etc.), up to the number of weeks needed for the series.
3. For each week column in the planner grid, the corresponding Sunday date is displayed in the week header for easy reference.
4. Optionally, the user clicks a Sunday in the calendar panel; the planner scrolls or focuses the associated week column, helping the user verify lessons assigned to that date.

Add more lessons to the series:
1. In the series header or planner toolbar, the user clicks an "Add more lessons" link or button.
2. The page reads lesson_series.target_min_age, lesson_series.target_max_age, lesson_series.month, lesson_series.year, and lesson_series.id.
3. The page navigates to lesson_plans.html, appending URL parameters minAge, maxAge, month, year, and seriesId based on the current series.
4. The user selects and assigns additional lessons on lesson_plans.html (handled on that page).
5. When the user returns to series_detail.html?id=<same_series_id>, the planner reloads via getLessonSeriesDetail and displays any newly assigned lessons.

Navigate back to all series:
1. The user clicks a "Back to all series" breadcrumb or link.
2. The page navigates to series_overview.html without additional parameters.
3. Any unsaved changes to ordering remain only in the current session and are lost if the user leaves without saving.

Use per-week planning notes (ephemeral):
1. In each week column, the user sees a notes field for that week.
2. The user types notes (for example, service details or volunteer reminders) into the notes field.
3. The notes are stored only in the client-side component state for the current page session and are not persisted via any backend API.
4. If the user reloads the page or navigates away, the notes reset because there is no storage interface connected to them.
interactions: Reading the id URL parameter triggers loading of series and its assignments via getLessonSeriesDetail.
After series data is loaded, the month and year values from LessonSeries trigger a call to getLessonPlannerCalendar to load Sunday dates.
Dragging a lesson card within a week column changes its display_order locally and marks the planner state as having unsaved changes.
Dragging a lesson card to another week column updates its week_number and recalculates display_order in the new week.
Clicking the "Save Series Order" button triggers a validation step (ensuring series data is loaded) and then calls updateSeriesLessonOrder with the current assignments ordering.
Receiving a successful response from updateSeriesLessonOrder updates the local assignments list with updated_assignments, resets the unsaved-changes indicator, and displays a success state.
Receiving an error from updateSeriesLessonOrder displays an error message and retains the current unsaved ordering so the user can adjust or retry.
Clicking a Sunday in the calendar panel (optional) causes the corresponding week column in the planner grid to be highlighted or scrolled into view.
Editing per-week notes updates in-memory notes linked to that week_number without calling any API.
Clicking "Add more lessons" navigates to lesson_plans.html with minAge, maxAge, month, year, and seriesId set from the loaded LessonSeries.
Clicking "Back to all series" navigates to series_overview.html.

Attempting to navigate away while unsaved changes exist can trigger a confirmation dialog implemented in client routing logic (no localStorage), allowing the user to decide whether to leave without saving.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses, not by localStorage. On initial load, the page reads the id parameter from the URL (e.g., series_detail.html?id=<lessonSeriesId>). If id is missing or empty, the page shows an error state and does not call any interfaces. When a valid id is present:
1) The page calls getLessonSeriesDetail(lessonSeriesId = id) and stores lesson_series and assignments in memory as the base state. The calendar_sundays property from this response may be ignored or used only as a fallback; the canonical calendar data is fetched via getLessonPlannerCalendar.
2) Once lesson_series is loaded, the page reads lesson_series.month and lesson_series.year and calls getLessonPlannerCalendar(month, year). The returned sundays array is stored in memory and used to render the calendar context and to label week columns with corresponding Sunday dates.
3) The number of weeks shown in the planner is determined from lesson_series.number_of_weeks when present; if absent, it is derived from the maximum week_number in assignments and the number of Sundays returned by getLessonPlannerCalendar.
4) The in-memory representation of SeriesLessonAssignment objects is considered the 'current working state' for the planner. Drag-and-drop interactions mutate this in-memory state (week_number and display_order fields for each assignment). A derived boolean flag (e.g., hasUnsavedChanges) is maintained in memory; when true, the 'Save Series Order' button is enabled and an unsaved indicator may be shown.
5) When the user clicks 'Save Series Order', the page constructs assignments_order from the current in-memory assignments (e.g., an array of objects containing each SeriesLessonAssignment.id with its updated week_number and display_order) and calls updateSeriesLessonOrder(lessonSeriesId = lesson_series.id, assignments_order = constructedPayload). On success, the page replaces its in-memory assignments with updated_assignments from the response and resets hasUnsavedChanges to false.
6) Navigation from this page to other pages is represented solely by URL changes: series_overview.html has no additional parameters, while lesson_plans.html receives minAge, maxAge, month, year, and seriesId in its query string. When the user later returns to series_detail.html with the same id, the planner reloads fresh from the APIs, so the only persisted state is what was saved via updateSeriesLessonOrder.

components.functionality: Reads the lesson series ID from the URL, calls getLessonSeriesDetail to load series details and assignments, then calls getLessonPlannerCalendar with the series month and year to load Sunday dates. Manages loading and error states and exposes data to child components.
Displays a simple breadcrumb or header navigation for the series detail page, including a link back to all series. Optionally displays the current series name for context.

Shows key information about the current lesson series (name, description if present, target age range, month and year) and provides an 'Add more lessons' action that navigates to lesson_plans.html with appropriate URL parameters pre-filled from the series.

Displays the Sundays for the series month using the sundays array from getLessonPlannerCalendar. Maps Sundays to week numbers and shows which Sunday corresponds to each week in the planner. Optionally allows the user to click a Sunday to focus the related week in the planner grid.

Renders week columns for the series and lists all lesson assignments grouped by week_number. Supports drag-and-drop of lesson cards to reorder within a week or move across weeks. Updates in-memory SeriesLessonAssignment fields (week_number and display_order) and notifies the save bar about unsaved changes.

Represents a single lesson within a week column as a draggable card. Displays key LessonPlan details (such as title, topic, age suitability) and any relevant assignment metadata. Participates in the drag-and-drop lifecycle to allow reordering.
Provides an optional notes field for each week column, allowing the user to type planning notes for that week. Notes are stored only in the page’s in-memory state and are not persisted via any API.

Shows the 'Save Series Order' button and any status messages related to saving. Listens for changes to the assignments ordering, enables/disables the save button based on whether there are unsaved changes, and calls updateSeriesLessonOrder when the user chooses to save.

Monitors the hasUnsavedChanges flag from the planner grid. If the user attempts to navigate away (via browser controls or internal navigation) while unsaved changes exist, it can trigger a confirmation dialog so the user can choose to stay and save or leave without saving.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure inside a centered, fixed‑width content area (~960–1100px). Immediately below the global site header, a full‑width gradient band begins. Within this band, the breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the fixed‑width column, followed by a single large, white planner ‘card’ that contains the series header summary at the top, then a two‑column layout (calendar on the left, weekly planner grid on the right), and finally a full‑width notes area beneath both columns. A save/action bar anchors the bottom of the viewport, visually tied into the gradient background. Loader and unsaved‑changes guard appear as overlays above this structure when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure inside a centered, fixed‑width content area (~960–1100px). Immediately below the global site header, a full‑width gradient band begins. Within this band, the breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the fixed‑width column, followed by a single large, white planner ‘card’ that contains the series header summary at the top, then a two‑column layout (calendar on the left, weekly planner grid on the right), and finally a full‑width notes area beneath both columns. A save/action bar anchors the bottom of the viewport, visually tied into the gradient background. Loader and unsaved‑changes guard appear as overlays above this structure when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The gradient band covers the rest of the viewport beneath the header and extends vertically beyond the fold as needed, with the main planner card centered and surrounded by generous margins (about 40–60px on top and bottom, and left and right within the gradient). Inside the white card, 24–32px padding on all sides is used. The header summary/toolbar uses roughly the top 20–25% of the card's height above the fold, the two‑column planner area (calendar + weekly grid) takes the main central 50–60%, and the notes/editor area occupies the bottom 15–25% of the card. Horizontally within the two‑column area, the left calendar panel uses about 40–45% of the card width, while the right weekly planner grid uses 55–60%, reflecting the mockup’s image‑left/text‑right weighting.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains moderately dense: smaller, text‑friendly fonts, multi‑paragraph descriptions and notes within a single card, and a planner grid that shows multiple lesson cards at once. Generous padding around the interior elements (24–32px inside the card, 16–24px between columns and sections) prevents the interface from feeling cramped. The skeuomorphic style is reflected through bevelled buttons in the toolbar and save bar, slightly inset panels for the calendar and notes, and subtle gradients on section headers instead of large, empty whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page uses a classic Z‑pattern: the eye starts at the breadcrumb and series title in the top‑left of the main card, moves horizontally across the header summary and action tools, then diagonally down to the left‑side calendar panel, and from there across to the right‑side weekly planner grid for detailed arrangement. After scanning the grid, the eye drops down to the full‑width notes editor, then finally to the bottom‑right area of the sticky save bar where the primary save button and unsaved status reside. The gradient background can subtly lighten towards the left behind the calendar and darken slightly towards the right to reinforce the left‑to‑right planning flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full‑width header, a warm, vertical gradient band fills the rest of the page, with a centered, fixed‑width content column (around 1000px wide) sitting squarely in the middle. At the very top of this column, small, text‑based breadcrumb links form a slim strip, aligned to the left and separated from the main planner area by about 16–24px of vertical space. Just below, a large white planner card with soft corners and a faint drop shadow becomes the focal point, floating on the gradient background with 40–60px of space on all sides. Inside this card, a horizontally oriented header section at the top combines the series title, series metadata, and a compact toolbar of beveled buttons, spanning almost the full width while still keeping comfortable side padding. Under that header, the card splits into two clear columns: on the left, a framed month calendar panel occupies about 40–45% of the card width and provides a visual, date‑based context for the series; on the right, a taller, denser weekly lesson planner grid fills the remaining 55–60% and lists Sundays and their assigned lesson cards. The two panels sit side‑by‑side with about 16–24px between them, and both are inset slightly from the card edges so they feel like coordinated sub‑panels. At the bottom of the card, spanning its full width, a week notes editor forms a single, low‑height but wide area for typed notes or reminders, with a subtle inset effect to differentiate it from the planner above. Outside the card, anchored along the bottom edge of the viewport, a full‑width or central fixed‑width save bar with a subtle top shadow remains visible as the user scrolls, echoing the warm gradient and containing the primary “Save Series Order” button on the right. When loading or when guarding against lost changes, semi‑transparent overlays dim the card and draw focus to a centered, modal‑style panel. The overall impression is that of a structured, brochure‑like planner: traditional, text‑centric, and reassuringly organized, with the calendar and planner grid mirroring the photo‑and‑text pairing from the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a full‑page overlay above the gradient background and planner card whenever the series data is being fetched. It covers the entire viewport from just beneath the global header down to the bottom, matching the fixed‑width content zone horizontally but dimming the surrounding gradient as well. Visually, it is a semi‑transparent dark veil (around 40–60% opacity) that allows the underlying card shapes to remain faintly visible. At the center of the viewport, aligned both horizontally and vertically, a small white panel or circular loader sits, sized roughly 260–320px wide if using a panel, with 24px padding and a bevelled border to match the skeuomorphic style. This central loading indicator becomes the temporary focal point, clearly communicating a paused state without shifting the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay that covers the main content area when active; positioned relative to the viewport (not scrolled away) so it always remains centered on screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is positioned at the very top of the page’s main content column, inside the gradient band but outside the white planner card. It is left‑aligned within the fixed‑width container and spans only as wide as its text content, typically 30–50% of the card width. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the bottom of the global header and about 16–24px above the top edge of the main white planner card, serving as a thin, textual lead‑in. The breadcrumb text is small and understated, with muted link colors that harmonize with the warm palette, ensuring it doesn’t compete visually with the more dominant card below. This arrangement mirrors a subtle, text‑only row above the main content that users glance at first but quickly move past to the central card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the page flow, moving upward and out of view as the user scrolls down, always bound to the top of the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series header summary toolbar sits at the very top inside the main white planner card, acting as the card’s header band. It spans nearly the full internal width of the card, with 24–32px of white space padding on the left and right from the card edges. Vertically, this header area is about 72–120px tall, depending on content, and occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the card’s above‑the‑fold height. On the left side of this band (about the left 60–65% of the card’s width), the series title, subheading (such as current series or date range), and any key metadata are stacked in one or two lines, aligned left to reinforce the starting point of the visual Z‑pattern. On the right side (about 35–40% of the width), a small cluster of beveled buttons and dropdowns forms the toolbar: these might include controls for week selection, filtering, or quick actions. The buttons are horizontally aligned, medium‑sized (about 8–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding), with subtle gradients and slight inner shadows to echo the early‑2010s aesthetic. A faint bottom border or soft gradient separates this header section from the two‑column planner area below, clearly signposting the series context before the user dives into planning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the top of the card; it scrolls with the card content and does not remain sticky, preserving the brochure‑like, sectioned feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar context panel lives inside the main white planner card, directly beneath the series header summary, forming the left half of the two‑column planner region. It begins about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the header summary band and is inset by the card’s internal padding (24–32px from the left side of the card). Horizontally, it occupies roughly 40–45% of the card’s internal width, mirroring the ‘photo’ column from the original mockup. Its content area is framed like a subtle sub‑card: a light border, a slightly darker white or cream background, and a gentle inner shadow, making it feel like a physical calendar sheet pinned within the larger planner. The calendar itself displays the month’s Sundays prominently, with the month title at the top and the grid of dates below. The panel’s height is sized to visually balance with the adjacent weekly planner grid—tall enough to show the full month calendar without feeling cramped, typically extending through the middle portion of the card. A small label or subtitle (e.g., “This Month’s Sundays”) can sit at the top left of the panel, providing a clear anchor point for the eye when it lands on this left column in the Z‑pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static left column element within the card; it remains side‑by‑side with the weekly planner grid on wider screens. On very narrow screens, it would naturally stack above the planner grid, still maintaining generous padding and its inset look.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The weekly lesson planner grid occupies the right side of the two‑column region inside the main planner card, directly to the right of the calendar context panel. There is a gap of about 16–24px between the right edge of the calendar panel and the left edge of the planner grid, keeping the columns visually distinct while still related. Horizontally, the grid uses the remaining 55–60% of the card’s width, beginning after the card’s left internal padding and the inter‑column gap, and ending about 24–32px before the card’s right edge. Vertically, it aligns with the calendar panel’s top edge (about 16–24px below the header summary) and typically extends slightly further down than the calendar panel due to the number of weeks and lesson slots, giving it a subtly dominant presence on the right. The grid itself is structured as rows for each Sunday or session, with columns for different service times or age groups if needed. Each row uses modest vertical spacing (8–12px between rows) to remain information‑dense but legible, and each grid cell has a slight inset or subtle line to define dropzones for draggable lesson cards. At the top of this panel, a right‑aligned label or small header (e.g., “Weekly Lesson Plan”) helps anchor the right side of the Z‑flow, similar to a text block heading in the original mockup’s right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right column element within the card; in a responsive context it would stack beneath the calendar panel, but still maintain its denser, grid‑like appearance and inset dropzone styling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each lesson-card-draggable is a compact, skeuomorphic card that sits within the cells of the weekly lesson planner grid. Visually, each card is a small rounded rectangle, spanning most of the width of its grid cell (leaving 4–8px of padding on either side) and using about 32–48px of height for a single‑line title, or 48–64px if including subtitle or tags. Inside the card, main text is left‑aligned, with a bolded sermon or lesson title on the first line and optional secondary text beneath it in a smaller font. The card surface features a soft gradient (slightly lighter at the top, darker at the bottom), a fine border, and a subtle drop shadow to suggest it can be ‘picked up’ and moved. When resting in a grid cell, cards are arranged in a vertical stack with 4–8px spacing between them, allowing multiple lessons to sit within one Sunday. During drag operations, the active card lifts slightly visually: its shadow becomes a bit stronger, and it may scale up by a couple of pixels, floating above nearby content. Placeholder areas in the grid appear as dashed or softly highlighted rectangles following the same inset sizing, indicating where the card can be dropped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Draggable elements that normally conform to the grid’s cell boundaries but temporarily float above all grid content while being dragged, following the cursor or touch point. They are otherwise static within their assigned cells.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The week notes editor is positioned inside the main white planner card below the two‑column region. There is a clear break of about 24–32px between the bottom of the calendar/planner grid area and the top of the notes editor, helping it read as a distinct but related section. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the card, respecting the same 24–32px internal side padding. The notes editor itself is styled like a recessed writing area: a large, rectangular field with a very light, slightly darker background than the card, a faint inner shadow, and a simple border, evoking a notepad or text area inset into the card. Its height is moderate—around 160–240px by default—large enough to accommodate multi‑paragraph notes without overwhelming the main planner, and it can scroll internally if content exceeds this height. A small header or label, such as “Week Notes” or “Teacher Reminders,” is left‑aligned directly above the text area, while optional helper text can sit right‑aligned in the same horizontal row. Vertically, this notes section occupies the bottom 15–25% of the card, echoing the centered caption under the photo in the original mockup by serving as a grounded, summarizing area beneath the more visual and structured content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored to the bottom of the main card’s content area; it scrolls with the card and does not detach or become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save series order bar appears as a horizontal strip at the very bottom of the viewport, visually separate from but related to the main planner card. It either spans the full browser width or, to mirror the fixed‑width design language, contains a centered inner section aligned to the same 960–1100px width as the card. The bar is about 56–72px tall, with a warm gradient background slightly darker than the main page gradient, and a subtle top shadow to make it feel like it is resting above the content. Inside this bar, content is horizontally arranged: on the left or center, small status text (e.g., “Unsaved changes to lesson order”) appears; on the right, the primary “Save Series Order” button is prominent and bevelled, with 10–12px vertical and 24–28px horizontal padding, likely the most visually striking button on the page. There is comfortable horizontal padding (24–32px) inside the bar, and the overall look is reminiscent of a persistent, skeuomorphic control panel that is always available without overshadowing the main card. When there are no unsaved changes, the bar may collapse or subtly change styling to a more neutral state while maintaining its position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom edge of the viewport, remaining visible as the user scrolls through the planner card. Its inner content stays horizontally aligned with the main fixed‑width layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The unsaved changes guard manifests as a modal overlay that appears atop all other page content when the user attempts to navigate away with unsaved changes. It uses the same full‑viewport semi‑transparent dimming layer as the data loader, slightly darkening the gradient background and the planner card beneath. At the center of the viewport, a rectangular dialog box is displayed, slightly larger than the loader panel—approximately 360–480px wide and 220–280px tall—with a white or very light cream background, rounded corners, and a subtle drop shadow and bevel to match the site’s skeuomorphic styling. Inside the dialog, the warning message text is center‑weighted (either centrally aligned or in a balanced left‑aligned block) at the top, with 24px of padding around. Below the text, two beveled buttons (e.g., “Discard Changes” and “Cancel” or “Stay on Page”) are horizontally aligned, centered or right‑aligned with 16–24px spacing between them. The modal is vertically and horizontally centered regardless of scroll position, ensuring it becomes the clear focal point while the rest of the layout remains faintly visible behind it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed modal overlay attached to the viewport; it disables interaction with the underlying planner card and save bar until the user explicitly responds, then disappears, restoring the normal page flow.

name: Shop / Product Listing
title: Shop
description: The Shop page lists ministry resources as products and allows users to search, filter, and sort items by category, age range, price, and other attributes, using product APIs. Users can open product detail pages or navigate to the cart from here.

core_features: Fetch and display all available product categories in a sidebar, using getProductCategories, and allow selecting a category for the current product listing.

Retrieve available filter and sort options for the selected category (age ranges, price range, sort options) using getProductFilterOptions and use them to render dynamic filters (no hardcoded filter values).
Execute product search queries using searchProducts with parameters derived from URL state (category, keyword, age range, max price, sort, pagination) and display results in a product grid.
Maintain the current search, filter, sort, and pagination state in URL parameters and reapply that state when the page loads or when the user navigates using the browser's back/forward controls.
Allow users to change category, filters, and sort options and immediately refresh the product listing via searchProducts with updated parameters.
Allow users to click a product card to navigate to product_detail.html with the product ID in the URL parameters.
Provide navigation to the cart page (cart.html) via a cart icon or link without manipulating cart contents on this page.
user_workflows:
Browse by category from navigation or sidebar:
  1. User arrives on shop.html either without URL parameters or with a pre-selected categoryId.
  2. The page controller reads categoryId (if present) from the URL.
  3. The page calls getProductCategories to load all categories.
  4. The category sidebar highlights the category that matches categoryId; if none is present, the application’s default selection logic determines the active category.
  5. The page calls getProductFilterOptions with the active categoryId to retrieve dynamic age ranges, price range, and sort options.
  6. The page builds a filters object from URL parameters (if present) and default filter logic and calls searchProducts with query, filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
  7. The product grid displays the returned products and pagination controls use total_count, page, and page_size from searchProducts.
  8. When the user clicks a different category, the categoryId in the URL is updated, page is reset to the first page, getProductFilterOptions is called again for the new category, and then searchProducts is re-run and the grid is refreshed.

Use keyword search from the Shop page:
  1. User types a search term into the search input and submits the search form.
  2. The search form updates the query parameter in the URL and resets pagination to the first page.
  3. The page controller constructs a filters object from current filter state (categoryId, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, and any other filter attributes available from getProductFilterOptions).
  4. The page calls searchProducts with the new query and existing filters and sort_by values.
  5. The product grid updates to show products matching the new query; the result summary reflects total_count from searchProducts.
  6. If the user clears the search input and submits, the query parameter is removed from the URL or set to an empty value, and searchProducts is called again to show non-keyword-filtered results.

Refine results with filters and sorting:
  1. The filter sidebar displays dynamic age range and price range options using data from getProductFilterOptions.
  2. When the user selects or changes age-related filter controls, the minAge and/or maxAge URL parameters are updated; page is reset to the first page.
  3. When the user adjusts the maximum price, the priceMax URL parameter is updated; page is reset to the first page.
  4. When the user selects a sort option, the sort URL parameter is updated.
  5. After each filter or sort change, the page controller rebuilds the filters object from URL parameters and calls searchProducts with the updated filters and sort_by values.
  6. The product grid refreshes to show filtered and/or re-sorted results, and a result summary component reflects the active filter state and total_count.

Paginate through results:
  1. After searchProducts returns results, pagination controls are displayed based on total_count, page, and page_size.
  2. When the user selects a different page (e.g., by clicking a page number or next/previous), the page URL’s page parameter is updated to the selected page.
  3. The page controller calls searchProducts with the same query, filters, sort_by, and the updated page.
  4. The product grid updates to show products for the selected page.
  5. If the user uses the browser’s back/forward navigation, the page controller re-reads page and other parameters from the URL and calls searchProducts accordingly, so the grid state remains consistent with navigation history.

Open a product detail page:
  1. The product grid displays each product as a clickable card using data from searchProducts.products.
  2. When the user clicks a product card, the browser navigates to product_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the clicked product’s id.
  3. The new page (product_detail.html) uses the id parameter to load and display full product details (outside the scope of this page).

Navigate to the cart:
  1. A cart link or icon in the page header is available for navigation.
  2. When the user clicks the cart icon or any explicit "View Cart" link, the browser navigates to cart.html.
  3. This page does not modify cart contents; it only provides navigation to the cart page.
interactions: Category click: When a category item in the sidebar is clicked, the categoryId URL parameter is set to that category’s id, the page parameter is reset to the first page, getProductFilterOptions is called with the new categoryId, and then searchProducts is called to refresh the product grid.

Search submit: When the search form is submitted, the query URL parameter is updated, pagination is reset to the first page, and searchProducts is called with the updated query and existing filters and sort settings.

Filter change (age): When age-related inputs (based on getProductFilterOptions.age_ranges) are changed, the corresponding minAge and/or maxAge URL parameters are updated, pagination is reset to the first page, and searchProducts is called with the updated filters.

Filter change (price): When the user adjusts price-related filters (e.g., a maximum price based on getProductFilterOptions.price_range), the priceMax URL parameter is updated, pagination is reset to the first page, and searchProducts is called with the updated filters.

Sort change: When the user selects a sort option from the sort dropdown (using getProductFilterOptions.sort_options), the sort URL parameter is updated, pagination is reset to the first page, and searchProducts is called with the updated sort_by value.

Pagination interaction: When the user selects a different page number or uses next/previous controls, the page URL parameter is updated and searchProducts is called with the updated page while keeping query, filters, and sort_by unchanged.
Product card click: When the user clicks anywhere on a product card that is defined as interactive, the browser navigates to product_detail.html?id=<product.id>.
Cart navigation: When the user clicks the cart icon or "View Cart" link, the browser navigates to cart.html without changing any product or cart data.

URL-driven reload: When the page loads or when the URL changes via browser navigation, the page controller reads categoryId, query, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, sort, and page (and optionally pageSize if implemented in the router) from the URL and reinitializes the filters and calls searchProducts to keep the product grid and filters in sync with URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Shop page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for search, filter, sort, and pagination state.

On initial load:
- The page reads the following URL parameters: categoryId, query, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, sort, page, and pageSize (if the application defines pageSize as a URL parameter).
- The active category is derived from categoryId if present; otherwise, application-level logic determines an initial category.
- The page calls getProductCategories to fetch all categories; once loaded, the sidebar highlights the category matching categoryId.
- The page calls getProductFilterOptions with the active categoryId to retrieve age_ranges, price_range, and sort_options. The filter controls are generated from these values, and the initial UI state is aligned to the minAge, maxAge, priceMax, and sort parameters when they are present.
- The page constructs a filters object that includes categoryId and any filter-relevant URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, priceMax, and any additional attributes supported by getProductFilterOptions that have corresponding URL parameters defined by the application).
- The page then calls searchProducts with:
  - query set from the query URL parameter (or empty/undefined if not provided),
  - filters constructed from URL parameters,
  - sort_by derived from the sort URL parameter (or left undefined if not provided),
  - page derived from the page URL parameter (or treated as the first page if not provided by the router),
  - page_size derived from the pageSize URL parameter if defined by the application router or from application configuration.

On interaction:
- Any user interaction that changes category, query, filters, sort, or pagination updates the corresponding URL parameters using the router or history API. After updating the URL, the page controller recomputes the filters object from the latest URL parameters and calls searchProducts again.
- For interactions that should reset pagination (category change, query change, filter change, sort change), the page parameter is set to represent the first page before calling searchProducts.
- The result of searchProducts (products, total_count, page, page_size) drives the product grid and pagination controls. UI components do not maintain separate, conflicting state; they observe the current URL and API responses.
- When the user uses browser back/forward controls, the URL parameters change. The page listens for URL changes, re-reads categoryId, query, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, sort, and page, then calls getProductFilterOptions (if categoryId changed) and searchProducts to restore the previous view.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent updates for categories, filters, search results, and pagination based on URL parameters. Ensures that getProductCategories, getProductFilterOptions, and searchProducts are called in the correct order and that UI components receive the necessary data.

Displays a list of product categories retrieved from getProductCategories. Highlights the category that matches the current categoryId URL parameter and allows the user to select a category, which updates URL state and refreshes filters and product results.

Provides a keyword search input for products. Initializes the input field with the query URL parameter if present. On submission, updates the query URL parameter, resets pagination, and triggers a new call to searchProducts via the page controller.

Renders dynamic filter controls for age ranges, price range, and other supported filter attributes using the output of getProductFilterOptions. Synchronizes UI with URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, priceMax, and any other configured filter parameters). When filters change, updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets pagination, and triggers searchProducts via the page controller.

Displays sort options based on getProductFilterOptions.sort_options. Aligns its current selection with the sort URL parameter. When the user selects a new sort option, updates the sort URL parameter, resets pagination, and triggers searchProducts via the page controller.

Shows a summary of the current result set (such as total_count and current page) and active filters, based on the response from searchProducts and the current URL parameters (query, categoryId, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, sort). Does not store its own state; it reads from the latest API results and URL.

Displays a grid or list of product cards using the products returned by searchProducts. Each card shows key product fields (such as name, price, age suitability, and thumbnail) as provided by the Product model. Clicking a card navigates to product_detail.html with the product’s id as a URL parameter.

Uses total_count, page, and page_size from searchProducts to render pagination controls. When the user changes the page, updates the page URL parameter and triggers searchProducts via the page controller while keeping other URL parameters (query, filters, sort) unchanged.
Provides navigation to the cart page. When activated, navigates the user to cart.html without modifying cart contents.

Listens for URL changes (including browser back/forward) and notifies the shop-page-controller to re-read categoryId, query, minAge, maxAge, priceMax, sort, page, and pageSize (if implemented) from the URL, then refresh data via getProductFilterOptions (when categoryId changes) and searchProducts. Also exposes helper functions for other components to request URL updates in a consistent way.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header bar with logo on the left, site title centered, and a cart link on the right, followed by a single horizontal navigation row (where the Shop item is one of the primary buttons). Beneath this, a full-width, vertically generous gradient band holds a single dominant white content card. Inside that card, layout becomes a two-column arrangement: a narrower left column for category and filter sidebars, and a wider right column for search, sort, results summary, the product grid, and pagination.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header bar with logo on the left, site title centered, and a cart link on the right, followed by a single horizontal navigation row (where the Shop item is one of the primary buttons). Beneath this, a full-width, vertically generous gradient band holds a single dominant white content card. Inside that card, layout becomes a two-column arrangement: a narrower left column for category and filter sidebars, and a wider right column for search, sort, results summary, the product grid, and pagination.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (logo/title/nav/cart) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The remaining 80–85% is the gradient-backed main area with one large, centered content card. That card sits within generous outer margins (about 40–60px on all sides inside the gradient band). Inside the card, the left column (categories + filters) uses about 35–40% of the card width; the right column (search/sort/results/grid/pagination) uses about 60–65%. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale with 24–32px between major blocks and 16–24px between related elements within a block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately dense, text-forward feel. Controls and labels are relatively compact, with no oversized modern white space, but each section still has clear breathing room: solid but not cramped padding inside the card, tight but readable line lengths, and stacked sections that are separated by 16–24px. Category and filter lists are allowed to be long, but are visually contained in the left column; the right column focuses attention on products with a compact but clearly structured set of search, sort, and result info above a modestly dense product grid.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page uses a classic Z-pattern adapted for a shop: the eye starts top-left on the logo, moves across the centered title and nav, then to the cart link on the right. From there it drops diagonally down into the main card, landing first on the upper-left of the card (category sidebar), then moves horizontally across to the search bar and sort control at the upper-right. From that right side, the user’s gaze then flows down through the results summary and into the product grid, scanning left-to-right across product cards. The gradient background and slightly stronger visual weight on the right column (where the grid lives) subtly pull the eye from left filters to right results.

layout.overall_description: The Shop page appears as a centered, fixed-width experience framed by a soft full-width gradient background. At the very top, a full-width header bar spans edge-to-edge: the ministry’s logo sits on the far left, the site title and perhaps a gentle tagline are centered, and a small but noticeable cart link anchors the top-right. Directly beneath, a single horizontal row of beveled navigation buttons stretches across the same fixed-width center, with the Shop button highlighted. Below this header zone, a broad horizontal gradient band fills the rest of the viewport, within which a single large white content card sits centered, with generous margins above, below, and to each side. Inside this card, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a structured vertical stack of category and filter panels in a narrower column; on the right, a wider area with a top row containing the search bar and sort dropdown, followed by a compact text result summary, then a tidy grid of product cards, and finally pagination controls at the bottom. The overall impression is of an earnest, brochure-like catalog: orderly, text-driven, and slightly traditional, with a clear path from top navigation down into refined searching and browsing of ministry resources.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-page-controller defines the full-page canvas. It sets a centered fixed-width content area (approximately 960–1100px wide) within a full-width background. The top 15–20% of the viewport height is reserved for the header and navigation row; beneath that, the controller fills the remaining vertical space with a full-width gradient band that visually frames a single, large white content card in the center. This card sits on the vertical axis with about 40–60px of gradient background visible on all sides inside the viewport when content is minimal, expanding downward with scroll if the product list is long. Internally, the controller enforces a two-column layout inside the card: a left column for sidebar elements and a right column for main shopping interactions, each with consistent vertical spacing and padding based on the 8px scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary layout container, spanning full viewport width and at least full viewport height. The centered fixed-width content constrains horizontal growth; vertical height is scrollable as product content expands.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-sidebar is positioned inside the main white content card in the left column, anchored at the top-left of the card’s inner padding. It occupies roughly the upper 40–50% of the left column’s height (not fixed, but conceptually the first major block) and about 35–40% of the card’s width. It is vertically stacked, with a subtle heading at the top and category links or checkboxes listed downward. There is 24–32px padding between the left card edge and the category content, and about 16–24px spacing below the last category item before the filter-sidebar begins. The right edge of the category panel aligns with the natural column divide, leaving a clear 16–24px gap between this left column and the right column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a normal block within the content card. On longer lists, the sidebar simply extends downward, pushing the bottom of the card down; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-form is placed inside the main content card at the top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top of the category-sidebar. It is positioned slightly to the left within the right column, occupying roughly 60–70% of that column’s width. The search input field spans most of this width with a small skeuomorphic touch such as a subtle inset effect or beveled search button on its right. Above the search bar, there is minimal or no additional labeling to keep the header area compact. Vertically, the search-form sits 24–32px below the top inside padding of the card and shares a row with the sort-dropdown to its right, with about 16px horizontal gap between them. There is 16–24px vertical spacing below the search row before the result-summary begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard inline-block in the right-column top row; it resizes horizontally with the right column but remains centered relative to that column’s left edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar sits directly beneath the category-sidebar in the left column of the main card. It spans the full width of that left column, aligning with the same left and right insets as the categories above. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the category list and the top of the filter panel. Internally, filter groups (e.g., age range, price range) are stacked vertically with 16px between groups, maintaining the moderately dense, text-heavy feel. The combined height of category-sidebar and filter-sidebar defines the total height of the left column, which may be equal to or slightly taller than the right column, allowing both sides to scroll together as one card. Ample padding (about 24px) between the filter content and the bottom of the card keeps the panel feeling contained.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a regular vertical stack, extending the left column downward as needed; no fixed or sticky behavior. On long pages, the filter section scrolls off screen with the rest of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-dropdown is positioned in the same horizontal row as the search-form, aligned to the right edge of the right column within the content card. It occupies roughly 20–25% of the right column’s width and appears as a compact, beveled select control with a label (e.g., “Sort by:”) to its left within the same inline group. There is about 16px of horizontal space between the right edge of the search-form and the left edge of the sort control. Vertically, it aligns with the middle of the search input, creating a unified top control bar that runs across the upper portion of the right column. Below this row, 16–24px of vertical spacing separates these controls from the result-summary text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floats to the right within the top row of the right column. Respects the fixed-width card center; on narrow configurations within this design system, it continues to sit to the right of the search, compressing slightly but remaining on the same row as long as possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result-summary is positioned directly under the search and sort row in the right column of the main card. It is left-aligned with the search-form’s left edge and spans roughly 70–80% of the right column’s width, occupying only one or two lines of text. There is about 16–24px of vertical space above it (separating it from the search/sort row) and about 16–24px below it before the product-grid begins. The text itself is small to medium sized, likely serif to match the brochure feel, conveying information such as number of results and which filters or search terms are active. This area is visually modest but clearly separated as its own line in the vertical stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the right column; it expands vertically if the summary text wraps but otherwise stays compact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the bulk of the right column below the result-summary, forming the visual heart of the card. It is left-aligned with other right-column elements and extends almost to the right inner edge of the card, leaving 24–32px of internal padding from the card border. Within this area, products are laid out in a classic grid—likely two or three columns across, depending on the fixed width—with modest horizontal gaps of 16–24px between cards and similar vertical gaps between rows. Each product card is a small white or very light panel, with a thumbnail image on top and text (title, short description, price) beneath, reflecting the moderately dense, catalog-like style. The grid stretches vertically, potentially exceeding the visible viewport and causing the page to scroll while the overall two-column card structure remains intact. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates the bottom of the last product row from the pagination-controls below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically as needed while constrained to the right column width. The grid itself is not fixed; it scrolls with the rest of the content card, keeping a stable, centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the right column, directly under the product-grid, inside the main content card. A 24–32px gap separates them from the last row of products, creating a clear visual break. The controls are horizontally centered within the right column, with individual page numbers and next/previous buttons laid out in a single row.
