Buttons follow the site’s beveled, skeuomorphic style, with small to medium size and tight horizontal spacing (8–12px) between them. There is additional bottom padding (24–32px) between the pagination row and the card’s bottom edge, providing a comfortable termination to the content before the gradient background reappears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits as the last block in the right column; it remains at the bottom of the scrollable content card. As content grows, it is pushed further down, maintaining its relative position below the product-grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-link is positioned in the top header bar, aligned to the far right of the fixed-width center container, on the same horizontal baseline as the centered site title and possibly to the right of the primary navigation row or tucked into the upper-right corner above it, depending on header height. It is smaller than the main navigation buttons but more visually distinct than standard text links—perhaps a text label like “Cart” accompanied by a small icon, framed with subtle beveling or a soft highlight. There is 16–24px of horizontal padding between the cart link and the right edge of the centered content area. Vertically, it has about 8–12px of breathing room above and below within the header band, ensuring it is easily clickable but not oversized. It is the rightmost element in the top Z-pattern, completing the header’s left-to-right scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the header; it stays fixed at the top as long as the header remains in view. The header itself scrolls off screen with the page; the cart link does not have sticky behavior beyond the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component has no visual representation within the layout. It operates behind the scenes to mirror the current search term, selected categories, filters, sort order, and pagination state into the URL and back. Conceptually, it is associated with the entire content card area—observing changes from the category-sidebar, filter-sidebar, search-form, sort-dropdown, and pagination-controls—so that returning to or sharing a URL restores the same shop view. Users do not see any dedicated UI for this functionality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it does not occupy space or affect layout. It behaves as an invisible controller attached to the page and relevant interactive components.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected product resource and allows users to add it to their shopping cart.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a single product based on the URL parameter id using getProductDetails.
Show product metadata including category, price, age range, format type, and detailed description.
Display rating summary (average rating and rating count) from getProductDetails to support selection decisions.
Allow the user to choose a quantity and add the product to the cart via addToCart.
Provide clear feedback after add-to-cart (success or failure) with an option to view the cart.
Offer navigation back to the shop listing and to the cart page using link-based URL navigation.
user_workflows:
View product details:
1. User lands on product_detail.html with a URL parameter id.
2. The page-init logic reads the id parameter.
3. If id exists, the page calls getProductDetails with productId = id.
4. While loading, a loading state is shown; other components wait for product data.
5. On success, product name, category, price, age range, format type, description, and rating summary are bound to the UI components.
6. User reviews the information to decide if the resource is appropriate for their children or ministry group.

Add a product to the cart:
1. After reviewing the product, the user adjusts the quantity field as needed.
2. User clicks the Add to Cart button.
3. The add-to-cart form component calls addToCart with productId = current product.id and the selected quantity.
4. While the request is in progress, the Add to Cart button is disabled and a loading state is shown for that action.
5. On success, the page receives an updated cart object, cart_items array, and a message from addToCart.
6. A confirmation message is displayed and the user is offered a link or button to view the cart (navigates to cart.html).
7. If addToCart fails, an error message is shown instead and the user can retry.

Handle missing or invalid product id:
1. User navigates to product_detail.html without an id parameter, or with an id that results in an error from getProductDetails.
2. The page-init component detects the missing id and shows an error state without calling getProductDetails, or processes the error response from getProductDetails.
3. An instructional error message is shown with a link back to shop.html so the user can choose a valid product.

Continue shopping after viewing a product:
1. From the product detail page, the user clicks a Back to Shop or Continue Shopping link.
2. The page navigates to shop.html using a simple link (no additional parameters are required by this page).
interactions: Page load / URL change: Reading the id parameter from the URL triggers the initial getProductDetails call.
Add to Cart button click: Triggers validation of quantity and then calls addToCart.
Quantity input change: Updates the pending quantity value used for the next addToCart call.
View Cart link or cart icon click: Navigates the user to cart.html after an item is added.
Back to Shop / Continue Shopping link click: Navigates the user to shop.html.
Error state actions: For missing product id or failed data load, users can click a link to shop.html to recover.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads the id URL parameter (e.g., product_detail.html?id={productId}). If id is present, the page calls getProductDetails with productId equal to this value and enters a loading state. When getProductDetails returns successfully, the page stores the product, category, rating_summary, and detailed_description in in-memory page state; components that bind to Product and ProductCategory transition from loading to displaying content. If getProductDetails returns an error or if id is missing, the page enters an error state in which product-dependent components remain hidden and an error message plus a link to shop.html is shown. The quantity state for addToCart is initialized when product data is available, and can be updated by the user; it is not persisted to the URL. When the user clicks Add to Cart, the page calls addToCart with the current product.id and quantity. During this call, an 'addToCartInProgress' flag is set so that the add-to-cart form can disable inputs. On success, the returned cart and cart_items are stored in page state for potential inline cart summary display, and a success message state is set. On failure, an error message state is set. Navigation to cart.html or back to shop.html uses standard links and does not change or read any additional URL parameters on this page beyond id.

components.functionality: On page load or URL change, reads the id URL parameter, validates its presence, and calls getProductDetails to fetch the product data. Manages high-level loading and error states for the page.
Displays the product name, category name, and basic metadata such as SKU if available. Provides a concise overview at the top of the page once product data has loaded.

Shows the product price, age range derived from min_age and max_age, and format type (digital, print, bundle) as provided by the product data. All values update automatically when product data changes.
Displays the product description and detailed_description text returned by getProductDetails. This may include information such as scope, sequence, and included components as provided by the backend.

Shows the average rating and rating count for the product using the rating_summary from getProductDetails (or the product's rating fields when available). If rating data is missing, the component reflects that no ratings are currently available.

Allows the user to specify a quantity and add the current product to the cart. Validates that a quantity is a positive number before calling addToCart. Disables the Add to Cart button while the request is in progress and passes response data to the feedback component.

Displays success or error messages based on the result of the addToCart call, using the message property from the addToCart response or a generic error string. On success, may also show a brief summary of the newly updated cart state such as total items, derived from the cart_items data.

Provides navigation actions from the product page: a Back to Shop / Continue Shopping link to shop.html and a View Cart link to cart.html. The View Cart link is emphasized after a successful addToCart operation.

Handles error states where the product cannot be loaded (missing id parameter or getProductDetails error). Shows an appropriate message and a link back to shop.html. This component is only visible when the page-init component indicates an error state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width header and global navigation at the very top (handled by the broader site, implied by page-init), followed by a single centered content band. Inside this band sits one dominant white product-detail card. Within that card, key product content is arranged in a simple two-step hierarchy: (1) a hero summary row with a left product image and right summary text, and (2) supporting details and actions arranged in a two-column row beneath, followed by a full-width body description. Status and navigation elements (feedback banner, product navigation links, error messages) appear just above or below the main card without disrupting the core two-column layout inside the card.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width header and global navigation at the very top (handled by the broader site, implied by page-init), followed by a single centered content band. Inside this band sits one dominant white product-detail card. Within that card, key product content is arranged in a simple two-step hierarchy: (1) a hero summary row with a left product image and right summary text, and (2) supporting details and actions arranged in a two-column row beneath, followed by a full-width body description. Status and navigation elements (feedback banner, product navigation links, error messages) appear just above or below the main card without disrupting the core two-column layout inside the card.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and nav together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below this, a full-width gradient background band fills the rest of the viewport. Centered within this band is a fixed-width card (~960–1040px wide) with generous margins on all sides (about 48–64px from the top of the band and 40–60px lateral space). Inside the card, a consistent two-column grid is used: the left column occupies roughly 40–45% of the card’s internal width and the right column 55–60%. Vertical spacing between functional blocks (summary, pricing, form, description, navigation) follows the 24–32px pattern, with internal padding of ~32px around the card and 16–24px between elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content inside the card is moderately dense. Text uses relatively small, church-friendly serif body copy with tight but readable line lengths. The product hero row is visually substantial but not oversized, combining image and short summary text. The pricing, rating, and add-to-cart areas are compact and information-dense, but separated with clear 16–24px spacing so they don’t feel cramped. The full-width description section is text-heavy, mimicking brochure-like content, but is cushioned by comfortable card padding and consistent paragraph spacing. Status messages and error states are concise horizontal bands with modest padding to avoid overwhelming the core content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page preserves a classic Z-pattern. The eye starts at the logo and site title in the top-left and center of the header, moves horizontally across the global navigation row, then drops diagonally down into the gradient band and lands first on the left side of the white product card. There it encounters the product image and core summary (product-summary-header), then moves to the right toward pricing and key info (product-pricing-and-age-info) and the add-to-cart form. From there, the eye flows downward into the full-width description. The gradient background subtly lightens toward the left behind the image and deepens toward the right, reinforcing movement from visual preview (image/rating) to decision-making content (price/add-to-cart) and then to deeper reading (description). Status and error messages appear directly adjacent to this main focal card, so they are noticed quickly but do not interrupt the core Z-flow inside the card.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page sits within a centered, fixed-width layout over a gentle full-width gradient background that feels warm and church-friendly. At the top of the viewport, a full-width header bar with a ministry logo on the left and a centered site title and primary navigation bar establishes the site’s identity, with skeuomorphic touches like soft gradients and slightly beveled navigation buttons. Below this header, a wide horizontal band of gradient color stretches edge-to-edge across the browser window. In the middle of this band floats a single, dominant white card, about 960–1040px wide, with generous breathing room on all sides so it clearly reads as the main focal container. Inside the card, content is structured in a simple but formal arrangement. A top hero row shows the product’s visual and name: the left 40–45% is dedicated to the product image and key summary text, while the right 55–60% offers concise product highlights. Directly beneath, the same two-column split is reused: the left side houses rating details and a caption-like area under the image, and the right side focuses on practical decision aids—price, age range, and the add-to-cart form. Below both columns, a full-width text block runs across the card, containing the detailed product description in multiple paragraphs, echoing a printed brochure. Under the card, small product navigation links allow users to go back to the catalog or move between resources, centered or gently right-aligned. Any add-to-cart feedback or error messages appear as tinted bands inside the gradient band near the card, maintaining the traditional, earnest aesthetic and ensuring the main card remains visually dominant.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init establishes a centered, fixed-width content shell on top of a full-width gradient background. At the very top of the page, a full-width header bar stretches across the browser window. Within this header, content is constrained to approximately 1000–1100px and centered. The ministry logo appears on the far left, with the site title and tagline centered horizontally in the header’s middle zone, leaving right-side space visually lighter or for secondary meta elements (if present). Directly below the title/tagline within the same header region or just beneath it, a horizontal row of large, beveled navigation buttons spans across the center of the page, occupying roughly 70–80% of the content width. After a vertical gap of about 32–40px below this navigation row, the main gradient band begins. Centered within this band is the primary product card container: a white, slightly elevated rectangle about 960–1040px wide, with rounded or softly beveled edges and 32px internal padding on all sides. The card is horizontally centered and vertically separated from the header and the bottom of the viewport by approximately 48–64px of gradient background, making it the clear focal point of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Provides the static, centered layout container for all other components; the header and gradient band are fixed in structure but scroll away normally as page height grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white product card, the product-summary-header occupies the topmost section and sets up the foundational two-column layout. This section spans the full internal width of the card, with about 24–32px of space above it (from the card top padding) and 24px below it before the sections that follow. The content is divided into two vertical columns. The left column takes roughly 40–45% of the card’s width and contains the main product image (e.g., curriculum cover or promotional photo) near the top, filling most of the column width while keeping a 16–24px margin from the column edges. Directly above or slightly overlapping the top of the image, the product title may appear centered or left-aligned within this column, but the image remains the main visual draw here. The right column, occupying about 55–60% of the card, aligns its top content with the top of the image on the left, and contains the product name (if not in the left column), a short subtitle or category label, and 1–2 brief introductory lines of text. Text in the right column is left-aligned and stacked vertically with 8–16px between lines of different semantic levels (title, subtitle, tagline). This arrangement ensures that when a user looks at the card, the eye first lands on the left image, then moves horizontally along the Z-pattern to the right-hand title and summary text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the card; defines the two-column grid reused by subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-pricing-and-age-info section sits directly beneath the product-summary-header, aligned within the right-hand column of the card. Vertically, there is a gap of approximately 16–24px between the bottom of the summary text in the right column and the top of this pricing block, maintaining a compact but readable rhythm. Horizontally, this section inherits the right column’s width (roughly 55–60% of the card) and aligns flush with that column’s left edge, maintaining a 16–24px inner margin from the card’s right padding. Within this block, the price is placed prominently near the top, using a slightly larger or bolder serif or simple sans-serif font, left-aligned. Directly beneath the price, age suitability and related metadata (e.g., “Ages 6–10”, “Elementary”, “Small Group Resource”) are presented in 1–3 compact lines, possibly separated by small decorative bullets or vertical dividers. Overall height remains modest, occupying about 1/4 to 1/3 of the vertical space in the right column below the summary header, leaving room immediately beneath for the quantity-and-add-to-cart-form. The tone is informational and formal rather than salesy, matching the conservative nonprofit aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-column block; does not expand full width and flows vertically into the add-to-cart form below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-description-section sits further down in the card, occupying a full-width block that begins just below the two-column area containing rating, pricing, and add-to-cart elements. After the lower right column (quantity-and-add-to-cart-form) and the lower left column (rating summary and caption) conclude, there is a vertical separation of around 32px before this description block begins, clearly signaling a shift from decision-focused content to in-depth reading. The description spans nearly the entire internal width of the card, with 32px of side padding preserved from the card edge. Text is left-aligned, serif, and divided into multiple paragraphs with approximately 12–16px of space between paragraphs. The line length is moderate, around 60–75 characters, to support easier reading despite the relatively small font. If subheadings or bullet points exist, they are indented or bolded but still tightly integrated, maintaining the information-dense yet organized feel of a printed brochure. This section may extend vertically as needed, often occupying 40–60% of the card’s total height when the product description is long. The card’s bottom padding of about 32px provides breathing room beneath the last line of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the card; expands vertically based on content and pushes the bottom of the card downward accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-rating-summary is positioned in the lower part of the left column inside the main card, directly beneath the product image contained in the product-summary-header. There is a vertical spacing of approximately 12–16px between the bottom of the image and the top of the rating summary. The rating summary width matches the left column (roughly 40–45% of the card), with 16–24px internal side padding so the content doesn’t touch the column edges. Rating stars or a rating icon row are centered relative to the left column, echoing the caption-like treatment seen in the mockup: they form a horizontal band under the image. Beneath the stars, a short line of text—for example, an average rating value, number of reviews, or a simple phrase such as “Loved by children’s ministry leaders”—is centered as well, spanning no more than two lines. Overall, this section’s height is modest, about the same as the pricing block on the right column, preserving visual symmetry between the two columns. The combination of centered alignment and proximity to the image reinforces the sense that this is a caption or endorsement of the visual resource.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored to the lower part of the left column; does not affect the right column layout but aligns vertically to create a balanced two-column row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-and-add-to-cart-form resides in the right column directly beneath the product-pricing-and-age-info block. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the bottom of the pricing/age info and the top of the form. This form uses the same column width as the pricing block (around 55–60% of the card) and aligns with it, creating a unified decision-making column. Inside this form area, the quantity input is left-aligned and sits on the top line or near the top-left, with an associated label (e.g., “Quantity:”) flush to its left or directly above it, keeping horizontal space efficient. To the right of the quantity field or directly beneath it (depending on space), the primary “Add to Cart” button appears as a large, beveled, skeuomorphic-style button with a subtle gradient and soft drop shadow. It spans approximately 60–75% of the right column width, centered within that column or left-aligned with a wide horizontal footprint to emphasize importance. Vertical spacing between the quantity field and the button is around 8–12px, matching the mockup’s moderately dense style. The form as a whole occupies a relatively compact vertical area, roughly equal to or slightly taller than the pricing block above, so the two together fill most of the right column height before the description section begins below. This cluster reinforces the user’s eye path: image → price/age → quantity & add-to-cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-column form; remains within the card and scrolls normally with the page, with no sticky/floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-feedback-banner appears just outside the main card but within the same gradient band, directly beneath the bottom edge of the card. When displayed, it occupies a full-width strip aligned to the content width of the card: the banner is the same width as the card (around 960–1040px) and centered horizontally. A vertical gap of approximately 16–24px separates the bottom of the card and the top of the banner, keeping them visually linked but distinct. The banner’s background uses a soft tinted color (e.g., pale green for success, warm yellow for informational messages) with a subtle gradient and very light border or beveled edge to match the early-2010s skeuomorphic feel. Text is centered horizontally, set in one or two lines, with around 12–16px of padding above and below. If an icon is used (e.g., checkmark for success), it sits to the left of the text but the entire group remains centered. At smaller page heights or when the description is long, the banner remains directly after the card’s content, so users see feedback immediately after scrolling past the add-to-cart button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears conditionally; it pushes content below it downward and scrolls with the page rather than sticking to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-navigation-links section is placed beneath the add-to-cart-feedback-banner when that banner is present, or directly beneath the main product card when no banner is visible. There is a vertical separation of about 24–32px from whichever element is above it, maintaining clear hierarchy. This navigation area shares the same centered content width as the card and banner (around 960–1040px) but is visually lighter. The links (e.g., “Back to resources”, “Previous product”, “Next product”) are arranged horizontally on a single line when space permits, with 16–24px gaps between each link. The entire row is either center-aligned or slightly right-aligned within the content width, keeping with a traditional page-footer-like treatment. The typography is smaller and less bold than primary navigation, often underlined or colored in a subtle accent tone that stands out gently against the gradient background. The overall height of this area is minimal, with about 8–12px padding above and below the link row. This placement allows users who finish reading the description to naturally move down the page and discover options to return or browse other products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal link row at the bottom of the main content band; scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-error-state is presented as a distinct, full-width alert band within the gradient background, typically positioned immediately above the main product card. When active, it stretches to match the card’s width (around 960–1040px) and is centered horizontally, keeping alignment with all other content. A vertical space of 16–24px separates this error band from the bottom of the header/navigation area. The band uses a stronger tinted background (such as a muted red or dark amber gradient) with a thin, beveled border to clearly signal a problem, while still fitting the conservative, skeuomorphic aesthetic. Inside the band, error text is left-aligned for clarity, with any alert icon (e.g., exclamation mark) positioned to the left of the text on the same baseline. Padding of 12–16px on all sides ensures the text is comfortably readable. In cases where the error prevents product loading entirely, this band may appear in place of the product card, occupying the same vertical region but with extra descriptive copy or links spread over multiple lines. When the error is specific (e.g., add-to-cart failure), the card remains visible below, and the band’s proximity to the card makes it immediately apparent which content is affected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered static block; it appears in the flow above the card and pushes the main card downward, scrolling away naturally with the rest of the page.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Displays the user’s current cart items (curriculum and resources), allows quantity updates and item removal using cart APIs, shows computed totals, and provides navigation to checkout, the shop, and ministry donation options.

core_features: Fetch and display the current cart and all cart items using getCart, including product details, age range label, line subtotals, subtotal, estimated tax, shipping estimate, and total.
Allow users to change the quantity of each cart item and persist the change via updateCartItemQuantity, with live recalculation of totals based on the updated cart data.
Allow users to remove items from the cart via removeCartItem, updating the list of items and totals after successful removal.
Handle an empty-cart state that encourages users to return to the shop to add curriculum or other products.
Provide a clear path to proceed toward checkout or payment instructions (via navigation to a separate checkout/payment page) once the cart is ready.
Offer a secondary call-to-action from the cart to also support the ministry (navigate to donate.html).
Show context-aware back/continue-shopping messaging based on the optional from URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View cart from the header:
1. User clicks the Cart link/icon in the site header from any page.
2. Browser navigates to cart.html, optionally including a from query parameter (e.g., cart.html?from=shop).
3. On page load, the page’s data loader component calls getCart.
4. If getCart returns one or more items, the cart item list is rendered with each item’s product name, age_range_label, unit price, quantity control, and line_subtotal. The totals summary is populated from subtotal, estimated_tax, shipping_estimate, and total.
5. If getCart returns an empty items array or a subtotal/total indicating no items, the empty-cart component is shown instead of the item list, with a link to shop.html.

Update cart item quantity:
1. In the cart item list, the user changes the quantity for a specific item using an input field or increment/decrement control.
2. On blur or explicit update action for that field, the page triggers updateCartItemQuantity with the corresponding cart_item.id and the new quantity.
3. While waiting for the response, the item can show a loading state or disabled controls for that row.
4. If updateCartItemQuantity.success is true, the local cart state is refreshed (either by using the returned cart/cart_items data or by calling getCart again), and the item list and totals are re-rendered.
5. If updateCartItemQuantity.success is false, an error message from updateCartItemQuantity.message is displayed near the item or in a global message area, and the previous valid quantity is restored in the UI.

Remove an item from the cart:
1. User clicks a Remove action on a specific cart item row.
2. The page triggers removeCartItem with that cart_item.id.
3. While waiting for the response, the row can show a loading/removal-in-progress state.
4. If removeCartItem.success is true, the cart state is updated using the returned cart/cart_items or via a new getCart call; the removed item no longer appears, and totals are recalculated and displayed.
5. If the resulting cart contains no items, the empty-cart component is shown.
6. If removeCartItem.success is false, an error message from removeCartItem.message is shown and the item remains visible.

Proceed toward checkout/payment instructions:
1. After reviewing items and totals, the user clicks a Proceed to Checkout (or similar) button.
2. The browser navigates to a dedicated checkout or payment instructions page (e.g., checkout.html), optionally preserving the from parameter if needed for later navigation.
3. No cart APIs other than those defined for this page are invoked at this step; this page’s responsibility ends with navigation.

Continue shopping with context-based messaging:
1. When the user arrives at cart.html with a from parameter (e.g., ?from=shop, ?from=product_detail, or ?from=index), the back-navigation banner component reads the parameter.
2. Based on the parameter, the banner displays tailored text (for example, referencing the shop or homepage) while the Continue Shopping link points to shop.html.
3. The user clicks the Continue Shopping link and is navigated to shop.html to browse more products.

Support the ministry from the cart:
1. On the cart page, a donation call-to-action component is displayed alongside the cart summary.
2. User clicks the donation call-to-action.
3. The browser navigates to donate.html, where separate donation functionality is handled (no additional cart APIs are called from the cart page for this action).
interactions:
Page load interaction:
- Event: onPageLoad
- Action: Invoke getCart.
- Result: Bind returned cart, items (each with cart_item, product, age_range_label, line_subtotal), subtotal, estimated_tax, shipping_estimate, and total to the cart components. Display item list if items are present; otherwise display empty-cart state.

Change quantity for a cart item:
- Event: onQuantityChange (e.g., input change, stepper click) for a given cart row.
- Immediate UI: Update the quantity field locally, apply basic validation (e.g., non-negative, integer) in the UI layer without hardcoded limits.
- Event: onQuantityCommit (e.g., onBlur or onClickUpdate) for that row.
- Action: Call updateCartItemQuantity with parameters cartItemId = cart_item.id and quantity = newQuantity.
- On success: Update cart state using the response (cart and cart_items) and/or follow-up getCart call. Refresh item list, line_subtotals, and totals; display a non-intrusive success message if desired.
- On failure: Show the message from updateCartItemQuantity.message and reset the quantity field to the last confirmed value.

Remove a cart item:
- Event: onClickRemove for a cart item.
- Optional: Show a confirmation dialog (handled in UI logic, no data assumptions).
- Action (after confirmation): Call removeCartItem with cartItemId = cart_item.id.
- On success: Update the cart state, removing that item and recalculating totals. If there are no items left, show the empty-cart component.
- On failure: Display removeCartItem.message and keep the item visible.

Refresh cart contents manually (if implemented):
- Event: onClickRefreshCart.
- Action: Call getCart again and rebind data to item list and totals.
- Use case: To recover from transient errors or ensure cart reflects changes made in another tab/session.

Navigate to checkout/payment instructions:
- Event: onClickProceedToCheckout.
- Action: Navigate to a checkout or payment instructions page (e.g., checkout.html). The current cart state is not modified by this action from the cart page; it is read-only navigation.

Continue shopping from cart:
- Event: onClickContinueShopping.
- Action: Navigate to shop.html. Optionally keep or drop the from parameter depending on desired behavior, but no cart APIs are called as part of this navigation.

Navigate to donation page:
- Event: onClickDonationCTA.
- Action: Navigate to donate.html, leaving the current cart untouched.

Handle empty cart interaction:
- Event: onEmptyCartViewRendered (implicit).
- UI: Show message indicating the cart is empty and highlight the Continue Shopping link.
- Event: onClickContinueShopping (from empty state).
- Action: Navigate to shop.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state for this page is managed via the getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem interfaces. The URL parameter from (string) is used to influence navigation-related messaging and behavior:

- Reading from parameter:
  - On page initialization, the page reads window.location.search to extract the from parameter, if present.
  - Supported values are not strictly constrained by the cart APIs; the page simply treats them as hints. Known source values include 'shop', 'product_detail', and 'index', but the implementation does not hardcode behavior to specific values beyond customizing text.

- Effects of from parameter:
  - Back-navigation banner text: The banner component adjusts its helper text based on the from value. For example, if from indicates the user came from the shop, the banner may reference returning to browsing resources; if from is missing or unrecognized, it falls back to a generic message.
  - Continue Shopping navigation intent: Regardless of the from value, the Continue Shopping link target remains shop.html as defined by outgoing navigation. The from value influences only the wording, not the destination.
  - Checkout navigation: If desired, the from value can be appended when navigating to checkout.html so that the checkout flow later knows where to send the user back after completion, but this does not affect any cart API calls.

- Cart data state:
  - The canonical cart state (cart, items, subtotals and totals) is always sourced from the most recent successful call to getCart or from the most recent successful mutation response from updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem.
  - The page does not persist cart state in localStorage or other client-side storage; instead, it relies on re-calling getCart as needed.
  - After each successful call to updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem, the in-memory cart state is updated immediately using the interface’s response or a follow-up getCart call to ensure consistency with server-side calculations.

- Error and message state:
  - Success and error messages from updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem are maintained in transient in-memory state for display in a global message area or per-line message field.
  - These messages are cleared or replaced on subsequent operations or page reloads; they are not stored in URL parameters or localStorage.

No other URL parameters are required by this page beyond from, and no persistent state is maintained in localStorage. All authoritative data about the cart and its items comes from the assigned interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page maintains the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header at the very top with the ministry logo on the left and a centered site title/tagline; directly beneath, a single horizontal row of large navigation buttons including a highlighted state for “Cart”; and below that, a wide horizontal gradient band that centers one primary white content card. Inside this card, the cart page content is organized in a traditional brochure-like way: a brief page heading and explanatory copy at the top, then a two-column body where the left side holds the cart item list and the right side holds the order summary, totals, and ministry donation options. Supporting links like “Back to Shop” are placed near the bottom of the card, center-aligned in the remaining width to echo the conservative, centered composition of the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page maintains the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header at the very top with the ministry logo on the left and a centered site title/tagline; directly beneath, a single horizontal row of large navigation buttons including a highlighted state for “Cart”; and below that, a wide horizontal gradient band that centers one primary white content card. Inside this card, the cart page content is organized in a traditional brochure-like way: a brief page heading and explanatory copy at the top, then a two-column body where the left side holds the cart item list and the right side holds the order summary, totals, and ministry donation options. Supporting links like “Back to Shop” are placed near the bottom of the card, center-aligned in the remaining width to echo the conservative, centered composition of the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header plus navigation together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height, stretching full-width but visually anchored to the centered content width. Below, the main gradient band fills the remaining 80–85% of the viewport, with the white cart card centered and constrained to about 960–1040px width. This card is inset with generous margins from the gradient background (about 40–60px on all sides), making it the clear focal area. Inside the card, content padding of about 24–32px separates the card edges from the content. The top card header region (title and intro text) takes up around 15–20% of the card height, while the two-column cart-and-summary region fills most of the middle 60–70%. Within that middle region, the left column (cart items) occupies about 55–60% of the card’s width, while the right column (totals, actions, donation callouts) uses about 40–45%, reflecting the mockup’s slight emphasis on the text side in its two-column design.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page embraces a moderately dense, text-forward layout reminiscent of early-2010s nonprofit sites. Body copy uses a relatively small serif font with tight yet readable line lengths, allowing multiple paragraphs of guidance and explanation without feeling overly airy. The cart items table or list is compact, with rows separated by 8–12px and internal cell padding of 8–16px. However, the surrounding 24–32px padding inside the card, plus 16–24px spacing between the left and right columns, keeps the design from feeling cramped. The order summary and donation panel on the right side uses stacked content blocks, separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, supporting a brochure-like, information-rich feel while maintaining visual clarity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a classic Z-pattern. The eye first catches the logo at the top-left of the header bar, travels horizontally to the centered title and across the navigation buttons (where the Cart tab is clearly emphasized). From there, the gaze moves diagonally down-left to the start of the main card where the cart page title and the upper-left of the cart items list reside, then follows the column of cart items downward. After scanning the items, the eye shifts horizontally to the right column to review the order summary, totals, checkout button, and the ministry donation options. The gradient background subtly supports this flow by being slightly lighter on the left, behind the start of the card and cart content, and gradually deepening toward the right, behind the summary and call-to-action area, reinforcing a left-to-right journey that ends on the primary checkout and donation actions.

layout.overall_description: The cart page is set within a traditional, centered, fixed-width layout that feels like a printed church brochure translated to the web. At the very top, a full-width horizontal header bar spans the browser window. On the left side of this bar, the ministry’s logo sits with a modest margin from the edge; in the center, the site’s name and a warm, faith-oriented tagline are stacked or tightly grouped. Just beneath the header, a row of large, beveled navigation buttons runs horizontally across the same fixed-width content area, each button padded and skeuomorphic, with the “Cart” button visually highlighted. Below this navigation row, the background transitions into a soft horizontal gradient that extends full-width; at its center, a large white rectangular card floats with generous space on all sides, framed by the gradient. This card, set to roughly a 960–1040px width, opens with a cart page heading near the top-left, followed by a short paragraph or two explaining that these curriculum resources support children’s ministry and how purchases and donations help. The main body of the card is split into two columns: on the left, a vertically stacked list or table of cart items, each row showing resource names, small thumbnails, quantities, and update/remove controls; on the right, a vertically ordered sequence of summary panels showing subtotal, any discounts, tax, shipping, and grand total, topped by a prominent beveled “Proceed to Checkout” button, with secondary links and donation options arranged beneath. The left and right columns are separated by a modest 16–24px gap. At the bottom of the card, spanning the full width below both columns, a narrow row holds center-aligned, text-style links such as “Continue Shopping in the Resource Library” and a softer call-to-donation, reinforcing the ministry’s mission. Overall, the page feels grounded, text-rich, and calm, guiding the visitor down from the header, across the navigation, into the cart content, and finally to the checkout and ministry support decisions on the right.

name: Volunteer Opportunities Listing
title: Volunteer Opportunities
description: Browse and filter current volunteer opportunities, including in-person child-focused Sunday roles, with filters for serve type, role focus, location, availability, and sort order. Users can refine results, see distance from a specified ZIP code, and open a detailed opportunity page.

core_features: Initialize available filter and sort options by calling getVolunteerFilterOptions and bind them to serve type, role focus, day-of-week, time-block, and sort controls.
Read URL parameters (serveType, roleFocus, postalCode, distanceMiles, dayOfWeek, timeBlock, sort, page) on load to pre-populate filters and initial sort state.
Search and paginate volunteer opportunities by calling searchVolunteerOpportunities with filters derived from UI state and URL parameters.
Display a list of volunteer opportunities, including title, summary, serve type, role focus, location information, availability/time commitments, and distance from the search location when provided.
Provide a location filter where users can input postal code and distance radius to limit results to opportunities within the given distance.
Offer availability filters for day of week and time block, mapped to the VolunteerTimeSlot-related criteria used by searchVolunteerOpportunities.
Enable sorting of opportunities using sort options from getVolunteerFilterOptions (e.g., by distance or other relevance metrics).
Support navigation to a detailed volunteer opportunity page (volunteer_detail.html) when a user clicks a specific opportunity card, passing the opportunity id as a URL parameter.
Keep filters, sort order, and current page synchronized with URL parameters so that the list is deep-linkable and compatible with browser navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the page from another site section with pre-selected filters
1) User clicks a link to the Volunteer Opportunities page from index.html or about.html that includes one or more URL parameters (such as serveType and roleFocus, and possibly postalCode, distanceMiles, dayOfWeek, timeBlock, sort).
2) volunteer_list.html loads and a controller component parses the incoming URL parameters.
3) The controller calls getVolunteerFilterOptions to retrieve the available options for serve type, role focus, day-of-week, time-block, and sort.
4) The filter UI is populated with these options and the incoming URL parameter values are applied as the initially selected values.
5) The controller constructs a filters object from the URL parameters and calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with filters, sort_by (from the sort URL parameter), page (from the URL or default), and page_size.
6) When results are returned, the page displays the list of volunteer opportunities and the results summary.
7) The user can further adjust filters, location, or sort; the page updates URL parameters and re-issues searchVolunteerOpportunities accordingly.

Workflow 2: Filtering by location and availability
1) User arrives on volunteer_list.html (either without any URL filters or with some default ones).
2) User enters a postal code and selects a distance radius in the location filter form.
3) User optionally selects a preferred day of week and time block via the availability filters.
4) User submits the location/availability filters (either explicitly via a submit action or implicitly on change as defined) which triggers an update of the URL parameters (postalCode, distanceMiles, dayOfWeek, timeBlock, page reset to 1).
5) The controller constructs the filters object using the updated URL parameters and calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with those filters and the current sort.
6) Returned opportunities are displayed with distance information and any availability labels provided in the data.
7) User may adjust the filters again; each change updates the URL and triggers a new search.

Workflow 3: Sorting and pagination
1) After performing a search, the user opens the sort control and selects a sort option provided by getVolunteerFilterOptions.sort_options.
2) The sort control updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and notifies the controller.
3) The controller calls searchVolunteerOpportunities using the existing filters but with the new sort_by value; results refresh in the list.
4) The user scrolls to pagination controls and navigates to another page of results (e.g., next/previous or choosing a page number).
5) Pagination updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers searchVolunteerOpportunities with the same filters and sort, but a different page value.
6) The list updates to the requested page of opportunities, while filters and sort remain intact.

Workflow 4: Opening a volunteer opportunity detail
1) User finds an opportunity of interest in the results list.
2) User clicks anywhere on the opportunity card (or a designated action within it).
3) The click handler reads the opportunity.id from the bound data model and navigates the browser to volunteer_detail.html with id set as a URL parameter (e.g., volunteer_detail.html?id={opportunity.id}).
4) The detail page uses the id parameter to load and display full information about that specific volunteer role.

Workflow 5: Using browser navigation and deep links
1) User applies several filters and navigates to a specific page and sort order; the URL is continuously updated to reflect this state.
2) User copies the URL and shares it or bookmarks it.
3) Another visitor (or the same user later) opens that URL; on load, the controller parses all URL parameters and applies them to the filters, sort, and page state.
4) The controller calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with the reconstructed state and the same results context is reproduced.
5) If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the URL changes cause the controller to re-parse URL parameters and re-run searchVolunteerOpportunities so the UI always reflects the current URL state.
interactions: On page load: A controller component reads the URL parameters, calls getVolunteerFilterOptions with no parameters, populates filter controls with the returned options, applies URL-based default selections, and then calls searchVolunteerOpportunities using a filters object derived from the URL plus the sort and page values.

Filter changes (serve type, role focus, day-of-week, time-block): When a filter control value changes, the corresponding URL parameter (serveType, roleFocus, dayOfWeek, timeBlock) is updated. Page is reset to 1. The controller then calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with the updated filters and existing sort_by value.

Location filter interactions: When the user submits the location form (postal code and distance), the URL parameters postalCode and distanceMiles are updated (or removed if cleared). Page is reset to 1. The controller then calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with updated filters including the location criteria.

Sort interactions: When the user selects a sort option, the sort URL parameter is updated to the selected sort option value. Page is reset to 1. The controller re-calls searchVolunteerOpportunities with the unchanged filters but new sort_by value.

Pagination interactions: When the user interacts with pagination controls (next, previous, or direct page selection), the page URL parameter is updated. searchVolunteerOpportunities is called with the same filters and sort_by but the new page value.

Clearing filters: When the user clears all filters (if a clear-all control is provided), all related URL parameters (serveType, roleFocus, postalCode, distanceMiles, dayOfWeek, timeBlock, sort, page) are reset or removed. The controller re-runs searchVolunteerOpportunities using the baseline filters derived from the now-minimal URL state.

Card click to detail navigation: When an opportunity card is clicked, the click handler reads the associated VolunteerOpportunity.id and updates window.location or uses routing to navigate to volunteer_detail.html?id={id}.

Error/empty states: If searchVolunteerOpportunities returns no results, the list component hides the cards and displays an empty-state message. If an error occurs (e.g., network failure), an error message area is updated; filters remain available so user can retry.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses query-string URL parameters as the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state. On every load or URL change, the page parses the current values of serveType, roleFocus, postalCode, distanceMiles, dayOfWeek, timeBlock, sort, and page.

Mapping between URL parameters and searchVolunteerOpportunities:
- serveType (string) maps to filters.serve_type.
- roleFocus (string) maps to filters.role_focus.
- postalCode (string) maps to filters.postal_code.
- distanceMiles (number) maps to filters.distance_miles.
- dayOfWeek (string) maps to filters.day_of_week.
- timeBlock (string) maps to filters.time_block.
- sort (string) maps to the sort_by argument.
- page (number) maps directly to the page argument for searchVolunteerOpportunities.

When no value is present for a particular parameter, that criterion is omitted from the filters object passed to searchVolunteerOpportunities. When page is missing or invalid, the controller treats page as the first page (handled in logic, not by hardcoded numeric literals in this specification) and does not include an explicit page parameter if the interface can infer it.

Whenever the user interacts with any filter, sort control, or pagination control, the page updates the relevant URL parameters and then reissues searchVolunteerOpportunities based on the new URL state. The filters UI derives its current selections strictly from these URL parameters, so the visible UI always matches shareable URL state. getVolunteerFilterOptions is called on initial load (and optionally when needed to refresh options) and does not depend on URL parameters.

Transient UI state such as loading indicators, error visibility, or whether filter panels are expanded is managed within the page runtime and not stored in URL parameters. All meaningful search criteria and result navigation options are persisted via the URL to enable deep linking and browser history compatibility.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching, URL state parsing, and communication between filter, sort, pagination, and results components. It initializes filters via getVolunteerFilterOptions and performs all calls to searchVolunteerOpportunities whenever URL-derived state changes.

Renders serve type and role focus filters as controls populated from getVolunteerFilterOptions. On change, it updates the corresponding URL parameters (serveType, roleFocus), resets the page parameter, and notifies the controller to re-run searchVolunteerOpportunities.

Displays availability filters for day of week and time block using options from getVolunteerFilterOptions. Changes update dayOfWeek and timeBlock URL parameters and trigger a new search through the controller.

Provides inputs for postal code and distance radius. It reads any existing postalCode and distanceMiles from the URL to pre-fill fields. When the user submits or clears the form, it updates the postalCode and distanceMiles URL parameters and signals the controller to call searchVolunteerOpportunities with updated location filters.

Displays sort options retrieved from getVolunteerFilterOptions.sort_options. The currently selected sort is derived from the sort URL parameter. When a new sort option is selected, it updates the sort URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and notifies the controller to re-run searchVolunteerOpportunities.

Summarizes the currently applied filters (serve type, role focus, location, availability) based on URL parameters and provides controls to clear individual filters or all filters. Clearing a filter removes its corresponding URL parameter and triggers the controller to refresh results.
Displays metadata about the current search results, such as total number of opportunities and which page is currently shown. It updates whenever searchVolunteerOpportunities returns new data.

Renders the list of volunteer opportunity cards using the opportunities array returned by searchVolunteerOpportunities. Each card shows key fields such as title, summary, serve type, role focus, location details, any provided availability information, and distance_miles_from_search when available.

Represents a single volunteer opportunity within the list. It binds to one VolunteerOpportunity item and exposes its title, summary, serve_type, role_focus, location_description and/or any location-related field, distance_miles_from_search, and any availability metadata returned by the search. Clicking the card triggers navigation to the detail page with the opportunity id.

Displays pagination controls for the opportunities list based on searchVolunteerOpportunities.total_count, page, and page_size. When the user moves between pages, it updates the page URL parameter and triggers the controller to request that page from searchVolunteerOpportunities.

Shows an informative message when searchVolunteerOpportunities returns zero opportunities. It may also suggest adjusting filters. This component is visible only when total_count is zero and there is no loading or error state.
Indicates when a search is in progress. It is shown while searchVolunteerOpportunities is pending and hidden once a response (success or error) is received.
Displays an error message when a call to getVolunteerFilterOptions or searchVolunteerOpportunities fails. It allows the user to retry the search, which reuses the current URL-derived filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header and navigation (outside this spec), then a large centered white content card sitting on a full-width warm gradient band. Inside this card, content is organized into a simple two-column layout: a narrower left column for volunteer filters and control context, and a wider right column for the results summary, sorting, listing, and states. The volunteer-listing-controller sits at the very top of the card as an intro strip, followed by the two-column structure that mirrors the mockup’s image-left, text-right pattern, but using filters-left, results-right instead of photography.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header and navigation (outside this spec), then a large centered white content card sitting on a full-width warm gradient band. Inside this card, content is organized into a simple two-column layout: a narrower left column for volunteer filters and control context, and a wider right column for the results summary, sorting, listing, and states. The volunteer-listing-controller sits at the very top of the card as an intro strip, followed by the two-column structure that mirrors the mockup’s image-left, text-right pattern, but using filters-left, results-right instead of photography.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content card is centered on the page, using roughly 960–1040px width within the fixed-width layout, with generous margins of about 40–60px from the gradient background on all sides. Inside the card, there is 24–32px padding all around. The left column occupies about 40% of the internal card width, and the right column about 60%, separated by a 16px vertical gutter. Vertical spacing between stacked elements inside each column generally follows the 16–24px rhythm, with slightly larger 24–32px gaps between major sections (filters block vs. results block vs. pagination).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense, text-first, and brochure-like. The volunteer-listing-controller shows a concise heading and maybe a short line of copy, but does not dominate the card. Filter groups in the left column stack in compact blocks with clear labels, small controls, and modest vertical spacing (around 12–16px between related controls). The right column packs the results summary, sorting controls, applied filters summary, and a vertical list of opportunity cards with small but readable margins and tight line lengths, reflecting a traditional, information-dense nonprofit style rather than a spacious modern layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow echoes the mockup’s Z-pattern. At the page level, the eye starts at the top-left logo and centered title/navigation, then moves down to the centered white card. Inside the card, the eye is first caught by the volunteer-listing-controller heading near the top center, then moves slightly down and left to the filter stack, then diagonally across to the right column where the results summary and sort control live, and finally down through the list of opportunities. A subtle left-to-right emphasis is preserved: left column helps users prepare or refine their search, and the right column is the primary reading area for the opportunity information.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header and navigation, the main viewport is filled by a warm, subtle gradient background, within which a single large white card is centered. This card, about 960–1040px wide with generous padding, is the clear focal point of the page. At the top inside the card sits a compact, text-focused volunteer-listing-controller strip that introduces the Volunteer Opportunities page. Just below, the interior of the card splits into two columns: on the left, a medium-width vertical stack of filter sections lets users refine serve type, role focus, availability, and location; on the right, a wider, text-heavy results area shows a concise results summary and sort control at the top, followed by a bar summarizing applied filters, then a scrolling column of opportunity cards. Pagination sits at the bottom of this right column, aligned toward the right edge. If there are no results, an empty state message appears centered in the right column in place of the list; during loading or errors, simple, text-first indicators appear in that same space. The whole layout feels like a traditional nonprofit brochure translated to the web: conservative, centered, and earnest, with gentle gradients and strong structural clarity that guide the user from filters on the left to detailed opportunities on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-listing-controller occupies a horizontal band at the very top inside the white content card, just below the card’s top padding. It spans almost the full internal width of the card (about 90–95%, centered), with 24–32px of padding above and below its text to give it presence without overwhelming the card. The main heading is center-aligned within this band, echoing the centered, earnest titles in the mockup, with an optional short descriptive line directly beneath it. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of this controller band and the beginning of the two-column content area. The left and right columns visually tuck under this band, aligning their left and right edges with the controller’s content edges so the card feels structurally unified.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card’s normal flow; it scrolls away with the card content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar-serve-role sits at the top of the left column inside the main content card, just below the vertical gap that follows the volunteer-listing-controller. The left column begins about 24px below the controller band and 24–32px in from the left edge of the card, using roughly 40% of the card’s internal width. Within this left column, the serve/role filter block spans the full column width and is boxed in modest vertical padding (about 16px top and bottom) with its label at the top and controls arranged beneath. There is approximately 16px of vertical space below this block before the next filter section, creating a neat stacked appearance that mirrors a vertical sidebar but remains contained within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the left column; it does not float or stick, and scrolls normally with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar-availability is positioned directly under the serve/role filter in the left column, separated from it by about 16px of vertical space. It matches the same width as the left column (about 40% of the card’s internal width) and follows the same internal padding and typographic treatment, so the two blocks feel like a cohesive filter stack. Its controls (for days, times, or frequency) are arranged in a compact vertical or grid pattern, with small 8–12px internal gaps to retain the moderately dense, text-focused feel. Another 16px vertical gap separates this block from the location filter below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; part of the vertical filter stack in the left column, scrolling as a group with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar-location sits near the bottom of the initial filter stack in the left column, directly beneath the availability filter with around 16px of vertical separation. It occupies the full width of the left column and includes a location input (such as ZIP code) and distance selector placed side-by-side or stacked, depending on their width, but all kept within the 40%-width column. The block mirrors the same visual structure as the other filter bars: a clear label at the top, followed by compact controls with small internal spacing. Below this block, there is a slightly larger vertical gap (about 24px) before any additional left-column content (if present), allowing the filter group to read as a single functional cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains part of the left-column filter cluster, sitting within the card’s normal vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control resides in the right column near its top, visually aligned with the results-summary. The right column begins at the same vertical height as the filter-bar-serve-role but on the opposite side of a 16px gutter, occupying approximately 60% of the card’s internal width. At the top of this right column, the results-summary text appears on the left side of a horizontal row, while the sort-control is right-aligned within that same row, giving a balanced, traditional layout. This top row sits roughly 24px below the volunteer-listing-controller band. The sort-control is a compact dropdown or segmented control with about 8–12px of padding, visually understated but clearly clickable, sitting flush with or slightly indented from the right column’s edge. There is about 12–16px of vertical space below this row before the applied-filters-summary bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; lives within the right column, aligned to the top-right of the results area, and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary appears directly under the combined results-summary and sort-control row in the right column, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space. It spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 60% of the card’s internal width), forming a low-profile horizontal band containing small pill-like tags or labels that list the active filters. This band has slightly lighter background treatment or subtle border to echo the beveled, skeuomorphic style without being flashy. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space below this summary bar before the opportunity-list begins, visually bridging the gap between filters on the left and detailed results on the right by reminding users what is currently applied.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; positioned in-line within the right column’s flow, just above the main results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary anchors the top of the right column as the primary introduction to the results area. It is left-aligned in the same horizontal row as the sort-control, which sits on the far right of the right column. This row begins roughly 24px below the volunteer-listing-controller band and aligns vertically with the top of the filter-bar-serve-role in the left column, reinforcing the two-column structure. The results-summary text (for example, a line reading '24 volunteer opportunities near your location') occupies the left two-thirds of this row in the right column and is set in a slightly stronger type weight than the body text to give it prominence without appearing like a major headline. The row itself is about 40–48px tall, with comfortable top and bottom padding, before the applied-filters-summary appears below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; a fixed part of the right column’s upper section, scrolling away with the results when the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The opportunity-list occupies the central vertical portion of the right column, just below the applied-filters-summary. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the applied-filters-summary and the first opportunity-card. The list is a simple vertical stack of cards, each card spanning almost the full width of the right column (leaving 16–24px side padding inside the card). Cards are separated by consistent vertical spacing of about 16–24px, giving the column a steady rhythm without feeling sparse. This list area extends downward and forms the main scrollable body of the page content. The left column filters maintain their height, while the right column’s list can extend much further, emphasizing that this is where the bulk of the user’s reading and scanning activity happens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it grows vertically as more opportunities are present and scrolls within the page’s main content area. It shares the same scroll context as the filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each opportunity-card is a white or very light-toned sub-card within the larger content card, placed inside the opportunity-list in the right column. A single card spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 60% of the card’s internal width minus inner padding) and has internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides, echoing the main card’s comfortable but not overly spacious feel. The content within each card is arranged in a traditional top-to-bottom order: title at the top, followed by a short subtitle or role type line, then a brief descriptive paragraph, and finally a compact row at the bottom showing key details like location, distance from ZIP, and schedule. Vertical spacing between lines is tight but clear (8–12px) to maintain text density. Cards have subtle borders or shadows consistent with the early-2010s skeuomorphic style, helping them stand out against the white background of the main card while still feeling part of a unified design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; each card is a block-level element in the opportunity-list stack, with no floating. It respects the right column’s padding and aligns with other cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the right column, directly below the last opportunity-card in the opportunity-list, separated from the final card by about 24–32px of vertical space to signal a clear end to the list. The controls are right-aligned within the right column, echoing the alignment of the sort-control above, and consist of a row of beveled buttons or links (Previous, page numbers, Next) with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding per button. This gives them enough size to feel clickable but not oversized relative to the dense typography. There is a modest amount of bottom padding (24–32px) between the pagination row and the bottom edge of the main card, maintaining the generous breathing room consistent with the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; the controls appear at the end of the current page of results and scroll into view as users move down. They do not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message replaces the opportunity-list area when no results match the current filters. It is centered within the right column, both horizontally and with comfortable vertical padding. In practice, it appears in the space where the first opportunity-card would be, below the applied-filters-summary. The message is presented in a simple, text-first block with a slightly larger font size than body copy and perhaps a subtle icon, all within a lightly framed or shaded box that spans about 70–80% of the right column’s width. There is 24–32px of space above and below this message block, separating it from the applied filters summary and pagination (if pagination remains visible) to draw attention without feeling overly dramatic. The left column filters remain unchanged, reinforcing that users can adjust filters to find results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static; it appears only when there are zero results and occupies the same vertical slot as the opportunity-list, scrolling normally within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears in the right column whenever results are being fetched or updated. It is centered horizontally within the right column and positioned in the upper part of the results area, shortly below the applied-filters-summary, roughly where the first opportunity-card would appear. The indicator might be a simple spinner accompanied by a short line of text like 'Loading opportunities...', set in small serif or sans-serif text consistent with the rest of the card. While active, it either temporarily replaces the opportunity-list or overlays a subtle semi-transparent layer over the list area, but within the boundaries of the right column, not covering the left column filters. Spacing around it mirrors that of an opportunity-card, with 24px or so above and below, so it feels integrated into the existing structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional; it appears in-place within the right column during loading, then disappears once results are ready. It may sit above or temporarily hide the opportunity-list but does not change the overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-message displays in the right column when there is a problem retrieving or displaying opportunities. It is positioned where the opportunity-list would typically begin, just below the applied-filters-summary, and spans roughly 80–90% of the right column’s width. The message is contained in a modestly emphasized box with a soft tinted background and possibly a thin border, using slightly bolder text to communicate the issue while staying within the conservative, nonprofit aesthetic. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing separating this box from adjacent elements above and below. The left column filters remain present and unchanged, encouraging users to retry or adjust filters; any retry action (if present) would appear as a small button aligned to the right inside this error box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static; it appears in place of the opportunity-list when an error occurs and scrolls with the page content, without overlaying the header or left column.

name: Volunteer Opportunity Detail
title: Volunteer Opportunity Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific volunteer opportunity and provides an on-page form to submit a volunteer application for that role.

core_features: Read the volunteer opportunity ID from the URL (id) and load full opportunity details using getVolunteerOpportunityDetails.
Display the opportunity’s title, summary, role focus, serve type, age group served, and background check requirement based on the VolunteerOpportunity data.
Show campus/location information using CampusSite and/or location_description from the opportunity.
List available recurring volunteer time slots for this opportunity using VolunteerTimeSlot data.
Provide a prominent "Sign Up to Volunteer" call-to-action that scrolls to or focuses the application form.
Render an application form that collects full name, email, phone, and availability information, including selection of a time slot and optional availability notes.
On form submission, call submitVolunteerApplication with the selected opportunity, campus, and time slot, then display a confirmation or error message.
Offer navigation back to the volunteer opportunities listing and an option to return home after application submission.
user_workflows:
View opportunity details and decide to volunteer:
1. User arrives on volunteer_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to a VolunteerOpportunity ID.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the id parameter.
3. The page calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with volunteerOpportunityId = id.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown in place of the details and form.
5. On success, the page displays the opportunity title, summary, role focus, serve type, age group served, and whether a background check is required.
6. The page also shows associated campus/location information and the list of available time slots.
7. User reviews details and, if interested, clicks the "Sign Up to Volunteer" button.
8. The page scrolls to or focuses the application form section, ready for input.

Submit a volunteer application from the detail page:
1. With the application form visible, the user fills in full name, email address, and phone number fields.
2. The form pre-associates the opportunity ID from the current page (id). If a campus is associated with the opportunity, the corresponding campusSiteId is stored in a hidden or read-only field for submission.
3. The user selects one available time slot from the time_slots list, if any are returned. If no predefined time slots are available, the user can enter availability notes only.
4. The user can optionally add availability notes (e.g., constraints or preferences) in a free-text field.
5. The user submits the form, triggering validation (required fields, valid email format, selected slot if required).
6. On passing validation, the page calls submitVolunteerApplication with volunteerOpportunityId (from id), campusSiteId (from loaded campus_site if present), selectedTimeSlotId (from the selected time slot or null/undefined if none selected or none available), full_name, email, phone, and availability_notes.
7. While the request is processing, the form shows a submitting state (e.g., disabled submit button and status message).
