component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces all other content within the main card’s content area while loading. The loader stays centered within the card and does not scroll independently; the entire card can still scroll if the viewport is shorter than the card height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child profile header defines the upper two-column band of the white content card. Starting roughly 8–16px below the back link and spanning the full internal width of the card, it is split into two columns: the left column occupies about 40–45% of the card width and is dedicated to the child’s main portrait image, while the right column occupies 55–60% and holds key textual details. The image in the left column is centered within its column, with 16–24px padding on all sides, a thin border, and a subtle drop shadow that echoes the skeuomorphic style. Directly below the image, still within the left column’s width, a single-line caption shows the child’s name or a short identifier, centered and separated from the photo by about 8px. In the right column, aligned to the top edge of the photo, the child’s name appears as the largest text in this area, followed by their age, location, and any short tagline or basic stats. These text elements are left-aligned and stacked with tight vertical spacing (8–12px) to maintain a moderately dense feel. Below these basics, there is a reserved strip where the sponsorship availability banner will sit, creating a visually cohesive header block on the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top section of the card. It anchors the two-column grid that other components (like story text and availability banner) align to. Does not scroll independently from the page; it moves with the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child’s story and interests text sits directly beneath the primary header text in the right column, continuing the same 55–60% width column established by the child-profile-header. There is a modest gap (about 12–16px) below the availability banner and any header metadata before the story begins. The story appears as a block of small serif text, left-aligned with fairly tight line spacing, and arranged in several paragraphs with about 8–12px between paragraphs to keep the content moderately dense but readable. Visually, this text block occupies most of the vertical space to the right of the child’s photo, creating the classic mockup pattern of image on the left, text paragraphs on the right. The left column (photo and caption) and right column (story) remain aligned at the top, with the story often extending lower while leaving a clear gutter of about 16–24px between columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as part of the right column inside the main card. It scrolls with the page and expands vertically as needed, pushing the sponsorship form content downward while always maintaining its right-column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship availability banner sits in the upper portion of the right column, nested within the child-profile-header region. It appears just below the child’s name and basic details, separated by about 8–12px, and spans roughly 80–90% of the right column’s width, left-aligned with the text above it. The banner is a low-profile horizontal strip with a gentle gradient fill and lightly beveled edges, echoing the navigation buttons’ skeuomorphic style. Its height is modest (around 32–40px), making it noticeable but not overpowering. Text inside the banner is centered both vertically and horizontally within the strip, using a slightly larger or bolder weight than the body text. The color and iconography may vary by status (e.g., warm green for ‘Available’, muted gray for ‘Already Sponsored’), but it always sits clearly in the eye path between the child’s name and the story text, anchoring the call-to-action context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position within the right column’s header stack; it does not float or stick. When not applicable (e.g., unavailable), the banner either changes style or the reserved space collapses, allowing the story text to shift upward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the lower sponsorship area of the card, the amount selector is positioned near the top of the form, aligned with the left side of the form panel. The sponsorship-form area starts after a clear vertical break (approximately 32–40px) below the bottom of the two-column profile band. Inside this form region, the amount selector sits in a two-column row alongside the start date picker: it occupies the left half of this row (about 48–50% of the form width) with a horizontal gap of around 16–24px to the right column. The label for the field is left-aligned above the control, with about 4–8px separation, and the control itself (dropdown, segmented options, or input) uses beveled edges and a slight gradient, consistent with the site’s button style. The control width fills most of its column, leaving about 8–12px inner margin from the form panel’s edges. This placement ensures the amount choice is one of the first interactive elements encountered after reading about the child.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form’s top row. On narrower viewports, it can stack above the start date picker while still remaining in the top portion of the form, but in the default fixed-width layout it maintains a side-by-side alignment with the date picker.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The start date picker is aligned horizontally with the sponsorship-amount-selector as part of the form’s first row. It occupies the right half of this row (about 48–50% of the form width), with a consistent gap (16–24px) separating it from the amount selector on the left. The field label sits above the date input, left-aligned, with 4–8px of vertical spacing. The input control itself is a traditional date field or a dropdown-like control with subtle beveling, matching the older-style aesthetic. Its width fills most of the right column, mirroring the amount selector’s width on the left. This alignment creates a balanced top row of form controls, guiding the user to set both how much and when their sponsorship begins before moving down to payment and contact details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form’s top row. It aligns to the right of the amount selector and moves downward together with it if the form content grows. On very constrained widths, it can break to a second line beneath the amount selector while still remaining near the top of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector appears directly beneath the top row of amount and start date fields, with approximately 24px of vertical spacing separating it from the row above. It is aligned to the left edge of the form panel and spans about 60–70% of the form width, leaving some whitespace to the right to keep the layout from feeling cramped. A label such as ‘Payment Method’ appears at the top, left-aligned, and the radio options are stacked vertically, each with about 8–12px of vertical spacing. Each option’s label is relatively text-heavy (e.g., ‘Mail-in check’ or ‘Bank draft’ with explanatory copy), so the radio buttons are placed on the far left with labels wrapping to the right within the same width, respecting the text-focused design. The group’s positioning just below the initial selectors makes it easy to understand how the sponsorship will be fulfilled before entering personal details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and left-aligned within the form area. It occupies a single block of vertical space; additional options simply extend the block downward. It pushes subsequent form sections downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsor contact fields form section sits below the payment-method-selector, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. This section spans nearly the full width of the form panel (around 80–90% of the card width, centered), but internally uses a simple two-column layout where appropriate to reflect the overall grid style. For example, in the top row of this section, ‘First Name’ and ‘Last Name’ fields are positioned side by side, each occupying about 48–50% of the section width with a 16–24px gap between them. Below, wider fields like email address or mailing address may span the full section width, while shorter fields (such as city and postal code) share rows in similar two-column pairs. Labels are left-aligned above each input, with 4–8px spacing, and inputs use beveled, slightly inset styles consistent with the older visual language. Vertical spacing between rows of fields is about 12–16px, producing a moderately dense but structured block that fills much of the lower card without feeling chaotic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the form. It expands vertically based on the number of fields and pushes the submit button downward. The internal two-column pairs remain aligned to the same left and right edges, reinforcing the fixed-width grid feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship form is the main interactive block occupying the lower half of the content card. It begins after a clear visual break (extra whitespace or a thin horizontal rule) roughly 32–40px below the end of the profile/story section. The form itself is contained within a slightly tinted or subtly bordered panel inside the card, centered and spanning about 80–85% of the card’s width, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. At the very top of this panel is reserved space for the sponsorship-form-error-message, followed by a heading like ‘Sponsor This Child’, then the stacked form content: first the two-column row containing the sponsorship-amount-selector and sponsorship-start-date-picker, then the payment-method-selector block, followed by the sponsor-contact-fields. At the bottom center of the form panel, after about 24–32px of vertical spacing from the last field, sits a large, beveled submit button (e.g., ‘Confirm Sponsorship’), roughly 200–260px wide, centered horizontally. The entire form feels like a cohesive sub-card within the main card, with enough padding and spacing to separate it visually from the child profile above while still reading as part of the same page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that holds all sponsorship-related input components. It grows vertically with content, pushing the lower edge of the main card downward. It does not scroll independently; scrolling is handled at the page level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship confirmation panel occupies the same spatial region as the sponsorship-form within the lower half of the card. Upon successful submission, this panel either replaces or visually overlays the form area, while keeping the child profile section above intact. It spans about 80–85% of the card width, centered, with 24–32px of internal padding, similar to the form. The panel has a softly tinted success background (e.g., pale green) with a subtle border and beveled edges, aligning with the site’s skeuomorphic style. At the top, a bold confirmation heading is centered, followed by one or two short paragraphs of text describing the next steps. Optionally, a modest icon (such as a checkmark) appears to the left of the heading or above it. Below the message, near the bottom center of the panel, a smaller, beveled button or text-link may offer navigation back to all children or to additional resources. The overall effect is that the lower half of the card becomes a calm, self-contained confirmation block while the upper profile content remains visible as context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or hides the sponsorship-form within the same space. It remains static within the card and scrolls with the page. It does not overlay the upper profile section; instead, it occupies only the form area below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-all-children-link is a small, understated navigational element positioned at the very top-left inside the white content card. It sits flush with the card’s internal left padding (24–32px from the card’s outer edge) and about 8–12px below the top internal padding, making it the first element within the card’s content flow. The link is text-based, possibly with a small left-pointing arrow icon, and is aligned along a single line. It does not span the width of the card; instead, it occupies only enough horizontal space for the link text, leaving clear whitespace to its right. Below this link, separated by about 8–12px, the child-profile-header begins. This placement gently reinforces the page hierarchy without visually competing with the main profile content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the top-left of the card’s content area. It scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down; it is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If the child profile fails to load, the child-load-error-message appears prominently within the main card, in the area where the profile header and story would normally be. It is placed below the back-to-all-children-link, with about 16–24px of spacing, and spans nearly the full internal width of the card (around 90–95% of the card width, centered). The message is presented in a colored alert box with a subtle bevel and gradient, typically in a warm red or amber tone, with 16–24px of internal padding. Text is left-aligned and may include a brief explanation and instructions or a link to return to the list of children. No profile image, story, or form content is shown beneath this message, so the card appears fairly shallow with the error message as its main content, emphasizing the error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the profile and form content region when present. The error box is static and occupies the upper central part of the card, pushing the card’s bottom edge down only as needed for its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-form-error-message appears at the top of the sponsorship-form panel, just inside its tinted or bordered container. Within the form’s 80–85% width central panel, the error message spans the full width of the panel, with about 16–24px of internal padding. It sits directly below the form’s title (if present) or directly at the top if no title is used, separated from the next form fields by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. The error box uses a warm red or amber tint with beveled edges and possibly a small warning icon on the left, aligning with the site’s traditional alert styling. The message text wraps across the panel width and remains left-aligned, making validation or submission issues very visible before the user returns to the fields below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a static block at the top of the form panel. It pushes the form fields downward when present and collapses out of the layout when not needed. It scrolls together with the form and does not float over other content.

name: Parent Newsletter Signup
title: Parent Newsletter Signup
description: Page where parents can subscribe to the ministry’s email newsletter, choose age-specific content for their children, select topics of interest, and set communication preferences.

core_features: Load available newsletter options (topics, email frequencies, communication preferences) from getNewsletterOptions and populate the corresponding form controls.
Optionally pre-fill the parent’s email address from the incoming email URL parameter when present.
Optionally pre-select a range of children’s ages for age-specific ideas based on minAge and maxAge URL parameters when present.
Allow parents to enter or confirm their email address, select one or more child ages, choose newsletter topics, select an email frequency, and set communication preferences.
Allow parents to enable or disable receiving age-specific ideas; when enabled, ensure selected child ages are included in the subscription payload.
Validate the form (required fields, basic format checks) before allowing submission.
On submit, call createNewsletterSubscription with the collected form data (email, child_ages, topics, email_frequency, communication_preference, send_age_specific_ideas).
Handle createNewsletterSubscription responses by showing success confirmation with a summary of saved preferences or displaying error messages when subscription creation fails.
Provide a post-success navigation option to return to the home page (index.html) via a "Back to Home" action from the confirmation state.
user_workflows:
Workflow A – Direct signup via navigation:
1) Parent clicks a "Parent Newsletter" link in the site footer and lands on newsletter.html without URL parameters.
2) Page-init logic calls getNewsletterOptions and constructs an empty signup form.
3) Parent enters email, selects child ages, chooses topics, picks an email frequency, and sets a communication preference.
4) Parent submits the form, triggering createNewsletterSubscription with the entered values.
5) On success, the page displays a confirmation state summarizing the subscription; parent may click "Back to Home" to go to index.html.

Workflow B – Signup from homepage with pre-filled email:
1) Parent clicks a promo link from index.html that opens newsletter.html with an email query parameter.
2) Page-init logic reads the email parameter, calls getNewsletterOptions, and initializes the form with the email field pre-filled.
3) Parent reviews/edits the email, selects child ages and topics, picks an email frequency, and sets communication preferences.
4) Parent submits the form, triggering createNewsletterSubscription.
5) On success, confirmation state appears with saved preferences; parent may navigate away using "Back to Home".

Workflow C – Signup from an age-specific resource page:
1) Parent is browsing crafts_activities.html or lesson_plans.html and clicks a link like "Get more ideas like this by email", which opens newsletter.html with minAge and/or maxAge (and optionally email) as URL parameters.
2) Page-init logic reads email, minAge, and maxAge, calls getNewsletterOptions, then pre-fills the email field (if provided) and pre-selects child ages in the given range. The send_age_specific_ideas setting is initialized to true when a valid age range is provided.
3) Parent can adjust the age selections, toggle age-specific ideas on/off, choose topics, select frequency and communication preference, then submits the form.
4) createNewsletterSubscription is called with the collected data; on success, the confirmation state shows that age-specific ideas will be sent for the selected ages.
5) Parent can click "Back to Home" to return to index.html or close the page.

Workflow D – Error and retry:
1) Parent completes the form and submits; createNewsletterSubscription is called.
2) If the API returns an error or success=false, the page displays an inline error banner and keeps all form values intact.
3) Parent can correct any highlighted issues (e.g., invalid email format or missing required fields) and re-submit.
4) Once createNewsletterSubscription returns success=true, the page transitions to the confirmation state.
interactions: On page load, the page-init controller reads URL parameters (email, minAge, maxAge), triggers getNewsletterOptions, and initializes the form’s state based on both the URL parameters and API results.
Editing the email input updates the in-memory subscription draft email field used when calling createNewsletterSubscription.
Toggling the age-specific ideas control updates the send_age_specific_ideas value and affects validation rules (e.g., if enabled, at least one age must be selected).
Selecting or deselecting child ages in the age selector updates a list of child_ages that will be passed to createNewsletterSubscription.
Selecting or deselecting newsletter topic checkboxes updates a topics list that will be sent to createNewsletterSubscription.
Selecting an email frequency from the dropdown binds the chosen value to the email_frequency field for createNewsletterSubscription.
Selecting a communication preference from the available options binds the chosen value to the communication_preference field for createNewsletterSubscription.

Clicking the form submit button triggers front-end validation; if validation passes, it calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current form state and switches the UI into a submitting/loading state until the API response arrives.

When createNewsletterSubscription returns success=true, the UI switches from the editable form view to a read-only confirmation view using the returned subscription, child_age_records, and topic_preferences.
When createNewsletterSubscription returns an error or success=false, an error message is displayed and the form remains editable for correction and re-submission.
Clicking the "Back to Home" action in the confirmation view navigates the browser to index.html without additional query parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads the URL query parameters email, minAge, and maxAge (if present). The page enters a 'loading' state while getNewsletterOptions is called. Once options are loaded, the page transitions to a 'ready' state and initializes the form state: if email is present, it pre-fills the email field; if valid minAge and/or maxAge parameters are present, the age selector pre-selects all ages in that range and sets send_age_specific_ideas to true. If minAge/maxAge are absent or invalid, the age selector starts with no pre-selected ages and send_age_specific_ideas uses a neutral default. While the user edits the form, all changes are stored in in-memory state only and are not written to the URL parameters. When the user submits, the page moves to a 'submitting' state, disables duplicate submissions, and calls createNewsletterSubscription with current form state. If the API call succeeds, the page enters a 'confirmed' state, hides or locks the editable controls, and shows a confirmation summary based on the API response. If the call fails, the page returns to the 'ready' state with error messaging. Navigation away from this page (e.g., 'Back to Home') does not preserve form state in the URL; it simply loads index.html as specified.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load: parses URL parameters, retrieves newsletter options from getNewsletterOptions, and initializes the signup form state (email, age selections, age-specific toggle, and available options). Manages high-level page states such as loading, ready, submitting, and confirmed.

Main form that collects all data required for a new newsletter subscription: parent email, child ages, topic preferences, email frequency, communication preferences, and whether to send age-specific ideas. Handles validation and triggers submission.

Single email input field bound to the subscription email. Pre-filled from the email URL parameter when available and editable by the user. Validation ensures the field is not empty and follows a basic email format before submission.

Boolean control (e.g., checkbox or switch) that toggles whether the parent wants age-specific ideas. When enabled, at least one child age must be selected; when disabled, all age selections are ignored for createNewsletterSubscription, and send_age_specific_ideas is set accordingly.

Control for selecting one or more child ages. Ages may be pre-selected based on minAge and maxAge URL parameters. Updates the internal list of child_ages that will be passed to createNewsletterSubscription whenever the selection changes.

Multi-select control (e.g., checkboxes) populated dynamically using the topics array from getNewsletterOptions. Each option corresponds to a topic that can be included in the topics list passed to createNewsletterSubscription. The component ensures the selection state stays in sync with the underlying NewsletterTopicPreference data representation.

Dropdown or select input bound to email_frequency, populated using the email_frequencies array from getNewsletterOptions. The user’s selected frequency is required and is included in createNewsletterSubscription.

Selector for communication_preference, populated from the communication_preferences array returned by getNewsletterOptions. The selection is required and saved with the subscription via createNewsletterSubscription.

Aggregates and displays validation errors for the newsletter-signup-form (e.g., missing email, no frequency selected, no topics chosen, or no ages when age-specific ideas is enabled). Updates in real time as fields change or upon submission attempts.

Primary action for submitting the newsletter signup. When clicked, triggers validation in newsletter-signup-form and, if valid, calls createNewsletterSubscription with current form state and shows a loading state until the response is received.

Displays transient status information during and after submission, such as a loading indicator while createNewsletterSubscription is in progress, and any error message returned by the API if the submission fails.

Shown after createNewsletterSubscription returns success=true. Presents a read-only summary of the created subscription (including email, selected child ages from child_age_records, topics from topic_preferences, email frequency, and communication preference) using the API response. Also hosts a "Back to Home" navigation action.
Navigates the user to index.html after a successful signup when clicked from the confirmation state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header bar with logo and site title (inherited from the overall site), a single horizontal row of navigation buttons below it, and then a large full-width gradient band. Centered within this gradient band is one dominant white content card for the Parent Newsletter Signup. Inside this card, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left column is reserved for a large ministry-related photograph and a brief explanatory text (non-interactive, atmospheric content), while the right column holds the entire newsletter-signup-form and its related feedback and confirmation components, stacked vertically.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width header bar with logo and site title (inherited from the overall site), a single horizontal row of navigation buttons below it, and then a large full-width gradient band. Centered within this gradient band is one dominant white content card for the Parent Newsletter Signup. Inside this card, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left column is reserved for a large ministry-related photograph and a brief explanatory text (non-interactive, atmospheric content), while the right column holds the entire newsletter-signup-form and its related feedback and confirmation components, stacked vertically.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and navigation together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport and remain full-width. The gradient background band below them occupies the remainder of the visible viewport height and slightly beyond, providing a soft frame behind the main card. The centered white card spans about 960–1040px in width (approximately 70–80% of a common desktop viewport), with generous margins (40–60px) from the gradient background on all sides. Inside the card, the left column uses about 40–45% of the card width for the illustrative photo/content, and the right column uses about 55–60% for the form and related messaging. Vertical spacing between form fields is around 16–24px, and larger breaks of 24–32px separate major groups (e.g., between topic-preferences-selector and email-frequency-selector).
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density is moderately high, reflecting a traditional, text-focused nonprofit style. The form fields and labels are relatively compact but not cramped, with serif or transitional serif labels and body copy. Multiple controls (toggles, selectors, and checkboxes) appear in a single scroll viewport on desktop, giving a sense of substantial, information-rich interaction. Inside the card, padding of about 24–32px on each side keeps the dense form content comfortable, while the large left-column image and generous top/bottom margins around the card prevent the page from feeling overloaded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the header, travels across to the centered site title and navigation buttons, then moves diagonally down into the gradient band and lands first on the left-column image within the central card. From there, the viewer’s attention shifts horizontally to the right-column form—the newsletter-signup-form and its key email-input and subscription-submit-button. Within the form, the eye follows a straightforward top-to-bottom reading order: status/validation messages, email, age-specific toggle and child-age selector, topic preferences, frequency and communication preferences, concluding at the primary submit button. After submission, the submission-status-banner at the top of the card and the signup-confirmation-panel in the right column become the dominant focal points, still anchored by that same left-to-right flow.

layout.overall_description: The Parent Newsletter Signup page sits within a traditional, centered, fixed-width layout framed by a full-width header and navigation bar at the top. Below the header, a soft, warm gradient band fills the rest of the viewport, and in the middle of this band a single white, beveled card floats, clearly separated from the background by 40–60px of margin on all sides. The card itself is wide but not full-screen, with 24–32px of internal padding and a quiet two-column structure. On the left, a large, vertically centered photograph of children’s ministry life sits above a short, warmly written paragraph, forming a welcoming visual anchor. On the right, all interactive components of the Parent Newsletter Signup form are stacked in a tidy vertical flow: status and validation messages near the top, followed by the email field, age and topic preferences, frequency and communication options, with a prominent beveled submit button at the bottom right. When the user submits the form, a colored status banner appears at the top edge of the card, and a confirmation panel gracefully replaces the form content in the right column, with a clear link to return home. The entire composition feels earnest and brochure-like, with a clear Z-pattern path from header to image to form, stable fixed-width alignment, and a slightly skeuomorphic visual treatment that feels familiar and church-friendly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init-controller is non-visual and does not occupy explicit screen space. Conceptually, it is associated with the entire viewport and initializes state before any visible components are rendered within the fixed-width centered layout. In terms of layout, it sits behind the full header, navigation, gradient background band, and central content card, orchestrating which visible components (such as submission-status-banner or signup-confirmation-panel) are shown within the main card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/controller; no rendered position. It affects which visible components appear in the normal flow but does not itself participate in layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-form occupies the entire right column inside the central white content card. The card is centered on the page, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, and internally split into two columns, with the right column taking about 55–60% of the card’s width. The form is top-aligned with the left-column image so that its heading and first field begin near the top edge of the card’s inner padding. Within this right column, the form stretches from the top internal padding down toward the bottom, with 24–32px of breathing room between its lowest element (the subscription-submit-button) and the bottom edge of the card. The form is arranged in a simple vertical stack of fields and groups, following the page’s text-heavy, traditional style: headings and instructions appear at the top, followed by validation messages, then a series of controls. Horizontally, the form fields align flush-left with each other, preserving a clean column edge about 24–32px inside the right edge of the card. When the signup-confirmation-panel is active, it visually replaces the main body of the form in this same right-column area, effectively hiding the input controls while maintaining the overall column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in normal document flow within the right column of the fixed card. It may be conditionally hidden or replaced by the confirmation panel, but it does not move, float, or stick relative to scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email-input field appears near the top of the newsletter-signup-form’s right column, directly beneath any form heading and just below the form-validation-messages block (when present). It spans almost the full width of the right column, leaving a consistent 24–32px of padding from the card’s inner right edge and around 16–24px of space below its label. The input box itself is moderately tall with slightly beveled edges to match the skeuomorphic style, and there is approximately 16px of vertical space separating it from the next control (the age-specific-toggle). This placement ensures that when a parent’s eye shifts from the left-column image to the right column, the first specific action they see is the clear, wide email field near the top center-right of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the form’s vertical flow, aligned to the left within the right column and expanding responsively only within the constrained fixed card width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The age-specific-toggle is positioned directly beneath the email-input within the right column of the card, with about 16–20px of vertical spacing. It uses the same left-aligned label as the other controls, starting at the left edge of the right column content. The toggle options (e.g., a pair of radio buttons or a segmented toggle bar) are arranged horizontally but kept within the width of the right column, with 8–12px of internal padding around each option to echo the mockup’s navigation button styling. Visually, it forms a compact horizontal band about one-third the height of the email input, creating a clear yet unobtrusive step before the more detailed child-age-selector below. There is another 16–20px gap below the control before the child-age-selector group begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in normal vertical flow; its state influences whether the child-age-selector group below appears active or disabled, but the toggle’s own spatial position stays fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-age-selector is placed directly under the age-specific-toggle, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the right column content area but organizes its inner controls in a slightly denser grid, reflecting the information-rich style. For example, age ranges or individual ages might appear as checkboxes laid out in two columns within this group, each column occupying about half of the available width with 8–16px gaps between rows. The entire selector block is visually grouped with a subtle boundary—such as a light box outline or a slightly tinted background—making it about as tall as one to two text paragraphs. This group sits approximately in the upper-middle portion of the right column, clearly associated with the age-specific-toggle above and with about 20–24px of space separating it from the topic-preferences-selector that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level group within the form’s vertical stack. It may appear visually de-emphasized or disabled if the age-specific-toggle is off, but it retains its spatial footprint to keep the layout stable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-preferences-selector appears in the midsection of the right column, following the child-age-selector after about 20–24px of vertical space. It spans nearly the full width of the right column, with its label left-aligned and a list of checkbox options or toggle buttons arranged in one or two columns depending on space. In keeping with the moderately dense brochure feel, individual topics are stacked vertically with around 8–12px between items, resulting in a block that might occupy one-quarter to one-third of the card’s height when fully expanded. The group’s bottom edge sits roughly at the visual midpoint of the card’s vertical height, with a larger spacing of about 24–28px before the email-frequency-selector below, marking it as the end of the main content preference section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically flowing group. It allows internal wrapping of options while maintaining the left and right padding of the form’s column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email-frequency-selector is positioned beneath the topic-preferences-selector, separated by a slightly larger gap of about 24–28px to visually indicate a new type of decision (schedule rather than content). It aligns with the left edge of the right column and uses either a horizontal row of radio buttons or a compact vertical list, staying within about half the width of the right column so it doesn’t feel as tall as the topic preferences above. Its overall height is modest—roughly the height of a label plus one or two lines of controls—leaving enough room below for the communication-preferences-selector and the submit button without making the vertical stack feel crowded near the bottom of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow control that maintains its position relative to the surrounding selectors; no special spatial behavior beyond simple stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The communication-preferences-selector sits just above the subscription-submit-button, with roughly 16–20px of vertical spacing from the email-frequency-selector. It spans the width of the right column and is slightly shorter than the topic-preferences-selector, containing options like email-only, SMS, or printed materials as a compact list of checkboxes or toggles. Its layout is primarily vertical: label at the left, followed by one option per line, each line using a small left indent to keep the list visually grouped. The bottom of this selector leaves a 16–24px gap before the submit button, anchoring the last set of preferences directly above the primary call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A simple, block-level group in the form’s flow; it expands or contracts slightly depending on the number of options, but its left alignment and top/bottom spacing remain consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-validation-messages block is positioned near the top of the newsletter-signup-form, immediately under the form’s title or introductory copy and directly above the email-input. It spans the full available width of the right column and appears as a horizontally centered, tinted box (for example, a light red or yellow background) with a thin border, about as tall as one to three lines of text. There is approximately 8–12px of vertical padding inside this block and about 12–16px of space separating it from the bottom of the form’s introductory text and from the top of the email-input. When no validation errors are present, this area collapses entirely, allowing the email-input to slide up and align more closely under the intro text, keeping the card looking clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block within the normal flow; it pushes subsequent fields downward when visible but does not overlay or float over other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription-submit-button is located at the bottom of the newsletter-signup-form in the right column, serving as the form’s visual endpoint. It sits roughly 16–24px below the communication-preferences-selector and is horizontally aligned toward the right side of the right column—either right-aligned with the column’s inner edge or slightly inset so that it appears anchored to the lower-right corner of the card’s content area. The button is large and beveled, echoing the navigation buttons in the header: about 40–48px tall, with 10–12px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding. It occupies roughly 20–25% of the right column’s width, leaving whitespace to its left. There is about 24–32px of vertical space beneath the button before the card’s bottom padding, allowing it to feel prominent without touching the edge of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element. It remains fixed at the bottom of the form’s content; on successful submission, the form (including this button) can be replaced by the signup-confirmation-panel, but the button itself does not move independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner is positioned at the very top of the white content card, spanning the full width of the card from left to right, just inside the card’s top padding. It appears as a narrow horizontal strip—approximately 40–60px tall—with a colored background (such as green for success or red for failure) and centered text. The banner sits above both the left-column image and the right-column form, forming a unified status bar that the eye encounters immediately after glancing down from the site navigation. There is a small gap of about 8–12px between the bottom edge of the banner and the main top content of the card (the image on the left and the form heading on the right). When not active, this banner area collapses, allowing the card content to shift upward and reclaim the vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered at the top of the card, within the normal flow of the central content. It does not stick to the viewport; it scrolls away with the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup-confirmation-panel appears in the same spatial region as the main body of the newsletter-signup-form’s right column once the user has successfully submitted the form. It is centered within the right column, maintaining the column’s width (approximately 55–60% of the card’s total width) but using more vertical padding—around 24–32px top and bottom—to feel like a distinct, self-contained message area. The panel may have a soft tinted background (light green or blue) and a subtle border to differentiate it from the underlying white card. Its confirmation text is center-aligned within this area, with the back-to-home-link placed just beneath the primary message. Above this panel, the left-column image remains unchanged, preserving the overall two-column card structure and the Z-pattern flow, but the interactive fields and submit button are no longer visible, replaced by this simpler, message-focused block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the visible portion of the form within the right column in normal flow. It does not overlay the entire card; instead, it occupies the same vertical space the form previously used.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is located within the signup-confirmation-panel, just below the main confirmation message text. It is horizontally centered in the panel, appearing as a traditional underlined text link or a small, low-emphasis button using the site’s accent color. There is approximately 12–16px of vertical space between the confirmation message and this link, and another 16–24px between the link and the bottom edge of the confirmation panel. When viewed in the context of the whole card, the link sits in the lower-mid area of the right column, visually aligned with the mid-to-lower region of the left-column image, providing a gentle exit path without overpowering the confirmation message itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the confirmation panel’s vertical flow, appearing only when the panel is visible. It does not float or stick; its position is fixed relative to the confirmation text above.

name: About / Contact / Policies
title: About / Contact / Policies
description: Combined informational page that shows the ministry’s organization details, provides contact options, and summarizes website policies, with content loaded dynamically from APIs and section focus controlled by URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display organization overview content (mission, vision, beliefs, focus areas, and high-level contact details) using the getOrganizationOverview interface.
Provide an About section that uses the organization overview data to explain what the ministry does, including clear links to giving and serving pages.
Provide a Contact section with both basic contact information (from getOrganizationOverview.contact_highlight) and a general inquiry contact form that submits via submitContactForm.
Pre-select the contact form reason based on the contact_reason URL parameter, so users coming from other pages land on a tailored contact form.
Provide a Policies section that lists key website policies (privacy, data use, terms of use, etc.) using getPolicySummaries, including titles, summaries, and last updated dates.
Use the section URL parameter to control which of the three sections (about, contact, policies) is active on initial load and when switching between tabs.
Offer navigation actions to donate.html (support/giving), volunteer_list.html (serve/volunteer), and index.html (back to home).
user_workflows:
Read About the Ministry:
1. User navigates to about.html (without specifying section) or with section=about.
2. The page router reads the section parameter and sets the active section to About.
3. The organization overview loader component calls getOrganizationOverview.
4. Once data is returned, the About content section displays the mission statement, vision statement, beliefs summary, and focus areas from the API response.
5. The user can click support calls-to-action within the About section to navigate to donate.html or volunteer_list.html.
6. The user can click the Back to Home link to go to index.html, preserving no additional state in the URL.

Submit a General Contact Inquiry:
1. User navigates to about.html?section=contact or clicks a Contact link in the footer that sets section=contact.
2. The router detects section=contact and activates the Contact tab.
3. The organization overview loader provides contact_highlight data to the Contact info panel, which displays the current email, phone, and mailing address supplied by the API.
4. The contact form component initializes its fields. If a contact_reason URL parameter is present (for example, when coming from index.html), that value is applied to the contact_reason dropdown or selection control within the form.
5. User enters name, email, subject, and message; optionally adjusts the contact_reason selection.
6. On form submission, the contact form component validates the fields on the client (e.g., required fields, simple email format) and then calls submitContactForm with { name, email, subject, message, contact_reason }.
7. The component receives the response from submitContactForm and shows a success or error state based on the success flag and message field, without hardcoding backend messages.
8. On success, the form can optionally reset its input fields while keeping the selected contact_reason, or leave values in place depending on chosen UX behavior.
9. The user can switch to About or Policies tabs, which updates the section parameter in the URL accordingly.

Review Website Policies:
1. User clicks a footer link such as Privacy & Terms that routes to about.html?section=policies.
2. The router reads section=policies and activates the Policies tab.
3. The policies loader component calls getPolicySummaries.
4. When the response returns, the Policies list component iterates over the policies array and displays each policy’s title, summary, and last_updated fields.
5. If configured by the broader application, each policy row may optionally include a link or action (for example, to a dedicated policy details page) derived from the policy_key, but this component itself only depends on the provided API fields.
6. The user may switch tabs to About or Contact, updating the section URL parameter, or click Back to Home to navigate to index.html.
interactions: Tab Switching: Clicking an About, Contact, or Policies tab sets that section as active and updates the URL section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=contact).

Initial Section Focus: On page load, a router component reads the section parameter and automatically scrolls/focuses to and activates the specified section; if section is missing or unrecognized, it defaults to About.

Contact Reason Pre-selection: When the page is loaded with a contact_reason parameter (e.g., about.html?section=contact&contact_reason=donations), the contact form initializes its contact_reason field with that value.

Contact Form Submission: When the user submits the contact form, the submitContactForm interface is invoked with user-entered values; the UI then shows success or error feedback based on the API response.

Support CTAs: Clicking a Give Now or similar support call-to-action in the About section navigates to donate.html. Clicking a Serve with us or similar volunteering call-to-action navigates to volunteer_list.html.
Back to Home Navigation: Clicking a Back to Home link triggers navigation to index.html (with no additional parameters).

Policy Item Interaction: Users can click on a policy item to expand/collapse or to navigate to a more detailed view if such a route is configured elsewhere; within this page the component at least displays summary text and last updated date for each policy returned by the API.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the primary source of state for which section is active and how the contact form is initialized. On every load, the page inspects window.location.search for the section and contact_reason parameters. If section is 'about', 'contact', or 'policies', the router activates the matching tab/section; otherwise it defaults to 'about'. When a user clicks a tab, the router updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., using pushState or replaceState) without reloading the page, so the active state can be bookmarked or shared. The contact_reason parameter is read only when initializing the contact form: if present and valid, it sets the initial value of the contact_reason field; if missing or invalid, the field starts with an unselected or default state. When a user manually changes the contact_reason field, the component may optionally update the contact_reason parameter in the URL to reflect the current selection, without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence. The data content for About and Contact sections relies on the getOrganizationOverview response, which is fetched on load (and whenever a forced reload is requested), while the Policies section relies on the getPolicySummaries response. These API responses are cached in memory for the life of the page session rather than being encoded into URL parameters. Error and loading states are managed per-section (e.g., one for organization overview, one for policy summaries) based on the success or failure of these API calls.

components.functionality: Controls which section (About, Contact, or Policies) is active based on the section URL parameter, and coordinates tab activation and scroll/focus behavior.
event_handlers: onLoad: Reads the section parameter from the URL and sets an internal activeSection state to 'about', 'contact', or 'policies' (defaulting to 'about' if missing or invalid).
onTabClick(targetSection): Updates activeSection to targetSection and updates the URL query string section parameter accordingly, without reloading the page.
onUrlChange: Listens to browser history changes (back/forward) and updates activeSection when section changes in the URL, ensuring tabs stay in sync with navigation.

components.functionality: Fetches organization overview information and exposes mission, vision, beliefs summary, focus areas, and contact highlight data to any section that needs it.
event_handlers: onInit: Calls getOrganizationOverview with no parameters as soon as the page is ready or the About/Contact sections need data.
onSuccess(response): Stores the mission_statement, vision_statement, beliefs_summary, focus_areas array, and contact_highlight object into an in-memory state that can be read by child components.
onError(error): Sets an error state that dependent components can use to show a retry option or an appropriate error message; may expose a retry function.
onRetryClick: Re-invokes getOrganizationOverview if the initial request failed.

components.functionality: Displays the organization’s mission, vision, beliefs summary, and focus areas using data provided by the organization-overview-loader, and includes navigation actions to support and volunteer pages.
event_handlers: onRender: Shows loading state until organization-overview-loader has successfully fetched data; then renders text content and repeats over the focus_areas array to output each title and description.
onClickGiveNow: Navigates the browser to donate.html when the user activates the support/giving call-to-action.
onClickServeWithUs: Navigates the browser to volunteer_list.html when the user activates the volunteering call-to-action.

components.functionality: Shows high-level contact information (email, phone, mailing address) provided by the organization overview API for quick reference.
event_handlers: onRender: Displays loading or error states based on organization-overview-loader status and, when data is available, renders the contact_highlight fields.
onClickEmailLink: Opens the user's default mail client using the email value from contact_highlight (if provided).
onClickPhoneLink: Initiates a tel: link using the phone value from contact_highlight (if provided).

components.functionality: Provides a general inquiry form where users can submit questions or requests to the ministry, sending data to the backend via submitContactForm, and uses the contact_reason URL parameter to pre-select a reason when applicable.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the contact_reason parameter from the URL when section is 'contact' and sets the initial value of the contact_reason field if the parameter is present.
onFieldChange(fieldName, value): Updates the internal form state for name, email, subject, message, or contact_reason as the user types or selects.
onContactReasonChange(newReason): Updates the contact_reason field and optionally synchronizes the URL's contact_reason parameter using history APIs.
onValidate: Checks that required fields (name, email, subject, message) are filled and that email meets a basic format check before submission.
onSubmit: If validation passes, calls submitContactForm with the current form state { name, email, subject, message, contact_reason }.
onSubmitSuccess(response): Uses response.success and response.message to display a success state via the notification-banner component; may clear or retain form fields depending on UX decisions.
onSubmitError(responseOrError): Displays an error state via notification-banner using response.message (if available) or a generic client-side error message.

components.functionality: Fetches policy summaries for privacy, data use, terms of use, and related policies via getPolicySummaries.
event_handlers: onInit: Calls getPolicySummaries when the Policies section is first activated or when the page loads, depending on performance strategy.
onSuccess(response): Stores the policies array from the response in an internal state, making each policy object (policy_key, title, summary, last_updated) available to child components.
onError(error): Sets an error state used to show an appropriate message and optional retry control.
onRetryClick: Re-invokes getPolicySummaries if the initial request fails.

components.functionality: Displays a list of policy summaries, including titles and last updated dates, using data provided by policies-loader.
event_handlers: onRender: Shows loading or error states based on policies-loader; when data is available, iterates over the policies array to render each policy entry.

onPolicyClick(policy): Optionally expands or collapses a given policy’s summary text for better readability; may also emit an event to navigate to a full policy page if such routing is configured elsewhere using the policy_key.
onRetryClick: Proxies a retry action to policies-loader.onRetryClick when users attempt to reload policy data after an error.

components.functionality: Provides the three main tabs (About, Contact, Policies) and keeps the visual selection aligned with the section URL parameter and router state.
event_handlers: onTabClickAbout: Calls page-router.onTabClick('about') and updates the selected tab styling.
onTabClickContact: Calls page-router.onTabClick('contact') and updates the selected tab styling.
onTabClickPolicies: Calls page-router.onTabClick('policies') and updates the selected tab styling.
onRouterUpdate(newSection): Updates the active tab highlight when the router changes activeSection (for example, from back/forward browser navigation).

components.functionality: Displays transient success or error messages, particularly for contact form submission results and API load errors.
event_handlers: onShow(message, statusType): Renders a message with a status type (e.g., success, error, info) passed from other components.
onDismissClick: Hides the currently displayed message and clears any associated status so it does not persist across interactions.
onApiErrorChange: Listens for error state changes from data loaders and shows appropriate messages when they appear.

components.functionality: Provides a simple navigation control that returns users to the site’s home page (index.html) from any section.
event_handlers: onClickNavigateHome: Navigates the browser to index.html when activated; does not add or rely on URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width centered grid, likely based on a 960–1100px content width with two-column splits inside sections rather than a modern 12-column responsive grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Content is arranged in a classic, linear top-to-bottom flow inside a single prominent white content card that sits centered on a full-width gradient background. Above the card, the site’s global header and primary navigation (defined elsewhere) occupy the top band. Inside the card, a small back-to-home link appears at the top-left, followed by a centered row of large skeuomorphic-style tabs for About, Contact, and Policies. Below the tabs, a slim notification banner area is reserved, and beneath that the router-controlled main content area switches between three sections: an About overview, a Contact layout (info + form), and a Policies list. Each section respects the mockup’s preference for simple two-column splits where appropriate, with text-heavy blocks on the right or center.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Content is arranged in a classic, linear top-to-bottom flow inside a single prominent white content card that sits centered on a full-width gradient background. Above the card, the site’s global header and primary navigation (defined elsewhere) occupy the top band. Inside the card, a small back-to-home link appears at the top-left, followed by a centered row of large skeuomorphic-style tabs for About, Contact, and Policies. Below the tabs, a slim notification banner area is reserved, and beneath that the router-controlled main content area switches between three sections: an About overview, a Contact layout (info + form), and a Policies list. Each section respects the mockup’s preference for simple two-column splits where appropriate, with text-heavy blocks on the right or center.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main gradient band fills the viewport beneath the header, with a centered fixed-width content card (roughly 960–1100px wide) occupying about 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens. The card has generous outer margins—around 40–60px from the gradient at top/bottom and sides—making it the focal element. Inside the card, 24–32px of padding on all sides keeps content comfortably inset. The tab navigation consumes roughly the top 15% of the card’s height, the notification banner a small 5–10% slice when present, and the remaining 70–80% is dedicated to the router-switched main content. Within those main sections, the two-column layouts assign roughly 40–45% width to supporting or secondary content on the left (like contact info) and 55–60% to primary reading or input areas on the right (like about text or the contact form).
layout_strategies.content_density: The card is moderately dense and text-focused, echoing a brochure-style nonprofit site. Body copy is set in a relatively small serif type with comfortable but not airy line spacing. Paragraphs are grouped into substantial blocks, with 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs and 24–32px between distinct subsections. Navigation tabs and the back-to-home link are compact but clearly tappable, with about 8–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding. The contact form fields and policy list items are closely stacked but separated by consistent 8–16px gaps for readability. Overall, the page feels information-rich without being cramped, due to consistent padding and clear card boundaries.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern within the card. The eye first catches the back-to-home link in the top-left corner of the card, then moves horizontally across to the centered section tab navigation, which feels like the main control surface. From there, attention drops diagonally down into the main content area, starting on the left side (organization info or contact details) and then moving rightwards to read primary text content or complete the contact form. The gradient background outside the card subtly fades from lighter on the left to slightly darker on the right, reinforcing left-to-right reading and helping the right-hand content column feel substantiated. Section transitions are controlled by the tabs and router but always maintain this left-to-right reading priority.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header and primary navigation, the user sees a warm, soft gradient band spanning the entire browser width, with a single white, beveled content card centered within it. The card is fixed in width, with generous margins around it so it appears as a prominent panel floating above the background. Inside the card, a small, text-only “Back to Home” link sits in the top-left corner, followed by a centered row of large, pill-like section tabs for About, Contact, and Policies that echo early-2010s skeuomorphic button styles. Directly beneath the tabs, a narrow horizontal strip is reserved for status notifications. The majority of the card is occupied by the router-controlled section area, which swaps between three layouts without changing the overall frame of the card. The About view presents a more text-centric two-column layout with organization details; the Contact view divides space between left-aligned contact information and a larger right-hand contact form; and the Policies view stacks policy summaries in a single, text-dense column. Throughout, spacing is consistent and moderately tight, emphasizing information delivery within a traditional, church-friendly aesthetic while the gradient background and strong card edges maintain a clear focal hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page router is not directly visible, but it governs the main content area occupying the central lower portion of the white card. Horizontally, this router-controlled zone spans nearly the full inner width of the card, leaving standard card padding (about 24–32px) on left and right. Vertically, it begins just below the notification-banner area and extends down to the bottom of the card, with about 24–32px padding at the bottom before the card’s edge. Within this zone, the router ensures that exactly one of the three primary views (About, Contact, Policies) is visible at a time. Each view fills the same rectangle, so switching tabs feels like swapping the content inside a framed window rather than changing the page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The router’s content follows normal document flow within the card. It does not scroll independently; instead, the entire page scrolls if the routed content exceeds viewport height. The router area maintains a consistent minimum height to prevent the card from collapsing visually when content is short, preserving the strong card silhouette against the gradient background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The organization-overview-loader is an invisible data layer that occupies the same space as the upper portion of the About section’s content. Visually, its presence is implied by a potential loading state: while data is being fetched, the top of the About content area (inside the router region) can show a simple text or subtle spinner centered horizontally within the left portion of the two-column area. This loader’s area sits approximately 24px below the notification-banner, within the main content rectangle, ensuring that any temporary loading message lines up with where the organization overview text will appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader does not permanently claim space; once data is loaded, its visual footprint is replaced entirely by the About-section content. During loading, it reserves enough vertical space for one to two lines of text or a small spinner so layout shifts are minimal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the About tab is active, the about-section fills the full width of the router’s content area beneath the notification-banner, inside the white card. Internally, it is organized into a gentle two-column layout. The left column occupies about 40–45% of the available width and is aligned flush with the card’s inner left padding. This column is used for brief, high-level organization details—such as a short mission statement, key facts, or a small supporting image if present—stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing. The right column, taking about 55–60% of the width, starts with a 16px horizontal gap from the left column and contains the denser, paragraph-style overview text. The text is arranged in multiple paragraphs with 16–24px vertical spacing, forming the main reading area. The two columns share the same top baseline about 24px below the notification-banner and extend downward until content ends, with at least 24–32px of bottom padding before the card edge. The overall effect is a structured, brochure-like layout that emphasizes reading while still echoing the mockup’s left-right visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The section scrolls with the page and remains anchored within the card’s boundaries. On narrower viewports (if responsive behavior is later introduced), the two columns would stack, with the left column appearing above the right, but the default layout assumes a desktop-width, fixed two-column presentation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the Contact tab view, the contact-info-panel sits in the left portion of a two-column layout inside the router area. It is aligned with the card’s inner left padding and spans roughly 40–45% of the content width. Vertically, it starts about 24px below the notification-banner, sharing a top alignment with the contact-form to its right. The panel is structured as a vertical stack of labeled blocks—such as mailing address, phone number, email, office hours—each block separated by 8–16px of vertical space. Headings (e.g., “Mailing Address”) are slightly emphasized, with the actual contact details in smaller, regular-weight serif text. The panel does not usually stretch to the full height of the contact-form; instead, it may occupy the upper half to two-thirds of the content height, leaving comfortable whitespace beneath its last item, which balances visually with the right-hand form’s length.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The panel is fixed in position relative to the contact-form, staying in the normal flow as the left column. It does not use sticky behavior; as the user scrolls the page, both the panel and form move together as part of the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the right column of the Contact tab view and is the dominant element in that section. It begins about 24px below the notification-banner, aligned horizontally with the top of the contact-info-panel. It sits to the right of the contact-info-panel with a clear 16–24px horizontal gap between the columns. The form spans approximately 55–60% of the content width and extends downwards, typically longer than the left panel, creating a visually weighted right side. Form fields (name, email, subject, message, etc.) are arranged in a single vertical column with 8–12px gaps between labels and inputs, and 16px gaps between separate field groups. The submit button appears near the bottom right of the form area, aligned either with the right edge of the inputs or centered within the form column, styled as a beveled, skeuomorphic button with around 8–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding. There is at least 24px of padding between the lowest form element and the bottom edge of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form occupies a consistent right-hand column and scrolls with the page. It does not float or overlay other content. Any inline validation messages appear directly beneath the relevant fields, adding small vertical increments but not changing the overall two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the Policies tab view, the policies-loader occupies the top portion of the main content area beneath the notification-banner. While policies are being fetched, this area presents a brief, centered loading indicator within the card—such as a single line of text or simple spinner—placed about 24px below the notification-banner and centered horizontally within the content width. This aligns with where the policy list header or first policy item will eventually appear, minimizing any perceived layout shift.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader’s visible placeholder is part of the normal flow, temporarily reserving space equivalent to one or two list items. Once data has loaded, it is fully replaced by the policies-list-section, maintaining the same top alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Policies tab is active and data is loaded, the policies-list-section fills the router’s content area beneath the notification-banner. Unlike the two-column About and Contact layouts, this section favors a single, text-dense column centered within the card, reflecting the legal / informational nature of policy content. The list starts about 24px below the notification-banner and spans nearly the full width between the card’s inner paddings, with 24–32px side padding. Each policy item consists of a bold or slightly larger title followed by one or more paragraphs of explanatory text. Policy items stack vertically with 16–24px spacing between them, creating distinct but compact blocks. If there are long policies, the text continues downward, allowing the card content to extend beyond the viewport and scroll. The consistent vertical rhythm and full-width text column give this section a document-like feel while still living inside the same card frame as the other views.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The policy list scrolls as part of the page. It does not introduce additional internal scrolling containers. The section maintains a minimum bottom padding of about 24–32px to keep text from touching the card’s lower edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-tab-navigation is a horizontal row of large, skeuomorphic-style tabs positioned near the top of the white content card, directly below the back-to-home link. It is centered within the card’s width and spans roughly 70–80% of the card’s inner width, leaving equal whitespace on the left and right. The tabs (About, Contact, Policies) are arranged side-by-side with small 8–12px gaps between them. Each tab has a beveled, pill-like shape with a subtle gradient and soft border, padded with approximately 8–12px vertically and 20–24px horizontally to feel comfortably clickable. The active tab appears visually raised or more brightly lit, while inactive tabs appear slightly subdued. Vertically, the tab row sits about 16–24px below the back-to-home link and about 16–24px above the notification-banner, forming a tight control band at the top of the card. This navigation becomes the primary section selector for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The tab navigation is fixed in position at the top inside the card and remains in normal flow. As the user scrolls down lengthy content (e.g., policies), the tab row can scroll off the top with the rest of the card; it is not sticky. Selecting a tab triggers the router to swap content in the main section area below without shifting the tab row itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner occupies a thin horizontal strip inside the white card, directly beneath the section-tab-navigation and above the router-controlled content area. It spans the full inner width of the card, respecting the card’s left and right padding. When inactive, this area either collapses to a minimal 4–8px spacer or is entirely invisible, preserving alignment between the tabs and the content below. When active—for example, to show a form submission success, an API error, or an informational message—it expands to a height of roughly 32–48px. The banner is rendered as a lightly colored bar (such as a soft yellow or green) with rounded corners that echo the card’s style, and it contains a single line or two of text centered vertically and aligned slightly left within the banner. A small icon may appear at the far left to indicate status. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the banner and the start of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The banner is part of the normal flow inside the card and pushes content below it downward when visible. It does not overlay the content or float. Once dismissed or cleared, the banner collapses, allowing the main content area to move up smoothly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is a small but clear textual navigation element placed at the very top-left inside the white content card, acting as the initial anchor point for the user’s eye. Horizontally, it aligns with the card’s inner left padding, starting flush with that margin. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the card’s top edge, leaving comfortable breathing room above it. The link is styled as a simple text label, possibly prefixed by a small left-arrow icon, and uses a slightly smaller font than section titles but is still clearly legible. It occupies only a short horizontal run on the left, leaving the remainder of the row empty and allowing the centered tab navigation below to dominate the horizontal midline of the card. There is approximately 8–12px of vertical space between this link and the section-tab-navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The link sits in normal flow and scrolls with the card. It does not remain fixed once the user scrolls downward; it functions like a breadcrumb-style escape hatch back to the home page from the top of the About/Contact/Policies card.
